[2022 Rugby Europe Championship - Wikipedia]
The 2022 Rugby Europe Championship was the sixth Rugby Europe Championship, the annual rugby union for the top European national teams outside the Six Nations Championship, and the 52nd edition of the competition (including all its previous incarnations as the FIRA Tournament, Rugby Union European Cup, FIRA Nations Cup, FIRA Trophy and European Nations Cup). [Match report Spain 38 - 21 Romania, 27/02/2022 - Rugby Europe ...]
Match Spain vs Romania Match between Spain squad and Romania squad was played at Estadio Nacional Universidad Complutense as of round #3 of Rugby Europe Championship . Before kick-off Spain was in 1st position and Romania 2nd.

[Rugby union match officials - Wikipedia]
Rugby union match officials are responsible for enforcing the laws of rugby union during a match. "Every match is under the control of match officials who consist of the referee and two touch judges or assistant referees." [1]
URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rugby_union_match_officials


=== FULL EXTRACTED CONTENT ===


--- [2022 Rugby Europe Championship - Wikipedia] ---
The 2022 Rugby Europe Championship was the sixth Rugby Europe Championship, the annual rugby union for the top European national teams outside the Six Nations Championship, and the 52nd edition of the competition (including all its previous incarnations as the FIRA Tournament, Rugby Union European Cup, FIRA Nations Cup, FIRA Trophy and European Nations Cup). 
The 2022 Championship was contested by Georgia, The Netherlands, Portugal, Romania, Russia and Spain.
Georgia enter the tournament as defending champions. They topped the table after winning all of their matches in the 2021 tournament, claiming their 13th title and 10th Grand Slam as a result.
As in several other sports, Russia were disqualified after Week 3 due to the 2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine.
This year's edition of the Rugby Europe Championship doubles as the second year of the 2023 Rugby World Cup qualifiers for the European region. The winner and runner-up of the two-year cycle automatically qualify for the tournament as Europe 1 and Europe 2 respectively while the team in third place advances to the final qualification tournament as Europe 3.




--- [Match report Spain 38 - 21 Romania, 27/02/2022 - Rugby Europe ...] ---

ª « ¬ ® ° ± ² ³ ´ µ ¶ · ¹ º » ¼ ½ ¾ ¿ À Á Â Ä Å Æ Ç È É Í Î Ñ Ó Ö × Ø Ú Ü Þ ß à á â ã ä å æ ç è é ê ë ì í î ï ð ñ ò ó ô õ ö ÷ ø ù ú û ü ý þ ÿ Ā ā ă ą Ć ć Č č ď Đ đ ē ė ę ě ğ ġ Ħ ħ ĩ Ī ī İ ı ļ Ľ ľ Ł ł ń ņ ň ŋ Ō ō ŏ ő Œ œ ř Ś ś Ş ş Š š Ţ ţ ť ũ ū ŭ ů ű ų ŵ ŷ ź Ż ż Ž ž Ə ƒ ơ ư ǎ ǐ ǒ ǔ ǫ Ș ș Ț ț ɐ ɑ ɔ ɕ ə ɛ ɡ ɣ ɨ ɪ ɲ ɾ ʀ ʁ ʂ ʃ ʊ ʋ ʌ ʐ ʑ ʒ ʔ ʰ ʲ ʳ ʷ ʻ ʼ ʾ ʿ ˈ ː ˡ ˢ ˣ ́ ̃ ̍ ̯ ͡ Α Β Γ Δ Ε Η Θ Ι Κ Λ Μ Ν Ο Π Σ Τ Φ Χ Ψ Ω ά έ ή ί α β γ δ ε ζ η θ ι κ λ μ ν ξ ο π ρ ς σ τ υ φ χ ψ ω ό ύ ώ І Ј А Б В Г Д Е Ж З И К Л М Н О П Р С Т У Ф Х Ц Ч Ш Э Ю Я а б в г д е ж з и й к л м н о п р с т у ф х ц ч ш щ ъ ы ь э ю я ё і ї ј њ ћ Ա Հ ա ե ի կ մ յ ն ո ս տ ր ւ ְ ִ ֵ ֶ ַ ָ ֹ ּ א ב ג ד ה ו ז ח ט י כ ל ם מ ן נ ס ע פ צ ק ר ש ת ، ء آ أ إ ئ ا ب ة ت ث ج ح خ د ذ ر ز س ش ص ض ط ظ ع غ ف ق ك ل م ن ه و ى ي َ ِ ٹ پ چ ک گ ہ ی ے ं आ क ग च ज ण त द ध न प ब भ म य र ल व श ष स ह ा ि ी ु े ो ् । ॥ আ ই এ ও ক খ গ চ ছ জ ট ত থ দ ধ ন প ব ম য র ল শ স হ ় া ি ী ু ে ো ্ য় க த ப ம ய ர ல வ ா ி ு ் ร ་ ག ང ད ན བ མ ར ལ ས ི ུ ེ ོ ა ე ი ლ ნ ო რ ს ᴬ ᴵ ᵀ ᵃ ᵇ ᵈ ᵉ ᵍ ᵏ ᵐ ᵒ ᵖ ᵗ ᵘ ᵢ ᵣ ᵤ ᵥ ᶜ ᶠ ḍ Ḥ ḥ Ḩ ḩ ḳ ṃ ṅ ṇ ṛ ṣ ṭ ạ ả ấ ầ ẩ ậ ắ ế ề ể ễ ệ ị ọ ố ồ ổ ộ ớ ờ ợ ụ ủ ứ ừ ử ữ ự ỳ ỹ ἀ ἐ ὁ ὐ ὰ ὶ ὸ ῆ ῖ ῦ ῶ ‐ ‑ ‒ – — ― ‖ ‘ ’ ‚ “ ” „ † ‡ • … ‰ ′ ″ ⁄ ⁰ ⁱ ⁴ ⁵ ⁶ ⁷ ⁸ --- [DTL_20241203_null_null_01 - Flipbook by Georgiana Vear | FlipHTML5] ---
PUBLISHER OF THE YEAR‘For goodness sake!The golf club is no placefor a Jaguar car owner’Seeing doubleLady Amelia Spencer and Lady Eliza Spencer attend this year’s Fashion Awards at the Royal Albert Hall. The twins, 32, paidtribute to their late aunt Diana, Princess of Wales with jewellery and gowns in the style of her 1994 ‘revenge dress’.By Ben Riley-Smith POLITICAL EDITORSIR KEIR STARMER has warned DonaldTrump that he will not side with Americaagainst the EU, despite calls from thepresident-elect’s allies to choosebetween Brussels and Washington.In a major foreign policy speech lastnight, the Prime Minister said it was“plain wrong” to suggest that MrTrump’s return to the White Housemeant Britain had to either get closer toEurope or the US. He vowed instead tobuild bridges to both continents, promising to “invest more deeply than everin this transatlantic bond with ourAmerican friends”, and to “rebuild ourties with Europe, too”.The speech included overtures to MrTrump, with the Prime Minister stressing the need to prepare Ukraine forpeace negotiations and calling onEurope to spend more on defence.Since Mr Trump’s resounding victoryin the presidential election last month,there has been debate about whether SirKeir should embrace America or Europe.Some of the Republican’s allies havewaded into the argument, with StephenMoore, his senior economic adviser,saying that the UK must reject the EU’s“socialist model”. Sir Keir has facedpressure from within his own party toreject Mr Trump and rebuild ties withthe EU. However, In his speech to theLord Mayor’s Banquet in London lastnight, the Prime Minister rejected the“either/or” choice.He said: “Against the backdrop ofthese dangerous times, the idea that wemust choose between our allies – thatsomehow we’re with either America orEurope – is plain wrong. I reject itutterly. [Clement] Attlee did not choosebetween allies. [Winston] Churchill didnot choose. The national interestdemands that we work with both. Ourrelationship with the United States hasbeen the cornerstone of our securityand our prosperity for over a century.We will never turn away from that. Wecall it the special relationship for a reason. It is written not in some dry, dustytreaty, but in the ink of shared sacri ce.”He added: “And we will rebuild ourties with Europe, too. Because I’m sorryto say the shocking legacy this Government inherited in so many areas – fromthe nation’s  nances to the state of theNHS – extends beyond our shores.”Downing Street insiders hope MrTrump will overlook fierce criticismissued about him in the past by manyLabour cabinet ministers, includingDavid Lammy, the Foreign Secretary,who previously called the presidentelect a “tyrant” and a “sociopath”.Also in his speech, Sir Keir said it wastime for Ukraine to prepare for peacetalks. Since Moscow’s invasion in 2022,successive British prime ministers haveargued that Ukraine must be supportedmilitarily to fully defeat Vladimir Putin’sforces. But Sir Keir explicitly said thatallies should now be focused onstrengthening Kyiv for “negotiations”.He said: “We must continue to backUkraine and do what it takes to supporttheir self-defence for as long as it takes.“To put Ukraine in the strongest possible position for negotiations so theycan secure a just and lasting peace ontheir terms that guarantees their security, independence and right to choosetheir own future.”Mr Trump has vowed to bring bothsides of the con ict together to end thewar swiftly, and his impending returnto the White House has led Kyiv andEuropean capitals to shift their positionon how the war will end. VolodymyrZelensky, the Ukrainian president,talked openly for the  rst time on Friday about the possibility that land occupied by Russian forces may have to begiven up in peace talks.The speech made clear that Sir Keirwould push ahead with his “reset” inrelations with the EU, which is expectedto include negotiations on a defence andsecurity pact next year. Sir Keir’s ministers and European diplomats have alsoheld conversations about letting moreyoung Europeans move to the UK – andvice-versa – in a youth movement deal.He also warned that “unsustainable”migration levels had led to a “feelingamong many that the system isn’t working for them”. It came as it was con- rmed that more than 20,000 migrantshad crossed the Channel since Labourcame to power.By Peter Allen in ParisEMMANUEL MACRON’S governmentis on the brink of collapse after hisprime minister forced through hisbudget without consulting parliament.Michel Barnier, 73, said he wouldbypass MPs and invoke a presidentialdecree to pass his controversial newsocial security reforms, having failed towin enough support for the bill. Themove triggered fury across parliament.The Left-wing La France Insoumiseparty responded by  ling a motion of nocon dence on behalf of the New PopularFront. Marine Le Pen, the leader of thehard-Right RN, announced that herparty would also  le its own motion, butsaid it would vote for any similar bill putforward by other parties.“The French have had enough,” shesaid. “Maybe they thought with MichelBarnier things would get better, butthey are even worse.”The motions, subject to votes in theNational Assembly within 48 hours, arelikely to be held tomorrow. Should allRN lawmakers vote with the Left to topple Mr Barnier, the government will notsurvive, meaning Mr Macron will haveto appoint another unpopular government or resign.If voted out, Mr Barnier will havespent less than three months in his position – making him the shortest-servingprime minister in the history of theFifth Republic. His budget was designedto tackle France’s large and growingde cit, and includes controversial pension increases and taxes on energy bills.In a pleading speech to the assemblyyesterday, Mr Barnier – who is not anMP – said his budget would save €60 billion (£50 billion) at a time when Franceis in deep  nancial trouble. “The situation is serious, the immediate daily livesof the French are at stake,” he said.Announcing he had “reached the endof dialogue” with opposition parties, hesaid that “at this moment of truth, it istime to act to implement article 49.3” oft h e c o n s t i t u t i o n . D e s i g n e d t ostrengthen presidential government,the measure allows any serving head ofstate to e ectively ignore parliament.Article 49.3 has frequently been usedby the prime ministers of Mr Macron,the French president, to get difficultlegislation through since he came topower in 2017.In this case, there were fears that MrBarnier’s social security measureswould have no hope of being votedthrough the Assembly.Mr Barnier told deputies: “It is nowup to you... to decide if our countryshould have a financial law that isresponsible, indispensable and usefulfor our fellow French citizens, or if weare to enter into uncharted territory.The French will not forgive us for putting the interests of individuals beforethe future of the country.”France’s National Rally of MsLe Pen and Jordan Bardella, the largestsingle party in the Assembly, said theywould  le for a no con dence vote. MsLe Pen said: “Things were clear, MichelBarnier did not wish to respond to thedemands of 11 million voters of theNational Rally.“We will assume our responsibility to le a motion of censure,” said Ms Le Pen,Continued on Page 4Macron’s government on brink after forcing through budgetPM defies president-elect’s allies as hetells Ukraine to prepare for peace talksStarmer: Iwill not sidewith Trumpover EuropeBy Craig Simpson and Dominic PennaTHE BBC last night refused to pull programmes featuring Gregg Wallace o airdespite Downing Street describing hiscomments about middle-class women as“inappropriate and misogynistic”.The first of the semi-finals of MasterChef: The Professionals aired last nightas planned, despite calls for it to bepulled from the schedule amid mounting allegations against the presenter ofsexually inappropriate behaviour.Mr Wallace “stepped away” from thecooking show last week when claimsemerged. The backlash against the60-year-old presenter grew on Sundayafter he said in an Instagram video thatthe complaints had come from a “handfulof middle-class women of a certain age”.The video was roundly condemned,with a Downing Street spokesman saying yesterday that “the comments wehave seen from the individual over theweekend were completely inappropriate and misogynistic”.Rupa Huq, the Labour MP, warnedthe BBC that continuing to broadcastMasterChef would “send the wrongsigns”. However, the rest of the series isstill due to run through December asplanned, with the broadcaster’s schedules also showing planned repeats ofChristmas specials of MasterChef andInside the Factory featuring Mr Wallace.A BBC spokesman said: “MasterChefis life-changing for the chefs that takepart, and the show is about more thanone individual.”It came as former TV presenter Melanie Sykes released a YouTube videodescribing her experience on CelebrityMasterChef, in which she said “everytime Gregg came over to the desk Ididn’t really like him being around,really, because it’s all about vibrationsand energy”.In her autobiography, published lastyear, Sykes wrote that the experienceled her to quit her screen career.Also, writing for The Telegraph, thebroadcaster Aasmah Mir, who appearedon the 12th series of Celebrity MasterChef and complained about inappropriate comments Mr Wallace madeduring  lming, said she had been madeto feel like a “fool” by the BBC.She wrote: “The BBC made me feelthat things would be fixed; I naivelyb elieve d they would s or t thi s.Subsequent allegations not just onMasterChef but on two other showswould suggest I was a fool.”The BBC has faced accusations of notdoing enough to act on complaintsabout Mr Wallace’s alleged past behaviour on Celebrity MasterChef made byEmma Kennedy, the author, in 2012,and by Mir in 2017.Insiders have complained that thebroadcaster has a habit of “mitigatingContinued on Page 7BBC refuses topull ‘misogynist’Wallace ofi air‘Every time Greggcame over to the desk onMasterChef I didn’t reallylike him being around’Democrats have accused Joe Biden ofputting “family before country” afterhe pardoned his son for federal tax andgun charges. The US president hadrepeatedly said he would not use hispower to pardon Hunter Biden butchanged his mind at the weekend.Members of his own party have sinceaccused him of laying the groundworkfor politically motivated clemencyagreements by Donald Trump, and ofundermining the rule of law with themost wide-ranging clemencyagreement since Richard Nixon.Page 13WORLDBiden puts ‘family overUS’ with son’s pardonExcessive belly fat may start causingAlzheimer’s disease 20 years beforesymptoms emerge, research suggests.Scans from dozens of middle-agedobese people found that those withvisceral fat – deep fat that surrounds thestomach, abdomen and organs – alsohad higher levels of sticky amyloid andtau tangles – which stop brain cells fromcommunicating and are present at highlevels in people with Alzheimer’s. Butthose with subcutaneous fat, the fatunder the skin that you can pinch,showed no increase in amyloid or tau.Page 12NEWSBelly fat may be earlycause of Alzheimer’sThe Post O ce’s embattled boss isexpected to lose out on a bonuspackage worth more than £750,000after abruptly stepping down from hispost in the wake of the Horizonscandal. Nick Read, who took over in2019, is understood to have forfeitedpayouts totalling as much as £767,000as a result of his decision to step downin September. It caps a torrid tenurefor the chief executive, whose time incharge has been overshadowed onmore than one occasion bydisputes over pay.Page 19BUSINESSPost Ofi ce axes bonusfor outgoing chiefThe Queen is to miss the ceremonialwelcome during the Qatari state visitafter su ering signi cantly diminishedreserves of energy following her chestinfection. Doctors have urged Camilla,77, to take time to rest and fully recoverfrom the lingering e ects of the illness,which she contracted a month agofollowing a trip to Australia and Samoa.The King, and the Prince and Princessof Wales, will welcome the Emir ofQatar and his wife to the UK today for atraditional outdoor welcome alongHorse Guards Parade.Page 4NEWSQueen lacking energyPuzzles 18 after chest infectionObituaries 27TV listings 29Weather 31INSIDEDAVE BENETT/GETTY IMAGES‘My dad wearsthem’ HowNike lostits edge FeaturesHe’s still standingWhy poor sightwon’t stop EltonFeatures‘My dad wearsJaguar’s ‘Barbie pink’ motor: Page 5 t:HRKDKH*bcdicf+ (ì}û)ISSN-0307-1235Tuesday 3 December 2024 *** telegraph.co.uk No 52,740 £3.502 *** Tuesday 3 December 2024 The Daily TelegraphThe Daily Telegraph Tuesday 3 December 2024 *** 3Louise Haigh will receive thousandsof pounds in severance pay after herresignation as Transport Secretary,No10 has said.Ms Haigh quit Sir Keir Starmer’sCabinet last week after she admittedpleading guilty to misleading thepolice a decade ago. All departingministers aged 65 or under are entitledto a quarter of their annual salary nomatter how long they are in their postor the circumstances in which theyleave their jobs.A research briefi ng by the House ofCommons Library states that a Cabinetminister, whose ministerial salary is£67,505, would be entitled toseverance pay of £16,876.Haigh in line for quarterof salary as severanceLucy Letby’s parents claimed she was“singled out for punishment” andtreated as guilty even before a policeinvestigation had concluded.In a letter after the police inquirybegan in 2017, John and Sue Letbycomplained the family had been livinga “nightmare” for a year. The letter,shown to the Thirlwall Inquiry,suggests they expected their daughterto be cleared and return to work. Theywrote to Sir Duncan Nichol, chairmanof the board at the Countess of Chesterhospital: “There is a saying, ‘innocentuntil proven guilty’ but it does notseem to apply to Lucy. She is still theonly one of all the staff on the neonatalunit to be singled out for punishment.”Letby singled out forpunishment, parents saidA teenage gangster who murdered awoman as she tried to protect herchildren during a turf war betweenrivals should be “hanged”, the victim’smother told a court.Joshua Alexander, 17, was sentencedto life imprisonment with a minimumterm of 29 years at the Old Baileyyesterday. Lianne Gordon, 42, had justreturned from a holiday in Jamaicawhen she was attacked in her home inHackney, east London, on Dec 5 2023.She fell prey to a gang feud, jurorsheard. In a victim impact statement,Ms Gordon’s mother Ella Leatham saidAlexander should be “hanged”, adding:“Dec 5 2023 will be with me for therest of my days.”‘Hang the teenager whomurdered my daughter’Ian Smith, known for playing HaroldBishop in Neighbours, has announcedhis departure from the show afterrevealing he has terminal cancer.The 86-year-old actor said in aninterview with Australia’s 10 NewsFirst that he has pulmonarypleomorphic carcinoma, a rare form oflung cancer. Smith fi rst appeared onthe show in 1987. His character – atuba-playing, vegetarian Samaritan– was only due to be in a few episodesbut stayed until 1991, when Harold waswashed out to sea while on holiday.Harold has returned to the Australiansoap over the years, and it wasannounced in April that he wouldreturn to Ramsay Street 15 years afterhis departure as a regular character.Neighbours actor to quitafter cancer diagnosisNEWS BULLETINis a member of theIndependentPress StandardsOrganisation (IPSO) and we subscribeto its Editors’ Code of Practice. If you are notsatisfi ed with our response, you mayappeal to IPSO at www.ipso.co.uk.The Daily Telegraph, 111 BuckinghamPalace Road, London, SW1W 0DTWhat to watch Where to buy What to wearDownload the Telegraph app free todayEXPLORE OUR APP’S NEW SECTIONFOR ALL LIFE’S DECISIONS, BIG AND SMALLNewsSTEPHANE DE SAKUTIN/AFPBy Charles HymasHOME AFFAIRS EDITORFOREIGN criminals who avoideddeportation committed 10,000 off encesin a single year, o cial fi gures show.Ministry of Justice (MoJ) datarevealed that a quarter of foreign criminals went on to reoff end in the UK afterbeing released from jail and remainingin the country.The 3,235 foreign offenders whowere freed from jail accounted for threecrimes, on average, giving the total inthe year to March 2022 of 10,012 furtheroff ences. That is a rise of 25 per cent onthe previous year’s 8,021 off ences committed by 2,462 freed foreign criminals.Over the four years of data, released bythe MoJ in a parliamentary question,foreign off enders were responsible forabout 40,000 crimes.Offences ranged from murder toknife possession and drug dealing. Aswell as including criminals who avoideddeportation, they also included off enders who returned to the UK to committheir crimes after being deported.The Home Secretary is required bylaw to deport any foreign criminal jailedfor more than a year but has discretionto remove those jailed for less than ayear if such a move is considered to be inthe public interest. Rupert Lowe, theReform MP, who extracted the data fromthe MoJ, said: “Everyone who commits acrime should be deported. Why are wetolerating this, particularly when we seethe reoff ending rates are so high.”He also urged the Government to bemore open over the amount of crimecommitted by migrants, includingthose who entered legally and illegally.Robert Jenrick, the former immigration minister, has proposed legislationrequiring the Government each year topresent a report to Parliament detailingthe nationality, visa and asylum status ofevery off ender convicted in English andWelsh courts in the previous 12 months.He said: “Tens of thousands of off encesa year would be prevented if the Government to ok a zero -toler-anceapproach to deporting foreign off enders. The public expects robust action.”There are more than 10,000 foreigners in UK prisons, accounting for 12 percent of the total. Albanians top the list,followed by Polish, Romanian, Irish andJamaican.Ernesto Elliott, a Jamaican jailed forknife crime, went on to murder a 35-yearold man in a knife fight after beingreleased from prison. Elliott was due tobe on a deportation  ight in December2020 after being convicted of knife crimebut he and 22 other serious criminals submitted appeals – including human rightsclaims – which led to them avoidingdeportation. They had been sentenced toa combined 156 years in jail. Their appealscame after 60 public figures signed anopen letter opposing the  ight.Elliott was jailed for at least 26 yearsfor murder after the knife fight in astreet in Greenwich, south-east London. People who saw the bloody,eight-minute confrontation suffered“signifi cant trauma”, police said.An MoJ spokesman said: “It costs tensof thousands to hold an offender inprison and since the new Governmentcame into power, we have returned 14per cent more foreign national off enders than in the same period last year.”10,000 crimes by foreigners after avoiding deportationMarine Le Pen, the hard-Right leader of the RN party, takes questions after voting on legislation at France’s lower chamber in Paris yesterdayContinued from Page 1adding: “We will vote on motions ofcensure – fi rst and foremost ours.”Mr Bardella said that Mr Barnier’sausterity measures would cause suff ering across the whole country.“The budget presented by the government is a budget of punishment thatwill weaken the purchasing power ofour compatriots,” he claimed.A no-confidence motion was alsofiled by members of the New PopularFront, the largest voting bloc in theNational Assembly. The opposition has24 hours to table a motion, and the votecan take place within 48 hours.No French government has beenforced out by such a vote since 1962, butthe current number of opposition MPsacross the political divide suggest MrBarnier is in deep trouble.While Mr Macron is not directlythreatened, he cannot call new parliamentary elections until June 2025, andso will have to try and create anothergovernment.The president could reinstate Barnieras prime minister, although this wouldprobably result in even more angerfrom lawmakers. He could also askFrance’s other political parties to build anew coalition government.Mr Macron could also install a technocratic government for six monthsuntil he is able to call elections again, orhe could resign, triggering presidentialelections.Mr Barnier had insisted his €40 billion (£33 billion) in spending cuts and€20 billion (£16 billion) in tax rises wereessential if France is to control its defi -cit, which is projected to hit 6.1 per centthis year.The New Popular Front alliance hassworn to back a no-confi dence motionagainst Mr Barnier, meaning he reliedon RN’s support. This kingmaker position had seen the RN put forwarddemands including a reduction inFrance’s contribution to the EU budget.Barnier budgetrisks causing‘sufieringacross France’By Daily Telegraph ReporterTHE Queen is to miss the ceremonialwelcome during the Qatari state visitafter suff ering signifi cantly diminishedreserves of energy following her chestinfection.Doctors have urged Camilla, 77, totake time to rest and to fully recoverfrom the lingering eff ects of the illness,which she contracted a month ago following a long-haul trip to Australia andSamoa.The King, and the Prince and Princess of Wales, are set to welcome theEmir of Qatar and his wife to the UK ontoday as part of the traditional outdoorwelcome on Horse Guards Parade.It will be the fi rst state visit the Princess has taken part in since her cancerdiagnosis as she continues her gradualreturn to public duties.She will meet Sheikh Tamim binHamad Al Thani and his wife, SheikhaJawaher bint Hamad bin Suhaim AlThani, with Prince William in London,before accompanying them to theparade ground to meet the waiting Kingand senior government fi gures.The King, the Prince and Princess ofWales and their guests will then takepart in a carriage ride back to Buckingham Palace, where they will attend alunch and visit a Picture Gallery exhibition in the royal residence.The Palace said the Queen’s programme had been tweaked, meaningshe will miss both the ceremonial welQueen too ill to attendwelcome for QatarisDoctors urge Her Majestyto take time to recoverfrom chest infection aftertrip to Australia and Samoacome and the carriage ride. However,she will join the Emir and his wife forthe lunch and hopes to be able to attendthe gallery display.The Queen will still be present at theglittering state banquet in the eveningand will pose for a group photo as itbegins. She will take a short breakbefore dinner while guests are beingmet in a receiving line, however.The Princess of Wales will not be inattendance at the banquet, but thePrince will be there.With temperatures expected to hoveraround 7C in London today, it is likelyroyal doctors were keen for the Queento miss the ceremonial welcome toavoid her standing in the chill December air for an extended period of time.The ceremony, which sees the royalparty gather on a royal dais, involves theKing inspecting a Guard of Honour withhis visiting head of state.Meanwhile, the occasion marks thestart of a busier week for the Princess ofWales. She will be hosting her annualChristmas carol service at WestminsterAbbey on Friday, when she is expectedto be joined by her children and husband for the event.The Emir of Qatar, Sheikh Tamim, 44,was educated in Britain, attendingSherborne and Harrow, before graduating from Sandhurst military academy.He set up Qatar Sports Investments in2005, which owns the French footballteam Paris Saint-Germain.Tomorrow, the fi nal day of the twoday visit, the Emir will visit Sandhurstbefore travelling to the Palace to bidfarewell formally to the King andQueen, with Her Majesty expected to bepresent. The car, which was set to be o -cially unveiled at Miami Art Week overnight, is shown in bold pink andmetallic blue.Despite Jaguar’s intention being topromote a “Miami Pink” colour scheme,the vehicle drew comparisons with theFAB 1 car driven by Lady Penelope inThunderbirds and the Barbie car. Othershave suggested that Jaguar should berenamed Pink Panther in light of theoverhaul.The styling suggests a marked gearchange from traditional Jaguar modelsas the British car maker prepares for anew generation of electric models.The company has been gearing upfor its relaunch for weeks, attractingcriticism online for seemingly abandoning its traditional customers infavour of a younger new market.A Jaguar spokesman said it was awareof the images circulating online and said:“Miami Pink celebrates the vibrancyof the city, whileLondon Blue, amodern take onthe OpalescentSilver Blue ofthe E-Type, is anod to Jaguar ’sBritish heritage.”In leaked images, the conceptcar features slim, LED lights and largealloy wheels. It does not have a rearwindscreen, instead using a rear-viewcamera on each side.The interiors also adopt a minimalistdesign with an oval-shaped steeringwheel, while the company’s divisivenew logo is emblazoned on the front andback. The marque last month ditched itshistoric “growler” cat logo, replacing itwith a new symbol featuring the letters J and L.Jaguar’s leaked design, whichis expected to form the basis ofits forthcoming electric GT,has already attracted ridiculeonline.Some s o cial me diausers compared the car toan air conditioning unita f t e r t h e c o m p a n yreleased a glimpse of thenew model last month.The concept car is at theheart of e orts by Jaguar toreverse a recent decline insales. The carmaker is hoping to shakeo its heritage reputation – embodiedby the “Jag man” stereotype – andinstead target a new generation ofwealthier, younger customers.Rawdon Glover, Jaguar’s managingdirector, has said that people such asNigel Farage are no longer important tothe brand’s vision as it pursues a “completely di erent audience” to its traditional customers.However, the rebrand has prompteda storm of controversy amid accusationsthat the company is abandoning loyalcustomers and dealerships.More widely, the company has beenmocked for its advert featuring catwalkmodels but no cars.The rebrand has attracted scrutinyeven from within the advertising industry. Paul Burke, an advertising copywriter, told The Telegraph: “As a piece ofcreative work it’s rubbish. It’s reallycheaply done and there’s no idea in it.”However, others have suggested thatit was too early to judge the success ofthe campaign, pointing out that Jaguarhas succeeded in attracting signi cantmedia attention.Jaguar said the rebrand was a “firebreak” between old and new as themanufacturer prepares for the electricshift. It took new cars o sale more thana year ago to focus on the strategy.Mr Glover has also defended thechanges, saying criticism of actors inthe campaign had featured “vile hatredand intolerance”.He told The Financial Times: “Weneed to re-establish our brand and at acompletely di erent price point so weneed to act di erently. If we play in thesame way that everybody else does,we’ll just get drowned out. So weshouldn’t turn up like an auto brand.”A Jaguar spokesman said: “We areaware of images circulating onlineahead of Jaguar’s o cial reveal at MiamiArt Week.“Tune in to the Jaguar Instagram andTikTok channels to see the real thing[on Monday night].”Jaguar’s ‘FAB’ rebrand sufiers launch failureLeaked pictures of brightpink concept car ridiculedonline as marque insists onshaking its ‘Jag man’ imageNewsThis once understated jewel in Britain’s carmaking crown has gone wokeIt could have restored the splendourof the leaping cat to the bonnet. Itcould have tried to summon up theelegantly sensual curves of some of itsclassic designs from the 1950s and1960s. Or it could at least have gonefor the deep blues, or serious blacks,along with plenty of walnut andtanned leather, that are the languageof the big executive saloons that theGerman auto giants specialise in.There were plenty of di erent waysworst pieces of marketing of all time,you might think someone at Jaguarwould decide it was time to press thereset button. Instead, they resolved todouble down.The  rst leaked pictures of the newcar feature a bright pink vehicle, a farcry from theunderstated splendourof earlier models. Andinstead of breakingnew ground, andrevolutionising theway its machinesare designed aspromised, itlooks, well, sort oflike a lot of theother upmarketcars on themarket. It could be the new Audi,Tesla, or Lexus.The launch looks set to becomeanother self-in icted disaster. True,everyone accepts that Jaguar faces atricky challenge. It is a nichemanufacturer, based in a high-costcountry, trying to make the transitionfrom petrol to battery-poweredvehicles.At the same time it is facing  ercecompetition, not just from itstraditional rivals, but from Tesla and awhole new range of Chinese brands,many of them with brilliant designs,packed with the latest technology,and sold for prices that few Westerncompanies can match.Against that backdrop, it wasalways going to be hard for Jaguar to nd its niche. And yet theimportant point is this: Jaguar hasone of the greatest brands inautomotive history.Classic car bu s still drool over thelikes of the E-Type, the Mark 2, or theXJ220. It needed to  nd a way ofpleasing the “Gin and Jag”traditionalists, while also bringing onboard a new generation of younger,stylish, upmarket customers.The way to do that was to draw onits heritage, and yet give it a moderntwist. Instead, it has produced abright pink Barbie-mobile that willplease no one. It will prove acatastrophe for one of the UK’s mosticonic brands – and if it doesn’tchange course soon, it will pay theprice.‘It was acelebrationof diversityandinclusionwidelyridiculed asone of theworst piecesof marketingof all time’that Jaguar could have made astatement with the launch of itsnew range of electric vehicles,scheduled to be unveiled in Miamitoday. The announcementcomes just weeks after a two-part ITVdocumentary explored the Prince’se orts to tackle homelessness, whichwas available to international audienceson Disney Plus.Prince William: We Can End Homelessness was broadcast in October andfeatured extensive interviews with thePrince as he described how he had triedto work out how best to use his platform, having taken inspiration andguidance from the work of his latemother, Diana, Princess of Wales.The Earthshot documentary is the latest example of efforts to ensure thePrince’s key work projects are disseminated to as wide an audience as possible.It comes as he gradually increases hispro le on the international stage, positioning himself as a global statesman.His latest foray into television willexplore the stories of environmentalinnovators working across the globe,from the US to Ecuador, South Africaand the UK, and their e orts to protectand restore our planet for future generations. It will feature interviews withEarthshot  nalists and winners including UK-based Notpla, which producesseaweed-based packaging as an alternative to plastic, and Amazon SacredHeadwaters, which brings togetherindigenous nations to protect 86 millionacres of forests.A Palace source said: “We are alwayslooking for innovative new ways to tellthese stories.”Waddingham has been closelyinvolved in Earthshot and presentedlast year’s award ceremony in Singapore.This latest documentary comes fouryears after ITV released Prince William:A Planet for Us All, which involved camera crews following him for two years tolearn about his “personal mission to nd ways to protect the natural world”.Prince uses BBC for Earthshot PR pushBy Joe PinkstonePEAS are a better alternative to meatthan fake burgers, a University ofOxford study has found.The health and environmentalimpact of 24 meat and milk alternativeswere compared to the animal-basedversions in a comprehensive analysis.Legumes, such as peas, were found tobe the best replacement when it cameto cost, emissions and health. Meanwhile, veggie burgers and meat-freesausages were found to be better thanthe meaty options but not as bene cialas natural, unprocessed alternatives.Dr Marco Springmann, the study’sauthor and a senior researcher at theUniversity of Oxford’s EnvironmentalChange Institute, said: “There is anincreasing interest in cutting out meatand dairy from our diets, in part due toenvironmental and health concerns.”He added: “However, consumers areoften wondering which foods are bestsuited to replace meat and dairy.”There have been a large number ofstudies linking meat consumption, anddiets rich in animal-based protein, witha high carbon footprint and increasedrisk of health issues such as cancer,heart disease and diabetes.The NHS recommends people eat nomore than 70 grams of red meat a day.“The wide scope [of our study] makes itone of the most comprehensive assessments to date,” added Dr Springmann.“Unprocessed legumes such as peasand beans were the clear winner in ourassessment. They performed well fromall perspectives, including nutritional,health, environmental, and cost.”Bottom of the list for health and environmental impact was lab-grown meat,which is not yet sold in the UK forhuman consumption but the production of which is being encouraged bythe UK Government.Dr Springmann’s analysis found labgrown meat to be “not a competitiveproduct, not even for normal meat”. Hesaid: “At current technologies, its emissions can be as high as those of beefburgers at up to 40,000 times its costs,whilst its health impacts when replicating beef would be similarly bad.”Replacing all meat or dairy with alternatives could cut early death by up to 6per cent, according to scientists involvedin the study – published in PNAS.Peas scored highest, data show, witha drop in the number of deaths of 6.1 percent. Veggie burgers and tofu reduceddeaths by 4 and 4.3 per cent, respectively, while veggie sausages led to adrop in deaths of 3.5 and 3.6 per cent.Swapping milk for soy milk wouldlead to a 5.2 per cent drop in deaths, thestudy also found.Fake-meat eaters told to give peas a chancefor its relaunch for weeks, attractingcriticism online for seemingly abandoning its traditional customers infavour of a younger new market.A Jaguar spokesman said it was awareof the images circulating online and said:“Miami Pink celebrates the vibrancyof the city, whileLondon Blue, amodern take onthe OpalescentSilver Blue ofthe E-Type, is anod to Jaguar ’sBritish heritage.”In leaked images, the conceptback. Defence companies will be involved in the exercises forthe first time.Mr Healey told journalists on a visitto the Honourable Artillery Companyyesterday to see drone technology:“The reason is that we need together –government and industry – to be capable of innovation at a wartime pace.”He said that the Government neededto take “to heart” lessons from Ukraine,which showed that a “nation’s armedforces are only as strong as the industrythat stands behind them”.He added: “Adversaries must knowthat we have an industry: with thecapacity to generate and regenerate theequipment our forces need; that wehave a science and tech base to ensurewe can deliver leading edge capabilities; and that we have the ability toinnovate at a wartime pace, if faced withconflict.”Concerns have been mounting overhow many supplies the UK has left in itsstore cupboards, having given so muchmilitary equipment to Kyiv.In September, Luke Pollard, the minister for the Armed Forces, warned thatWestern forces had almost nothing leftin their stockpiles after helping Ukrainefight Russia for more than two years. Hesaid the UK had “depleted our ownstocks” to keep arming Ukraine.When the UK announced it wouldgive 14 Challenger 2 tanks to Ukraine lastyear, Gen Sir Patrick Sanders, formerhead of the Army, said it would leaveBritish forces “temporarily weaker”.However, the head of QinetiQ, adefence technology company, said earlier this year that Western nations weretaking steps to put their armed forceson a war footing for the first time in decades because of Ukraine, concerns aboutChina’s ambitions in the Pacific andescalations in the Israel-Hamas conflict.Mr Healey said: “We need industryworking together with Government,and that is at the heart of the newdefence industrial strategy.”He added that the Government was“staging war games” with key companies in the defence industry “in order tobe able to sustain our war fighters onthe front line when supply chains aredisrupted, or when we may be facedwith extended conflict”.Earlier this year, Mr Healey admittedthe British military was not “ready tofight” a war against Russia.However, when asked yesterdaywhether Britain is “war ready”, he said:“What I’m doing is recognising that ourforces need to be better equipped.“Behind them needs to be an industry that is better able to produce whatthey need and innovate at the pace ofwartime pressures.“My message is this, invest in defenceand send a strong signal to Putin andother adversaries that would do usharm,” he said.We need to transform militaryat ‘wartime pace’, says HealeyAssad’s forcesfight back againstthe rebel attacks.Syrians in Londoncelebrate afterjihadist forcestook control ofAleppo, leftHUSEYIN NASIR/ANADOLU VIA GETTY IMAGES; ZAK IRFAN /AVALONMigrants crossthe Channel bydinghy in a bidto reach BritainBy Ben Farmer and James KilnerIRAN has sent militia groups into Syriato prop up Bashar al-Assad, as Chinaalso gave its backing to the regime.At least 300 fighters crossed fromIraq as Tehran said it would provide fullsupport to the beleaguered dictatorafter rebel groups seized the city ofAleppo and charged south towardHama and Damascus.Fighters crossed from outposts inIraq late on Sunday using a dirt road toavoid the official border crossing, security sources said.Yesterday, Russia again joined Syrianregime forces in bombing rebel-heldcities, killing at least 25 people.Meanwhile, China said it “supportsSyria’s efforts to maintain national security and stability” in its first commentson the reignited civil war. However, it isunlikely to lend military support.Hezbollah, the Iran-backed terroristgroup, also refused to deploy units toSyria to help Assad, after being badlymauled by Israel’s air and land campaign in Lebanon.The Kremlin said yesterday thatVladimir Putin had held a phone callwith Masoud Pezeshkian, the Iranianpresident, about the renewed fightingin Syria, and made his first publicstatement since rebels captured Aleppolast week.“ U n c o n d i t i o n a l s u p p o r t w a sexpressed for the actions of the legitimate authorities of Syria to restore constitutional order and to restore thepolitical, economic and social stabilityof the Syrian state,” the Kremlin said.Russia has backed Syria since intervening in the civil war in 2015. It hasalso become a firm ally of Iran duringits war in Ukraine, relying on Iranianmissiles and drones to supply its forceson the front line. It has also growncloser to China.Syrian government troops were yesterday reported to be building a fortified defensive line in northern Hama inan attempt to stall an offensive that hasreignited the Syrian civil war after yearsof stagnant front lines.The militiamen were expected tosupport the Syrian army’s push backagainst the insurgents.The Assad regime has for years beenheavily reliant on support from Iran andRussia to maintain its grip on power,after 2011 pro-democracy protests escalated into a brutal civil war.Tehran has poured in billions of dollars, as well as troops, arms, equipmentand advisers to keep Assad in government.Abbas Araghchi, Iran’s foreign minister, said yesterday that Syria’s militarywas capable of confronting the rebelsbut, “resistance groups will help andIran will provide any support needed”.currently planning to send troops tohelp Assad.Hezbollah forces fought for years inSyria, before pulling back as the groupcame under attack from Israel in October. The group’s fighters had not yetbeen asked by Assad to intervene, butwere not ready to send forces becauseof the war in Lebanon, sources said.Assad said the offensive led by theHayat Tahrir al-Sham (HTS) jihadistgroup and its allies was an attempt toredraw the map of the region, inremarks during a call with Iran.A statement from the governmentsaid Assad had told Iran: “The terroristescalation reflects the far-reachinggoals of dividing the region and fragmenting the countries in it and redrawthe map in line with the objectives ofthe United States and the West.”Inside rebel-held territory, Russiacontinued air strikes and was accusedof hitting five hospitals and threeschools within Aleppo and Idlib.The speed of Syrian regime troopwithdrawal had shocked Aleppo’sinhabitants, residents told the Telegraph, who now face aerial bombardment and an uncertain future underrebel control.Commanders of the HTS group,which had its roots with al-Qaedabefore turning on the extremists, havetried to assure Aleppo’s minorities thatthey will be safe. The rebels havereleased a statement urging Aleppo’sKurds not to flee the city and declaringthat “diversity is a strength”.A Syrian Christian from insideAleppo, who gave his name as George,said: “After the rebels took control, wethought we would be in danger, but thatdid not happen.“However, we were very surprisedby the withdrawal of the Syrian armyand its allies. I think that was agreedupon by the major countries. We are inour homes and praying to be safe.“Some neighbourhoods were subjected to air strikes and that must stopimmediately. There are civilians andthey should not be bombed.”Mohammad Malah, another resident,said: “Suddenly, the Syrian army andits allies withdrew. We thought thatthey would resist more, but that did nothappen. So far, we are fine, but the situation is very complicated and we do notknow what to do or what will happen inthe future.”Dr Abu Aboida, another Aleppo resident, said the rebel forces who capturedthe city had treated residents well.He said: “We don’t feel afraid of theanti-government military, but we areafraid of the air strikes. The strikes areattacking everywhere. Yesterday, wehad five or six strikes and a lot of civilianpeople died.”Iran sends militias to help Assad fight rebelsTehran says it will providefull support to prop up thebeleaguered Syria dictatoras China also backs regimetator Bashar al-Assad’s regime couldtrigger a “flood” of refugees.The UK has granted asylum to 41,216Syrians since 2014, mostly through aresettlement scheme, but that has beengradually scaled down. Some 3,300 hadtheir initial claims rejected.In the year ending September 2024,5,548 Syrians claimed asylum in the UK– a record high for the group and thefourth highest of any nationality.Roughly half of these arrived via theChannel and 99 per cent of Syrians whoarrive in the UK are granted asylum.Since the start of 2014, 1.6 million Syrian asylum seekers have arrived in theEuropean Union, peaking in 2015 and2016, when 693,175 arrived over thecourse of two years.Julien Barnes-Darcy, from the European Council on Foreign Relationsthink tank, said the capture of Aleppowas a “stunning reversal and has reallyreignited the civil war”.However, he added that Assad’sregime could not tolerate the loss of thecity and would strike back viciously,forcing people to flee. “There is a bigfear about refugee outflows and whatthat means,” he told The Telegraph.‘Invest in defence andsend a strong signal toPutin and other adversariesthat would do us harm’Washington and the United ArabEmirates have reportedly been discussing driving a wedge into the Tehran-Damascus alliance by lifting sanctions onAssad if he peels himself away from Iranand cuts off weapons routes to Lebanon’s Hezbollah.Hezbollah has been a key bulwark forAssad, having fought for his regimesince 2012.However, the group’s leadership andinfrastructure have been hammered byan intense Israeli assault in southernLebanon over recent weeks, and it is not‘After the rebels tookcontrol, we thought wewould be in danger, butthat did not happen’STEVE FINN6 *** Tuesday 3 December 2024 The Daily TelegraphI was naive to believe the BBC would tackle the Gregg Wallace problemIN 2017, my daughter was 18 monthsold, hardly sleeping and I was onlyworking two short days a week. Yes, itwas on a job I loved – Radio 4 – and Iwas ridiculously lucky to have becomea mother at the age of 43, but I wasgoing crazy. So when my agentsuggested Celebrity MasterChef, Ijumped at the chance.But on the fi rst day on set, I had anasty shock. I placed my fi rst dishdown in front of Gregg Wallace andJohn Torode and the director shouted“cut” as they reset the shot. In themeantime, Gregg and John just staredcoolly at me – not hostile, just stayingin character I suppose.Then Gregg asked me if I knew aperson at Radio 4 and, delighted to havethe ice broken, I enthused: “Oh yes – Ido!” He narrowed his eyes and saidBy Daily Telegraph ReporterCALLING a colleague sour and bittercould be sexist, a tribunal appeal hasheard.Sue Kemsley, a former Cambridgeshire county council archivist, sued forsexual harassment after she was maderedundant in 2019 over her managerreferring to her as “that woman” andcalling her a “sour and bitter individual”in an email.Her case was dismissed, but anappeal judge has ruled Ms Kemsley canresue the council for sexual harassment, unfair dismissal and age discrimination.The judge ruled the case should bereheard after stating the original panelrelied too heavily on the council’s comments, disregarding Ms Kemsley’s.Ms Kemsley had an “uneasy” working relationship with Richard Andersonwhich got “out of hand” during her tenure, resulting in her threatening to raisea grievance against him, an employment tribunal in Norwich heard.She started working as a data inputter for the Cambridgeshire countycouncil’s archives service in 2006. By2014, her relationship with Mr Anderson had “entirely broken down”, resulting in Ms Kemsley asking for a diff erentinterim manager, which the councilagreed to, but four years later hebecame her line manager again.When Ms Kemsley was made awareof this, she “made it clear” she did notwant the archivist in charge of her and“threatened to raise a formal grievanceif he were her line manager”.After alleging she had been treated inan “unacceptable manner” by MrAnderson, another line manager – AlanAkeroyd – was appointed.In the summer of 2019, Ms Kemsleywas made redundant and she later suedfor unfair dismissal, sex harassment,and age discrimination.Employment Judge Robin Postleheard evidence in which Mr Andersondescribed the council worker as a “sourand bitter individual” in an email to MrAkeroyd. But the fi rst tribunal in January 2022 ruled this was not related toher sex because of his use of the word“individual”, which refers to “men andwomen equally”.The council worker has now won anappeal against the ruling at an Employment Appeal Tribunal.Judge Charles Bourne said thejudgment failed to suffl ciently acknowledge Ms Kemsley’s case.Referring to the “sour and bitter”email, Ms Kemsley told the appeal hearing the fi nding that the comments werenot related to her sex was “perverse”.“In his email of 10 Aug 2018, which Isaw on 19 July 2019, Mr Andersonreferred to me as a sour and bitter individual,” Ms Kemsley told the panel.“While off ensive, I would like to askthe tribunal to consider whether it isrelated to sex, bearing in mind that healso referred to me as ‘that woman’.”The new panel approved the case tobe handed back down for a full employment hearing.Sexist to refer to a colleague assour and bitter, tribunal hearsNews‘He referred to me as asour and bitter individual…he also referred to meas “that woman”’By Charles HymasHOME AFFAIRS EDITORSTALKING victims are to be granted alegal right to know the identity of theperson who is targeting them under anew law.Police will be placed under a statutory duty to tell a victim who is pursuing them “at the earliest opportunity”.The “right to know” rules will beenshrined in law after police were lambasted by offl cial watchdogs for failingto protect stalking victims by not identifying, investigating or prosecutingthose who were plaguing their lives.About one in seven people aged 16and over in England and Wales has beena victim of stalking at least once, butonly 5 per cent of reports made to policeresult in a charge by the Crown Prosecution Service (CPS).Police dropped their investigations in30,000 cases - a quarter of the total -because of “evidential diffl culties”, eventhough the victim supported actionagainst the perpetrator.The new statutory guidance forpolice was inspired by the experience ofbroadcaster Nicola Thorp, a former Coronation Street actor who was stalkedand abused online in a two-year ordeal.She was told by police they could notreveal the identity of the off ender evenafter he was arrested, despite theperpetrator once saying he hadcome so close to Ms Thorp on atrain that he “could smell” her.He also set up almost 30 socialmedia accounts to send her astream of violent misogynisticmessages.The man, who calledh i m s e l f t h e G r i mReaper, is serving a30 -month pri s onsentence and washanded a lifetimerestraining orderafter his appearance in court –which was thefirst time MsThorp learned ofhis true identity.Ms Thorp, who has been workingwith ministers on the plans, said: “Fortoo long, stalking victims have been atthe mercy not only of their stalker, buta justice system that failed to protectthem. These new measures willempower victims to regain somemuch-needed control of theirlives and police to bring abusersto justice.”Victims will also be able tosecure protection orders againstthe person targeting them moreeasily, barring them fromcoming within a certaindistance of them ormaking contact.T h e y w i l l b eextended to allowv i c ti m s t o s e e korders from thecourts through theirlawyers – even incases where theirsuspected stalkerwas acquitted – provided there is enoughevidence to suggestthey still pose a risk.Soap opera star’s stalking ordealgives rise to right-to-know rulingreveal the identity of the off ender evenafter he was arrested, despite theperpetrator once saying he hadcome so close to Ms Thorp on atrain that he “could smell” her.He also set up almost 30 socialmedia accounts to send her astream of violent misogynisticThe man, who calledh i m s e l f t h e G r i mReaper, is serving a30 -month pri s onsentence and washanded a lifetimerestraining orderafter his appearance in court –which was thefirst time MsThorp learned ofhis true identity.a justice system that failed to protectthem. These new measures willempower victims to regain somemuch-needed control of theirlives and police to bring abusersto justice.”Victims will also be able tosecure protection orders againstthe person targeting them moreeasily, barring them fromcoming within a certaindistance of them ormaking contact.T h e y w i l l b eextended to allowv i c ti m s t o s e e korders from thecourts through theirlawyers – even incases where theirsuspected stalkerwas acquitted – provided there is enoughevidence to suggestthey still pose a risk.Coronation Streetactor Nicola Thorpcalmly: “Tell her from me ... she’s a sexyb----.” He didn’t say it with a laugh in hisvoice or any aggression. He stated it as afact. I was blown away. None of it madesense; it was a non-sequitur. Who saysstuff like that? There was a long silence– but maybe it was just a second – andthen I did what people do when they areuncomfortable – I fi lled the gap bylaughing. My laughter faded away andboth Gregg and John were still juststaring. It felt like a trap, or a joke – orboth. I was so confused. I looked down,my cheeks burning.Then the director told the cameraoperator to start up again as the judgesenthusiastically tasted my dish –noodles and broccoli, I recall – and toldme it was quite nice. I picked up theplate and stumbled back to my station.Ulrika Jonsson, I think, gave me aninquiring thumbs-up. I just nodded.What the heck just happened there? Idon’t think I even mentioned it to manyof the other contestants – I was justnumb. Surely the producers and crewhad heard? He had said it loudly. But noone said anything so it was left to dancearound my head. I put on a happy faceand fi lmed two more days, then I wasgone – disappointed but relieved to getout of the weird cult-like atmosphere.His words burnt in my mind for thenext six months. It wasn’t just what hesaid, but it was how it made me feel. Hemade me feel small and humiliated andpowerless. He controlled everything.It was a bit like: now I’ve had my fun,let’s smile and say how tasty yourcooking is! It felt psychopathic.Why did I not complainstraightaway? Because no one seemedshocked, no one showed any care orinterest, it was business as usual. Whatchanged my mind was that I heard fromcontestants who stayed in, that Wallacehad said while the cameras were rolling,to one female contestant who didn’t likehandling fi sh: “You look like a rapistdoing foreplay.”Another contestant had walked outof the room in disgust – a fuss wasrightly made and Wallace was made toapologise. I didn’t have the guts to takeon these TV titans. I had to put it right– the series was about to air so I draftedan email to Endemol Shine. I got noreply and was later told it had gone tothe wrong address although it did notbounce back. So I went to the top andeventually emailed Kate Phillips, headof entertainment at the BBC.It turned out that both the “sexyb----” and “rapist” incidents had beenlogged, that Wallace had been warnedand that this would not happen again. Ifonly someone had had the courtesy totell me that the incidents had beenconsidered serious enough to belogged; that I was not going mad. TheBBC made me feel that things would befi xed; I naively believed they would sortthis. Subsequent allegations not just onMasterChef but on two other showswould suggest I was a fool.Since I recounted my experience, thereaction has been predictable andlargely binary. How disgusting, sackhim! Never liked him anyway. Or: whydid you wait until now, you snow ake?It’s astonishing how the “sticks andstones” brigade are so entrenched intheir view they think a rape joke is fi ne.But two things are more interesting.People who would usually say I ampathetic have been so outraged byWallace’s “middle-class women of acertain age” comment that they havereluctantly found themselves on theopposite side of the debate. WhenWallace said the quiet part out loud– middle-aged, middle-class womendon’t like me and I don’t like them – hejust stated the bleedin’ obvious but veryimportant: we have nothing to lose atthis age except our integrity. When wesit next to younger women beingharassed on the train, we have nothingto lose. When we refuse the unwantedadvances of misguided men, we don’tfeel the need to apologise. In a way webecome a lot more like men, althoughnot all men.The other thing I’ve noticed has beenthe huge cross-section of people whohave got in touch to tell me that they aredisgusted by Wallace’s comments andthe allegations against him. Dads,grannies, women of a certain age,students, bricklayers, electricians.There’s plenty on the other side, too,but this seems a very personal divide –in uenced by personal experience orhow much of a culture warrior youalready are.At best, Gregg Wallace is a bully andan unpleasant person to work with. Butthe BBC and the production companiesthat indulged him need to look atthemselves, too.They were complicit. They gave himtoo many chances – and we’ll probablynever know how many or why. Mymum and dad read this paper everymorning. I haven’t been able to bringmyself to explain to my dad what’sgoing on. I spoke to my mum and shesaid: “OK if you’re sure, I support you –but the BBC will never employ youagain.” I don’t know that that is true– BBC News go pretty heavily in onother parts of the corporation whenthere is a scandal – Huw Edwards,Jimmy Savile, Strictly etc – but even ifit’s the case, I really don’t care. Maybeit’s because I’m a middle-class womanof a certain age. We. Are. Fearless.I was naive to believe the BBC would tackle the Gregg Wallace problemCommentaryBy Aasmah MirBy Investigations TeamMELANIE SYKES, the former televisionpresenter, has said that an experiencewith Gregg Wallace led her to quit herscreen career.Sykes, who appeared in the 2021series of Celebrity MasterChef, said MrWallace had greeted her on set by asking if models eat, which she said shefound “unprofessional” and driven by“ignorance and disrespect with an extrahelping of arrogance”.She also claimed that he spent time“barking orders” in a way that made herwant to leave.Sykes said she made an informal complaint about his behaviour on the show,and that she found some of the “unprofessional” conduct on the set “jawdropping”.The former presenter, who is bestknown for the Boddingtons advert inthe 1990s and as the co-host of Todaywith Des and Mel in the early 2000s,made the comments in her autobiography published last year, and elaboratedon them in recent days in a video postedto YouTube.In her book, Illuminated, Sykeswrote: “The MasterChef experiencecontinued to be an eye-opener, andsome unprofessional behaviour on setwas jaw-dropping.”While she did not give further detailsin her book, she said in her recent YouTube video that “every time Greggcame over to the desk, I didn’t really likehim being around really because it’s allabout vibrations and energy”.Mr Wallace, 60, “stepped away” fromthe cooking show last week in the wakeof allegations published by this newspaper as part of a four-month investigation into his alleged behaviour.At the end of fi lming the show, Sykessaid that she had a short conversationwith Mr Wallace, which was instrumental in her leaving television.The former greengrocer told her thatappearing on the show would do a lotfor her. “I didn’t know what to say, so Ismiled and said yes, but I was reallythinking, ‘Yes, you have fi nally helpedme decide to end my television careeronce and for all’. I was done,” Sykeswrote.Sykes fi nished fi lming a series of ShopWell For Less, but subsequently steppedaway from her television career andmoved to Lancashire.In her YouTube video, she alsoclaimed that you are “not allowed” tocriticise other stars when you are a presenter, and called for reform of the TVindustry.In the caption for her video, she said:“If production companies and channelsfacilitate unprofessionalism, ie, ignoreit, they too are a massive part of theproblem.”Speaking generally about why the TVindustry fails to crack down on allegations of poor behaviour by celebrities,she said: “I know that people think, ‘Ohwell, you know we can’t get rid of himreally because it’s such a successfulshow’, and it’s sort of fear about changing the format in case viewers don’twatch. So you don’t let [the talent knowthey are] misbehaving and not reallybright and just a bit of a prick. Butthere’s plenty of them out there and Ijust fi nd it amazing.”Lawyers for Mr Wallace have deniedthat he has engaged in any sexually harassing behaviour.Working withWallace mademe give upTV, says Sykes‘Jaw-dropping’ experiencehelped former presenter‘decide to end televisioncareer once and for all’‘It wasn’t just what he said.He made me feel small andhumiliated and powerless.He controlled everything’Continued from Page 1risk” rather than tackling problemshead on.One source said: “The issue iswhether they have the will to pull themup on their behaviour, and whetherthere’s ever any will in these caseswhere there’s big name talent or seniorleaders involved.”Lisa Nandy, the Culture Secretary,met BBC bosses over the weekend,Downing Street said. A spokesman saidshe spoke about “this matter and widerworkplace culture issues to seek assurances that there are robust processes inplace to deal with complaints”.Mr Wallace’s lawyers have said theclaims against him have no basis, whilethe presenter used Instagram yesterdayto apologise for his comments about“middle-class women”. He said: “I wantto apologise for any offence that Icaused with my post yesterday and anyupset I may have caused to a lot of people. I wasn’t in a good head space whenI posted it. I’ve been under a hugeamount of stress, a lot of emotion. I feltvery alone, under siege yesterday whenI posted it.”Dr Huq, the MP for Ealing Central andActon, said that although Mr Wallacehad stepped back that would make nodifference because he would still be“dangled on our screens”.Speaking on BBC Radio 4’s Todayprogramme, she said: “Possibly there isan argument for pausing [broadcasts]while this investigation takes its courseand maybe not airing it tonight. It couldbe massively triggering for the womeninvolved – in fact, any woman involvedin any type of similar incidents. Becauseotherwise – I know you’re saying he’sstopped presenting – but to the casualviewer there’s not going to be any difference if it’s on TV tonight. It looks likehe got away with it, and I think the BBCshould send a strong signal.”Banijay UK, MasterChef’s producer,suggested the show was not underthreat, saying production would be“unaff ected”.
