Left, Gregg Wallace apologises on Instagram and Dec 18The Daily Telegraph Tuesday 3 December 2024 *** 7By Nick GutteridgeCHIEF POLITICAL CORRESPONDENTTAXPAYERS face having to foot a bill ofmore than £50 million a year to coverthe cost of the National Insurance raidon train companies.Rail companies are expected to beable to charge the extra tax back to theGovernment, reducing the amount ofcash that the increase will raise.It comes as the Tories today launch alast ditch bid in Parliament to stop the“jobs tax” and accuse Labour of waginga “war on business”.Currently a network of 16 privatetrain companies are paid a set amountby the Government to deliver most ofthe rail services across England.They do not receive any of the pro tsmade from running the routes, but as aresult are allowed to bill unexpectedcosts to the taxpayer.The train companies employ about57,000 sta on an average basic salaryof almost £42,000, according to theO ce of Road and Rail. From April, theextra National Insurance on a £42,000salary will be about £1,000. Industrysources said that they will almost certainly be able to bill the cost to the taxpayer as an “allowable expense” outsideof their contracts.It comes amid warnings that businesses could have to cut up to 130,000jobs in response to the budget, whilstthe public sector is protected.Economists have said private sector rms are also expected to slash salaryincreases for their staff and to put upprices to recoup the £18 billion a yearcost. However, Ms Reeves has set aside£6 billion a year to compensate publicbodies.That is expected to cover the e ect ofthe hike for rail companies that arealready owned by the public, whichinclude Transport for London.A Treasury spokesman said: “All trainoperating company costs will be considered in the usual way, in annual business planning discussions with theDepartment for Transport.”NI rise to costtaxpayer £50min payouts totrain companies‘All train operatingcompany costs will beconsidered in the usualway, in annual ... planning’By Danielle Sheridan DEFENCE EDITORRUSSIA is exploiting the UK’s dependence on technology to cause “maximumdisruption and destruction”, the UK’scyber security chief has warned.In his first major speech, RichardHorne, the head of GCHQ’s NationalCyber Security Centre (NCSC), willtoday highlight the “widening gap”between the threats posed by hostileactors and the defences in place to protect businesses and public serviceswithin the UK.Speaking at the launch of the NCSC’sannual review in London, Mr Hornewill say: “Hostile activity in UK cyberspace has increased in frequency,sophistication and intensity. We see thisin the intelligence we can accessthrough being part of GCHQ.“Actors are increasingly using ourtechnology dependence against us,seeking to cause maximum disruptionand destruction.”The threats are understood to comefrom both state-backed hackers andonline criminals.The NCSC’s report shows a threefoldincrease in the most serious cyber incidents a ecting the UK in 2023-24.In all, the NCSC received 1,957reports of cyber attacks, 430 of whichneeded support from the centre’s incident management team, a rise of 371from the previous year.Of these incidents, 89 were nationallysigni cant, 12 of which were at the topend of the scale and more severe innature. Despite the rising figures MrHorne will warn that the danger is stillbeing “widely underestimated” by bothpublic and private sector organisations.Mr Horne, who took over as the cybersecurity agency’s chief in October, willadd: “What has struck me more forcefully than anything else since taking thehelm at the NCSC is the clearly widening gap between the exposure andthreat we face, and the defences that arein place to protect us.“And what is equally clear to me isthat we all need to increase the pace weare working at to keep ahead of ouradversaries.”It comes after Pat McFadden, theChancellor of the Duchy of Lancaster,last month warned that Moscow is prepared to launch a wave of cyber attacksacross Britain in a deliberate attempt tocause havoc on the UK’s critical infrastructure. Mr McFadden’s warningscame after the director general of MI5warned that Russian spies are targetingBritain with Cold War-style sabotage,by targeting businesses to underminethe UK’s security and economy.Mr Horne will add: “Last week, theChancellor of the Duchy of Lancasterwarned about the aggression and recklessness of cyber activity we see comingfrom Russia. And with our partners,including at the NPSA (National Protective Security Authority), we can see howcyber attacks are increasingly important to Russian actors, along with sabotage threats to physical security, whichthe director general of MI5 spoke aboutrecently.“All the while, China remains a highlysophisticated cyber actor, with increasing ambition to project its influencebeyond its borders.“And yet, despite all this, we believethe severity of the risk facing the UK isbeing widely underestimated.”Mr Horne will warn that the UK mustnot be complacent regarding the severelevel of threats posed by cybercriminals.The NCSC’s report described Russiaas a “capable, motivated and irresponsible threat actor in cyberspace”, andthrough its actions in Ukraine MrPutin’s government is also inspiring“non-state threat actors” that are noto cially linked to the Kremlin to carryout cyber attacks against criticalnational infrastructure.It adds that Chinese hackers such asthe Volt Typhoon group had targetedUS infrastructure and “could be layingthe groundwork for future disruptiveand destructive cyber attacks”, while inthe UK, Beijing-linked groups arebelieved to have targeted MPs’ emailsand the Electoral Commission’sdatabase.The report also warns that Iran “isdeveloping its cyber capabilities and iswilling to target the UK to ful l its disruptive and destructive objectives”,while North Korean hackers were targetting cryptocurrency to raise revenueand attempting to steal defence data toimprove Pyongyang’s internal securityand military capabilities.Russia exploiting our reliance on technologyNational Cyber SecurityCentre’s new chief warnsMoscow is seeking to cause‘maximum destruction’‘Hostile activity in UKcyberspace has increasedin frequency, sophisticationand intensity’By Poppy Wood EDUCATION EDITORMARLBOROUGH COLLEGE has cancelled its summer school after 50 yearsbecause of the impact of RachelReeves’s Budget.The Wiltshire boarding school,whose alumni includes the Princess ofWales, said its annual summer eventsprogramme would not take place in2025 because of a “series of factors”,including “additional taxation measuresannounced in the October Budget”.It said increases to National Insurance and minimum wage unveiled bythe Chancellor, plus the Government’splans to hit private schools with VAT,had made the current pricing model forits summer school “untenable”. The£59,142-a-year school said the changescoincided with a “sudden loss of severalexperienced members of staff ” on itssummer school team. It will halt theevent to reconsider costs, with the hopeof relaunching it in years to come.Established in 1974, the summerschool has seen Marlborough Collegeopen its historic grounds each July for afour-week event, offering more than500 courses. Catering for students“from three years old to 90 yearsyoung”, it usually o ers week-long programmes in subjects ranging fromwatercolour painting to Napoleonic history, ballroom dancing and ying.The courses, which started at £215for five half-day sessions in this summer’s event, were previously exemptfrom VAT but will be hit with the taxunder new rules coming into force fromJan 1. Guidance published by the Treasury last month said “extra-curricularactivities which are educational innature will be subject to VAT when provided by a private school” from January.Some could still be exempt if they canbe proven to constitute “childcare-based holiday clubs”.Private schools have warned theycould be hit with a “double whammy” ofVAT and increased National Insurancecontributions under measures unveiledin the Budget. From April 2025, privateschools in England with charitable status will also be forced to pay full business rates on their buildings for the  rsttime. The most elite private schoolshave largely remained quiet on the Government’s VAT raid, but The Telegraphrevealed in October that more than1,400 private schools will sue the Government en masse over the policy,including leading institutions.Marlborough College’s most recentcompany accounts said while the schoolenjoyed “another year of very strongpupil numbers” in 2023 with more than1,000 on its roll, “there is also the futurethreat on affordability with Labour’spledge to put 20 per cent VAT on schoolfees”. It said Marlborough College’scouncil was “looking to try to mitigatethis as much as possible”. Boarding feesrose from £15,665 per term last year to£19,714 this year including VAT, according to the school’s website.M a r l b o r o u g h C o l l e g e w a sapproached for comment.Marlborough College axes summer schoolBy Dominic PennaPOLITICAL CORRESPONDENTAN OFFICIAL who compared Covid tochickenpox has been made the country’s top civil servant.Sir Chri s Wormald has b e enappointed the new Cabinet Secretaryand tasked by the Prime Minister withthe “complete rewiring” of the Britishstate. The 56-year-old was permanentsecretary at the Department of Healthand Social Care (DHSC) for the pasteight years, giving him key responsibilities during the pandemic. He will takeup his new post on Dec 16 and replacesSimon Case, who is stepping down onhealth grounds after he was diagnosedwith a neurological condition last year.Messages exchanged with Lord Sedwill about a fortnight before the firstlockdown and released as part of theCovid Inquiry showed Sir Chris backedherd immunity at the start of the pandemic. On March 12, 2020, the day BorisJohnson encouraged people to stay athome for seven days if they had symptoms, Lord Sedwill wrote: “I don’t thinkPM & Co have internalised yet the distinction between minimising mortalityand not trying to stop most people getting it.“Indeed presumably like chickenpox, we want people to get it anddevelop herd immunity before the nextwave. We just want them not to get it allat once and preferably when it’s [warm]and dry etc.”Sir Chris replied: “Exactly right. Wemake the point every meeting, theydon’t quite get it.”Sir Keir Starmer said Sir Chris’s workwould be pivotal in working to “changethe way government serves thiscountry”.He said: “From breaking down silosacross government to harnessing theincredible potential of technology andinnovation, it will require nothing lessthan the complete rewiring of the British state to deliver bold and ambitiouslong-term reform.” He added there was“no one better placed” to push throughhis flagship Plan for Change than SirChris.Sir Chris said: “I am delighted thatthe Prime Minister has appointed me tothe privileged role of leading our talented Civil Service, as we rise to thechallenge of delivering the Government’s focused agenda to deliver itsPlan for Change.“The Government has set a clearmandate – an ambitious agenda withworking people at its heart. That willrequire each and every one of us toembrace the change agenda in how theBritish state operates.“So I look forward to working withleaders across Government, to ensurethat the Civil Service has the skills theyneed to deliver across the breadth of thecountry.”Before his stint at DHSC, Sir Chriswas a permanent secretary at theDepartment for Education between2012 and 2016 under Michael Gove andthen Baroness Morgan.Case successor likened Covid to chickenpoxThe only problem with Labour’s tripartite Chagos plan? Two parts hate itTHIS is a genuine quote on the Chagosnegotiations: “We are not trying torush the deal,” said a governmentsource, “our ambition is simply to getthe deal done as quickly as possible.”The problem? It’s a tripartite planand two parts now hate it: Mauritius,which upon re ection has probablydecided it would like some moremoney (“make it out to CASH”) and thepresident-elect Donald Trump, who,after watching a video made by a14-year old in Wanstead, has decidedit’s a sell-out to al-Qaeda.The Tories posed a question; LukePollard, minister for catapults, came toanswer, supported by StephenDoughty, a large, sweaty man whoalways looks as if he ran for, andmissed, a bus. As Doughty struggled tocatch his breath, Pollard reassured theHouse that the deal “is backed by theentirety of the US security apparatus.”But Trump is planning to put all thosepeople in jail and, as Julian Lewispointed out, it’s more useful to havethe support of the “incomingadministration” than the “outgoing”one. Nigel Farage observed that “manypeople think [Biden] is gone already.”Doughty broke into a hot sweat.Nige claimed to have spoken withRepublican operatives (Roseanne Barrspeed-dialling on an Ambien rush) and“every single one of them is appalledby this deal.” Sir Bernard Jenkin askedif Labour was pursuing “informaldialogue” with the Trump team, whichwould be easily done. Just buy a drinkfor any stripper in the Palm Beach area.Doughty gripped his right arm.Pollard ploughed on, telling MPs thatthey would surely endorse the dealwhen they saw the  nal language. ToryJames Cartlidge (shadow catapults),became irate, cupped his hand andwaved it side to side, mid-air. Sourcesin the Conservative Party noted that hehad a pen between his  ngers, so thiscouldn’t have meant “Barclay’sbanker”. Sources at the Chagossianembassy said that in their culture, thepen makes the gesture a thousandtimes worse. Something about forcedcongress with a squid.Doughty slumped forward, dead –reducing the number of MPs on thegovernment side to nine (that’s howseriously Labour takes this issue).Tan Dhesi, new chairman of thedefence committee, proved his worthwith a surgical analysis of the situation:“The UK/US base on Diego Garcia is ofgreat signi cance in terms of defenceas well as in terms of strategic, erm,international signi cance… So can theminister advise what furtherrepresentations have been made toboth of our partners to ensure that wehave the support of our internationalpartners?”Do our signi cant partnersappreciate the signi cance of theirpartnership? I dunno. But if theGovernment can’t ram this dealthrough pre-inauguration, and ifTrump then tanks it, it’ll be the  rstcase in history of a monarchy beingordered to keep its empire by arepublic.Poor, dear Chagos, tropical paradiseof our Victorian dreams. I googled theweather for today: “thundery showersand a gentle breeze.” Still, I bet thecockroaches are grateful for thatgentle breeze.It’ll be the first case inhistory of a monarchybeing ordered to keepits empire by a republicSketchBy Tim StanleyDowning Street decorations Sir Keir Starmer, the Prime Minister, and his wife, Lady Starmer, are joined by health service workers, military families andmembers of the police during the offcial lighting of the Christmas tree outside No10 last night, where they were regaled with carols from an NHS choir.LEON NEAL/GETTY IMAGESNews8 *** Tuesday 3 December 2024 The Daily TelegraphNewsBy Hayley DixonSPECIAL CORRESPONDENTTHE leader of a Russian spy ringdescribed one of his operatives as a“true sexy b----” as he discussed usingher as a honeytrap, a court has heard.Female spy Vanya Gaberova, 30,befriended investigative journalistChristo Grozev on social media. Shewas to gather information on behalf ofthe Kremlin, it is alleged.Jan Marsalek, the Russian agent controlling the team, and Orlin Roussev,the ringleader managing UK operations, praised the beauty salon manageras “very assertive and strongly independent”. The Old Bailey was told MrRoussev called her a “true sexy b----”.“They clearly considered that [MsGaberova] would be capable of pullingo a honeytrap operation without therisk that she would fall in love with [MrGrozev],” said prosecutor Alison Morgan KC.They had discussed kidnapping MrGrozev, who at the time was lead Russia investigator for Bellingcat, aninvestigative journalism group.The details have emerged duringthe trial of Ms Gaberova, love rivalKatrin Ivanova, 33, and ex-boyfriendTihomir Ivanchev, 39. All three denyconspiracy to spy on behalf of Russiabetween 2020 and 2023.Mr Roussev, 46, and Bizer Dzhambazov, 43, have already admittedthe charge.The prosecution alleges thatMr Marsalek, an Austrian, wasthe Russian agent using an alias“Rupert Ticz” who engaged MrRoussev, who in turn passed onthe instructions to the other Bulgarians who were all living inthe UK.The trial, which enteredits second day yesterday,has heard that they plotted“high-level espionage”Honeytrap spywas ‘sexy b----’ butno sucker for loveNo risk of Russian suspectfalling for dissident targetas she was ‘very assertiveand strongly independent’and used sophisticated equipmentincluding drones and devices to intercept data from nearby mobile phones.Jurors will be told about six “operations” including the surveillance ofPatch Barracks, a US military base inStuttgart, which the ring thought wasbeing used to train Ukrainian  ghtersdefending their country against theRussian invasion.They also planned to stage a demonstration outside the Kazakh embassy“to create a pretense that they were inpossession of genuine intelligenceabout those responsible” so they couldpass it to the Kazakh secret service “togain favour… on behalf of Russia”.And they targeted Bergey Ryskaliyev, a former Kazakh politician who hasbeen the subject of “politically motivated” criminal charges, RomanDobrokhotov, the editor of investigative media site The Insider and Russianlawyer Kirill Kachur.Mr Roussev and Mr Marsalek discussed robbing Mr Grozev, putting himunder surveillance, kidnapping himand taking him to Moscow or possiblykilling him, the court heard.He is considered an opponent by theKremlin as his work includes uncovering Russian links to the 2018 Salisburyattack, the poisoning of oppositionleader Alexei Navalny, the downing ofMH17, the Malaysian Airlines plane shotdown over Ukraine in 2014, and theactivities of intelligence agencies.The Bulgarians followed Mr Grozevin Montenegro, Valencia and Viennaand passed this information back to MrMarsalek. They also had his villa inBulgaria under surveillance.Ms Gaberova sent Mr Grozev afriend request on Facebook in August2021, which they noted he was “quickto accept”.Messages shown to the jury showthat Mr Roussev told Mr Marsalekthat “Grozev seems hooked and inlove with Vanya.. he has startedliking her pic… posts”.He suggested setting up a dateand said that they “can de nitelyrecord something” for PornHub,adding: “The girl is real hot. Sheis a swinger, too.”The case continues.Vanya Gaberovabefriended ajournalistconsidered hostileto the Kremlin BUTTERCUPS/FERRARI PRESS AGENCYBy Albert TaitAN animal sanctuary has blamed viralvideos on the internet for a suddenin ux of abandoned goats.Buttercups Sanctuary in Kent hastaken in 30 goats in the past threeweeks, pushing its site to capacity andleaving it “bursting at the seams”.Its herd now includes 130 animals,some of which are former pets that havebeen abandoned by unknown owners.Sta said more people have boughtpet goats in recent years after watchinghumorous clips of the animals. Videosof goats have been popular on the internet for decades – a clip of a goat screaming like a man went viral in 2007 – butthey have become more popular withthe rise of social media sites such asTikTok.However, owners are said to be abandoning their pets after realising they aretoo di cult to care for, with expensivefencing, stabling, feed and enrichmentall needed.Ben Ward, 26, head of hoof stock atthe shelter, said: “In the past few yearsthere have been a lot more funny goatvideos on social media sites, and peopleGoat sanctuary is overwhelmed by unwanted petssee them and want a pet goat – but theyjust don’t realise how much work it is.“There’s also the fact that you can getgoats for less money than a dog – youcould probably  nd one for £50 if youwent to certain markets.”He added: “The popularity does helpus in terms of fostering, because morepeople can take on goats and free upspace for more rescues, but people needto be aware of the responsibility..”Matt Huggins, Buttercups communications administrator, told the BBC: “It’san emergency. Sheis a swinger, too.”The Daily Telegraph Tuesday 3 December 2024 *** 9By Fiona ParkerSENIOR NEWS REPORTERTHE oldest victim of the Post Officescandal has described being treated“like dung” after receiving a “paltry”compensation o er.Betty Brown, 91, spent more than£50,000 of her savings when her andher husband’s branch started recordingunexplained losses, before beinghounded out of her job as a sub-postmistress in 2003. She said she has beeno ered 29 per cent of what she claimedin compensation.More than 900 subpostmasters werewrongfully prosecuted as a result of theHorizon scandal, after the faulty Fujitsusoftware incorrectly recorded shortfallson their branch accounts.Mrs Brown was one of the 555sub-postmasters, led by Sir Alan Bates,who won a successful High Court battleagainst the Post O ce in 2019.She told the BBC: “I never expectedthe Post Office to really do what theyshould do and treat us properly.She added: “What do they treat uslike? They treat us like dung.”Mrs Brown’s former branch in Ann- eld Plain, Co Durham, was one of themost successful in her region. She previously told of how she was initiallyreluctant to tell her late husband,Oswall, when losses began mounting atthe branch. She was eventually forcedout of her business by a Post O ce manager who thought she was “too old” andwanted “new blood”. Mrs Brown saidadvisers paid for by the governmentassisted her in preparing multiplereports to back up her detailed claim forcompensation.M o r e i n f o r m a t i o n w a s t h e nrequested by lawyers acting for the government, which oversees the group litigation order for those who wereinvolved in the High Court case. However, she said her eventual “paltry”o er did not include any redress for lossof future earnings. A third of the  gureshe claimed for past loss earnings wasoffered. Mrs Brown was also onlyo ered part of what she claimed for theharassment she experienced, evenwhile the Department for Business andTrade acknowledged she experiencedthis as a result of the issues caused byHorizon. The former subpostmistresshas now rejected the o er and told thebroadcaster she would take her claim toan independent panel for assessment.However, this will take more time.A government spokesman said: “Werecognise the immeasurable su eringthat victims of the Horizon scandal likeBetty have endured and we are workingtirelessly across government to providethem with full and fair redress.”Oldest Post Ofice victim saysshe has been treated ‘like dung’By James Sweetlandand Charles HymasCHIEF CONSTABLES are less experienced on average than at any time in thepast 20 years, an analysis suggests.The 43 chief constables headingforces in England and Wales have onaverage served two years and 10 monthsin their current roles, two years fewerthan the average of four years and twomonths over the past 20 years.This is a further drop on 2018, whenthe average tenure had fallen to3.5 years.The research comes amid a slump inpublic con dence in the police after aseries of misconduct scandals, fallingcharging rates and cuts to neighbourhood policing. The proportion of thepublic saying they have con dence inthe police has fallen from 50 to 38 percent in the past four years, according toYouGov.The data does however show amarked increase in the number ofwomen at the top of policing, which hasdoubled in two decades. Women makeup 36 per cent of current chief constables in post, compared to 14 per centover the past 20 years on average.There are no current chief constablesfrom an ethnic minority background,and only one person from an ethnicminority background – Mike Fuller –has made it to the top job since 2005,according to the data up to Novemberthis year. Mr Fuller was chief constablein Kent from January 2004 to April2010.Dame Sara Thornton, the  rst chairof the National Police Chiefs’ Council(NPCC), said she was “disappointed” bythe decline in experience of the o cersleading forces, suggesting it could belinked to delays in promoting them tothe top jobs.“At the NPCC  ve years ago, I commissioned work which found that (onaverage) chiefs had less experience thanthey used to. It’s disappointing that thesituation has worsened,” she said.“As a chief who spent eight years inone force, I believe that consistency andcontinuity are good for organisations.”Police chiefs’ experience hits 20-year low‘As a chief who spenteight years in one force,I believe that consistencyis good for organisations’Betty Brown ran their Annfield Plain branch alongside her late husband, OswallNewsBy Daily Telegraph ReporterA TRADER spent £1,500 on dinner andchampagne at a club before crashinginto and killing a 12-year-old girl and eeing the scene, a court heard.Edward Tully, 30, who admittedcausing death by dangerous driving andcausing serious injury by dangerousdriving, was jailed for 12 years and disquali ed from driving for 13 years yesterday.Tully had been out with his friends atthe cabaret bar Proud Embankment onthe Victoria Embankment on the nightof the collision on Aug 20 2022.He spent over £1,500 on four fivecourse meals with prosecco, as well asfive bottles of Veuve Cliquot champagne before driving home in his Bentley, which was going between 56 and60mph, double the speed limit.Tully overtook a car at a set of redlights at a junction in Barnet, northLondon, before striking the red Vauxhall Astra that the girl’s mother wasdriving. The Bentley then hit a post on atra c island.The collision sent the Astra spinningacross the junction, leaving the girlunconscious and not breathing and herfather badly injured, while her motherscreamed for help. The girl died in hospital two days later.A witness described someone fromthe Bentley making a gesture as if to say“up yours”, the court heard. Tully thenfled the scene and walked back to hisflat. He then called his father, whopicked him up from his home.After police officers repeatedlyattempted to contact Tully, he handedhimself in to Colindale police station thenext day.Jocelyn Ledward, KC, prosecuting,earlier said: ‘The girl was not conscious.She was not breathing and clearly verybadly injured. Her mother and brotherscreamed for help, and members of thepublic including other drivers came totheir assistance.“While an ambulance was called, oneof them used his tools to cut the girl’sseatbelt, removed her from the car andbegan CPR.“Paramedics arrived, and she wasresuscitated to the point her heart wasre-started, but she remained unconscious. She was taken to the Royal London Hospital. Sadly, she had sustainedbrain and spinal injuries which werenot survivable.”The prosecutor said a receipt fromthe club revealed Tully “spent something of the order of over £1,500 in theclub that night”. Referring to CCTVfrom Proud Embankment Ms Ledwardadded: “The defendant can be seendrinking regularly and repeatedly, over20 times at least, from glasses whichhave been refilled from the bottles,including by himself, throughout thetwo hours.’David Spens, KC, defending, said:“Mr Tully accepts full responsibility forher death. He is genuinely and profoundly sorry. While it may seem inadequate given the circumstances, he isprofoundly sorry for the loss he broughtto this family. He accepts that hedeserves serious punishment.“He left school at the age of 17. Hebuilt two successful businesses,”he added.In a victim impact statement read tothe court, the girl’s mother said: “Mydaughter was my best friend. I relive theincident. I get  ashbacks of that night. Ifeel pain. I feel helpless. This incidenthas caused devastation to a happy family. This incident has taken everythingaway from us.”The judge said that Tully had been“impaired” by alcohol, but added: ”It isnot known precisely how impaired bydrink he was because he took the cowardly decision to  ee the scene.”Tully said “I love you” to his sobbingparents as he was led from the dock.City traderffed crashscene afterkilling girl, 12Bentley driver spent£1,500 on champagnemeal before collision andthen walked back to his fiatBy Daily Telegraph ReporterBRITAIN’S “biggest-ever drug ring”run by a gangster nicknamed “The BigFella” smuggled up to £7 billion worthof heroin, cocaine, amphetamines andcannabis into the UK.Paul Green, 59, was the point of contact for numerous gangs which paid afee to ship drugs into Britain over twoand-a-half years by hiding them in pallets of fresh produce – in particular,onions, garlic and ginger.Green and his fellow conspiratorswent to “extraordinary lengths” to disguise their involvement as they set upfront companies and warehouses in theNetherlands and the north of Englandby using false and stolen identities.Drugs would be hidden among freshproduce at the Dutch end of the enterprise and then shipped into the UK byinnocent haulage  rms before the criminals would remove the drugs for distribution to their fee-paying customers aspart of the “transport service”.Onions, garlic or ginger were oftenthe produce of choice as they helpedconceal any smell of drugs.Among the customers was John Kinsella, the 53-year-old Merseyside mobenforcer who was shot dead by a hitmanin May 2018 as he walked his dogs withhis pregnant partner.Andrew Thomas KC, prosecuting,said a feature of the sequence of conspiracies between March 2016 and September 2018 was the “determination tocontinue the importations even afterarrests and/or drug seizures”.He told jurors: “As soon as one company became exposed, they wouldswitch to another one.”Only six seizures of drugs were madebut National Crime Agency investigators were able to prove at least 240 consignments took place with up to fourshipments per week.Among the many items of evidencegathered were messages on Green’sencrypted EncroChat phone, with hishandler name of “Duckfarmer”.At Manchester Crown Court, Green,from Widnes, Cheshire, was jailed for32 years for conspiracy to import drugsand fraud by false representation. His“right-hand man”, Steven Martin, 53, ofBolton, who organised the finances,was imprisoned for 28 years, whileMuhammad Ovais, 46, of Burnage,Manchester, in charge of distributing togangs, was sentenced to 27 years.It is believed to be the largestdrug-smuggling op eration everdetected in the UK, the court heard.During sentencing, Judge Paul Lawton told gang members: “The harmcaused beyond the importation is incalculable. What you were actually distributing was addiction, misery, socialdegradation and death.”Smugglers hid£7bn of drugsin garlic, gingerand onionsGang leader Paul Green – also known as The Big Fella – used consignments of onions to mask the scent of drugs, aboveEdward Tully’sparents weresobbing as he wasled from the dock tobegin his sentence10 *** Tuesday 3 December 2024 The Daily TelegraphGOING IN TO WESTMINSTER BRIDGE… ...AND COMING OUTNewsBy Daily Telegraph ReporterTHE medals of a First World War fi yingace who went on to spy on the Nazishave gone on sale.Major Christopher Draper shot downnine German aircraft and balloons during the war. The airman was later giventhe nickname “Mad Major” when hefi ew under 14 of London’s bridges in abizarre protest for veterans’ rights.He was a stunt pilot for ff lms in the1930s before becoming a double agentfor MI6. After a chance meeting withAdolf Hitler at a 1932 air show, he wasapproached by the Nazis to see if hewould spy on Britain. Draper informed MI6, which suggested he spy on the enemy instead, andhe worked as a double agent for fouryears. At the start of the Second WorldWar, he returned to the RAF andbecame the only person to commanda squadron in both wars.Draper’s medal group, including aprestigious Distinguished ServiceCross, is expected to fetch £4,500 atthe London-based auctioneers Morton& Eden.James Morton, a specialist at theauctioneers, said: “This award isimportant and has a fantastic doubleagent story behind it.“We are delighted to be able toinclude it in our December sale.”Draper was born in Bebington, Liverpool in 1892 and learnt to fly in 1913before joining the Royal Naval Air Service the year after.During the First World War heMedals of the ‘Mad Major’ who barrelled under bridges go on saleBy Poppy Wood EDUCATION EDITORTRAINEE mechanics and electricianswill be able to work from home for apart of their industry placements, underrules announced yesterday.The Department for Education (DfE)said it had relaxed requirements aroundwork placements for pupils taking theirT-levels – the technical equivalent ofA-levels in England.From January, T-level pupils will beable to complete 20 per cent of their 315-hour work placement requirementremotely “rather than having to do themin a physical workplace”, the DfE said.It means pupils training to becomemechanics, plumbers, electricians andcarpenters will be allowed to carry outup to nine days of their 45-day industryplacements from home.Pupils taking digital T-levels, whichare designed to help teenagers preparefor careers including computer gamesdevelopment, web design and internetsecurity, will be allowed to completehalf of their work placement hoursremotely – or about 23 days.“Simulated activity” will also beallowed to form part of pupils’ industryplacements in some circumstances.The fi exibilities will apply to pupilson all 20 T-level courses except earlyyears and dental nursing, which havespecific requirements to obtain alicence to practise.The DfE said the changes wouldallow the new T-level qualification,which was launched in 2020, to “stayahead of workplace trends, ensuringstudents gain practical experience indynamic, evolving industries”.Gavin Williamson, a former Conservative education secretary who wasinvolved in the launch of T-levels, saidthat “any watering down of the workexperience element is a real shame”.“I’d really urge ministers to look atwhat more support can be given to colleges and employers to help deliverT-levels. It feels as if they’ve gone forthe cheap and easy route as against theright route,” he said.“The whole beneff t of a work placement was getting to see ff rsthand [and]to really understand the work environment… especially when for lots of theseskills, there is a practical element thatyou should be learning as well. So it justseems to work completely counter tothe ethos of what the T-levels are.”Baroness Smith, the skills minister,insisted the Government had “listenedto businesses” about the barriers inplace to boosting uptake of T-levels.Announcing the relaxation of workplacement requirements at the start of“T-level Celebration Week” yesterday,she said ministers were “cutting redtape to make it easier than ever foremployers and providers” to offerT-level placements while ensuring“high-quality experiences” for pupils.T-levels were designed to combineclassroom learning with industry placements and better prepare students forthe world of work.Brought in following calls for technical education reform by former Labourpeer Lord Sainsbury, the two-year qualifications are broadly equivalent tothree A-levels.Pupils spend about 80 per cent oftheir course in the classroom and 20 percent with an employer, with the placement trumpeted as the critical component of the qualiff cation.A government survey from the ff rstT-level cohort, published in April, said“the industry placement was seen asthe most important element in preparing T-level completers for what theywent on to do, followed by technicalknowledge”.However, T-levels have also beenbeset by di culties since their rolloutfour years ago, with a high number ofpupil dropouts. About 29 per cent ofpupils who started T-levels in 2022ditched their courses, with 7,262 receiving their results this summer out of10,253 who signed up.An Ofsted report published last yearalso warned of “teething issues”. Theeducation watchdog said pupilsreported feeling “misled and ill-informed” about the content and structure of T-levels, adding that “the qualityof industry placements varies considerably across individual providers and ondi erent T-level courses”.Previous rules speciff ed that placements “cannot be delivered virtually/remotely, except for work taster activities”, though these were temporarilyrelaxed during the pandemic.Government guidance states thatwhile any work placement days conducted remotely should be done from asuitable environment such as an o ce,pupils can work from home in “exceptional cases” if companies have agreed.The DfE was asked for comment.Trainee mechanics able to work from homeGovernment waters downwork placement rules sostudents can complete partof their T-levels remotelyMORTON AND EDEN/BNPS; TRINITY MIRROR/MIRRORPIX/ALAMY STOCK PHOTO‘The whole benefit of aplacement is getting toreally understand thework environment’years. At the start of the Second WorldWar, he returned to the RAF andbecame the only person to commandDraper’s medal group, including aprestigious Distinguished ServiceCross, is expected to fetch £4,500 atthe London-based auctioneers MortonJames Morton, a specialist at theauctioneers, said: “This award isimportant and has a fantastic doubleMaj Christopher Draper’s medals, which he earned before gaining wider fame In 1953,above, when he flew an Auster Autocrat under 15 of the 18 Thames bridges in LondonBy Tom McArdleTHE discovery of a will is the “smokinggun” evidence in the unsolved murdercase of the Princes in the Tower, historians believe.In 1483, uncrowned Edward V and hisyounger brother Prince Richard, right,disappeared from the Tower of Londonwithout a trace.Many believe they were murdered onthe orders of their uncle, Richard III.But now, the discovery of the willshows the prime suspect in the murdersleft a piece of Edward V’s jewellery tohis sister-in-law.The chain – likely to have been achain of o ce, a golden symbol of statusand his prized possession – was part ofLady Margaret Capell’s estate.She is related to Sir James Tyrell, whowas a trusted knight of Richard III andbelieved by some to have confessed tothe murders under his orders.Prof Tim Thornton, a Royal HistoricalSociety fellow and deputy vice-chancellor of the University of Huddersfield,uncovered the ff nd in Lady Margaret’spreviously overlooked will.In the 1522 document, she left herson his “faders Cheyne which wasyoung king Edwarde Vth” (his father’schain which was young KingEdward V’s).Tracy Borman, who isco-curator of HistoricRoyal Palaces, said the“extremely exciting”ff nd gave credibility tothe theory the boyswere murdered on theorders of their uncle,Richard III.“This chain provides a vital link to theyoung Edward V, andthe fact that it was in thepossession of someoneclosely linked with one ofthe prime suspects in theirpresumed murder could be hugely significant,” she said. She called it the“smoking gun” in the case.The discovery backs up Prof Thornton’s previous written work on SirThomas More, a former lord chancellor,who blamed Richard III for theprinces’ deaths.Prof Thornton told TheTimes: “You usually get atleast some alleged materialsleft by the people whodisappeared, but theywent without a traceuntil now. This, for theff rst time, is an indication that we have aphysical survival fromthe princes.”The research in thejournal History will bepart of tonight’s Channel 5 documentary ThePrinces in the Tower: ADamning Discovery.‘Smoking gun’ find could prove Richard IIIwas behind Princes in the Tower murdersBy Tom McArdleMOST Church of England churchesdeclined to apply for a free portrait ofthe King, new ff gures show.To celebrate the start of his reign,public institutions across the UK wereo ered a photograph of the King wearing the Royal Navy uniform of an Admiral of the Fleet. The picture was takenby phtographer Hugo Burnand atWindsor Castle last year.While the o er was taken up by all 23HM Coastguard centres, just 25 per centof CofE churches said yes.The portrait was ordered by 4,031 ofthe 15,815 churches of which Charles isthe Supreme Governor.His Majesty the King’s PortraitScheme was a voluntary programmeoffering a free, framed portrait of theKing to any eligible public institutionthat requested one.It was launched by the previousgovernment following his Coronationin May 2023 and has cost £2.7 million.The scheme ran from November 2023to last August with more than 20,500portraits provided – with varyingdegrees of acceptance.Lord lieutenancies followed HMCoastguard with the highest rate ofacceptance, as 75 of the 99 agreed tohome one. Then came governmentdepartments, arm’s-length bodies andlocal authorities, cadet forces and coroners’ courts.A third, or 8,384 of the near 30,000schools took up the offer. However,there was less enthusiasm from hospitals with just three per cent – 40 out of1,454 – ordering the photo.Only 35 of 275 universities and highereducation institutes said yes.With varying take-up for the scheme,its deadline was extended repeatedly.Final ff gures from the Cabinet O ceshow a total of 20,565 orders werereceived.This equated to a “total take-upacross the UK from all eligible publicauthorities, including governmentdepartments, arm’s-length bodies andlocal authorities and all other institutions” of 31 per cent.The take-up was notably low frompublic authorities in Scotland, Walesand Northern Ireland, which hadacceptance rates of 13.7, 13.8 and 10.5 percent, respectively.The total cost, which included delivery, was £2,710,705 – an average price of£131.81 per portrait.Editorial Comment: Page 17Churches snub offer of free portrait of theKing – who is their Supreme Governorson his “faders Cheyne which wasyoung king Edwarde Vth” (his father’schain which was young KingTracy Borman, who isco-curator of HistoricRoyal Palaces, said the“extremely exciting”ff nd gave credibility tothe theory the boyswere murdered on theorders of their uncle,“This chain provides a vital link to theyoung Edward V, andthe fact that it was in thepossession of someoneclosely linked with one ofthe prime suspects in theirThomas More, a former lord chancellor,who blamed Richard III for theprinces’ deaths.Prof Thornton toldTimesleast some alleged materialsleft by the people whodisappeared, but theywent without a traceuntil now. This, for theff rst time, is an indication that we have aphysical survival fromthe princes.”journalpart of tonight’s Channel 5 documentaryPrinces in the Tower: ADamning Discovery4,031Number of churches that ordered thefree portrait out of 15,815, around aquarter of the totalduelled with enemy Fokkers before hewas given command of No 8 NavalSquadron in October 1917. He claimedhis final victory in a DWF reconnaissance aircraft in May 1918. He became acommercial pilot in 1919 but was seriously injured in a crash at Hendon Aerodrome in March 1920.Draper then had a career change,becoming a stage actor, while leading protests against the government’s treatment of RAF veterans.In September 1931, he was arrestedafter fi ying a Puss Moth under TowerBridge and Westminster Bridge toraise awareness of the cause.In 1932, he met Hitler, which led tothe Nazis “approaching” him aboutproviding information on British air-ff elds.Draper informed the authoritiesand for the next four years passed onfalse information fed to him by MI6 tothe Germans. The enemy finally realised they were being misled in 1936 andstopped contacting him.In the Second World War, Draperreturned to the RAF as a squadronleader in West Africa and the Far East in777 Fleet Air Arm Squadron.In 1953, in another protest against thetreatment of RAF veterans, he fi ew anAuster Autocrat under 15 of the 18Thames bridges between Blackfriarsand Kew, earning the nickname the“Mad Major”. He was arrested again andfined 10 guineas for flying at under1,000ft in a built-up area.Draper’s pilot’s licence was withdrawn for medical reasons in 1965 andhe died aged 86 in 1979.His medal group consists of a Distinguished Service Cross; 1914-15 Star;British War and Victory Medals; 1939-45 Star; France & Germany Star;Defence and War Medals; France, Croixde Guerre.The sale takes place tomorrow.The Daily Telegraph Tuesday 3 December 2024 *** 11NewsBy Sarah Knapton Science EditorEXCESSIVE belly fat may start causingAlzheimer’s disease 20 years beforesymptoms emerge, new research suggests.Body scans from dozens of middle-aged obese people found that thosewith visceral fat – deep fat that surrounds the stomach, abdomen andorgans – also had higher levels of stickyamyloid and tau tangles in the brain.Amyloid and tau stop brain cells fromcommunicating and are present at highlevels in people with Alzheimer’sdisease.In contrast, people with general subcutaneous fat – the fat under the skinthat you can pinch – showed no increasein amyloid and tau.Symptoms of Alzheimer’s disease donot usually start until mid-60s or later,but the study suggests that people withhigh levels of visceral fat may begin todevelop dementia 20 years earlier.“Our study showed that higher visceral fat was associated with higher levels of the two hallmark pathologicproteins of Alzheimer’s disease-amyloidand tau,” said lead study author DrMahsa Dolatshahi, of Washington University School of Medicine in St Louis.“To our knowledge, our study isthe only one to demonstrate thesefindings at midlife where our participants are decades out from developingthe earliest symptoms of the dementiat h a t re s u l t s f ro m A l zh e i m e r ’sdisease.”“This study reveals key insightsabout why obesity can increase risk forAlzheimer’s disease.”The team also found that people withhigh visceral fat had lower blood flow tothe brain, but there were no blood flowissues for people with high levels ofsubcutaneous fat.Dr Cyrus Raji, an associate professorat the Mallinckrodt Institute of Radiology, said: “Knowing that visceral obesity negatively affects the brain opensup the possibility that treatment withlifestyle modifications or appropriateweight-loss drugs could improve cerebral blood flow and potentially lowerthe burden of and reduce the risk forAlzheimer’s.”Latest figures suggest that nearly 26per cent of adults in England are obesewhile nearly 38 per cent are overweight,but because visceral fat is hidden deepinside the body, it can be difficult tospot.According to Harvard, women withwaists larger than 35 inches and menwith waists above 40 inches are at riskof health problems from visceral fat.As well as Alzheimer’s, too much visceral fat has been linked to raised bloodpressure, stroke, heart attack, type 2diabetes, breast cancer and colorectalcancer.For the new research, 80 people of anaverage age of 49 were given brainscans using positron emission tomography (PET). None showed any signs ofcognitive decline.Participants also had magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) scans of theirabdomen to measure the volume of subcutaneous and visceral fat as well as ametabolic assessment to check theirglucose, insulin levels and cholesterollevels.The findings revealed that higher levels of visceral fat were relatedto increased Alzheimer’s signs in thebrain.Higher insulin resistance and lowergood cholesterolwere also associatedwith high amyloid in the brain.Although previous studies havelinked high BMI to damaged brain cells,no study has investigated how the effectis altered with different types of fat.The researchers believe that helpingpeople stay slimmer could help stave offAlzheimer’s in later life.“This crucial result was discoveredbecause we investigated Alzheimer’sdisease pathology as early as midlife-inthe 40s and 50s-when the disease is atits earliest stages,” added Dr Dolatshahi.The studies were presented at theannual meeting of the RadiologicalSociety of North America in Chicago.Belly fat may be early trigger of Alzheimer’sStudy suggests thatobese people may beginto develop effects ofdementia in their forties‘Treatment with lifestylemodifications couldpotentially lower theburden of Alzheimer’s’By Daily Telegraph ReporterA ROLEX thief who tried to rip an£18,500 watch from a grandfather’swrist had been sought by police fordeportation, a court heard.Amine Bentaib, 30, admitted onecount of attempted robbery and wasjailed for 45 months and made subjectto a criminal behaviour order.He had targeted Christopher Harrisfor deportation since Septemberthe year before.Judge Adam Hiddleston jailed Bentaib for 45 months, adding that he “willqualify as a foreign criminal and be subject to automatic deportation”.The judge Gattenenvironment editorA PIG farm has been suspended from awelfare standards scheme after undercover footage showed its employeesbeating animals with pitchforks.Footage from the Innovis farm inGreat Yarmouth appears to show malepigs being hit with spades and pipes,and forced to walk on concrete coveredin faeces despite injuries and lameness.The Norfolk farm, which is linked tosupermarket pork and bacon suppliers,suspended two members of staff overthe alleged abuse and launched aninternal investigation.A spomesman for the Red Tractorscheme, which certifies farms as meeting standards on animal health and welfare, said: “We are disgusted. Afterverifying the footage we revoked thefarm’s Red Tractor certification andimmediately reported the incident tothe Animal and Plant Health Agency.”The farm keeps boars for semen production to supply the Hermitage AIfacility, which distributes to pig farmsthat produce pork and bacon for Britishsupermarkets. Hermitage told the Independent that it had cut ties with Innovisuntil it had received assurances over thetreatment of its animals.Innovis said it operates to the highesthealth and welfare standards, includingweekly visits from a specialist pig vetand inspections from the APHA everysix months.Farm sanctioned after pigs‘stabbed and struck’ dailyBy Daily Telegraph ReporterA RETIRED police dog on its first walksince recovering from major surgeryrescued a vulnerable man after locating him in woods.Bear, a German shepherd, was beingwalked by owner Julia Pope near Eastbourne, East Sussex, when it sensed thepresence of the man, who was the subject of a police search.The dog, which celebrated its 12thbirthday on the day of the incident,took Ms Pope to the missing person.Ms Pope said: “This was his firstproper walk since his surgery and wethought it would be a gentle wanderbut then it ended up being quite dramatic.“Bear went into work mode. He suddenly stopped and indicated towardsan area of dense underg row th –and then dragged us to it, where helocated and alerted us to a vulnerableman.“The man was confused, wet, coldand had fallen over, and was unable toget up unaided. I’m not sure he wouldhave survived the night as it wasextremely cold and almost dark at thatpoint with no one else in the vicinity.“We were able to assist the man backto an area where we could co-ordinatewith police officers who had beensearching for him and thankfully reunite him with his family, with Bearleading the way back.“It’s an amazing stroke of luck reallythat we were in the right place at theright time – two former police officersand a retired police dog.”Ms Pope adopted Bear in 2020 afterit retired but his keeper was unable tokeep it.The dog continues to work as a wellbeing and trauma support animal forpolice staff and is supported by theThin Blue Paw Foundation.The charity, which rehabilitates,protects and supports working andretired police dogs, supported Bear lastsummer when it lost the use of its rearlegs and was struggling to walk.It needed surgery to treat four herniated discs, thought to have been causedby its years of work.Bear then underwent surgery aftertwo benign tumours were spotted in ascan two weeks ago.Retired policedog out forwalkies rescuesmissing manBear, a retiredpolice dog, was ona walk soon afterrecovering fromsurgery, when it‘went into workmode’ and took itsowner to where amissing man hadfallen overBy Daniel SandersonScottish CorrespondentA SCANDAL-HIT LGBT charity whichworks in hundreds of schools in Scotland is being investigated by Scotland’scharity watchdog, it has emerged.The Office of the Scottish CharityRegulator said it had identified “regulatory issues” at LGBT Youth Scotlandafter examining “concerns about thecharity” raised over the past 12 months.It is understood OSCR has launchedan inquiry which has involved holdingprivate discussions with senior figuresat the controversial charity, which islargely funded by taxpayers’ cash.Its response to the Cass Review, thelandmark report into child gender services, at the centre of the probe. But bySunday night MrBiden had changedhis mind,apparentlychoosing familyloyalty overkeeping his word.What were thecharges BidenHunter faced?Both of the casesHunter, 54, wasdue to besentenced on thismonth relate to histime as a crackcocaine addict.In September, hepleaded guilty tonine federalcharges offalsifying recordsand failing to filetax returns duringthe heights of hisaddiction. In June,a jury found himguilty on threecounts of lying on afederal firearmsapplication. Hedeclared he wasnot on drugs whenhe purchased arevolver inWilmington,Delaware in 2018.He illegally ownedit for 11 days beforethen girlfriendHallie Biden, thewidow of his latebrother Beau,found the weaponand threw it in a bin.What sentencewas he facing?Hunter was due tobe sentenced onthe firearmscharges on Dec 13.The maximumsentence for sucha conviction is 25years, but he wasexpected to face ashorter sentence,of up to 16 months.Three days later hewas due to returnto court to hear hisfate in the taxcharges. He facedup to 17 years inprison, but expertssaid he was likelyto receive no morethan 36 months.How did thepresident justifyhis son’s pardon?Mr Biden said hehad long avoidedinterfering with thejudiciary, despitewhat he describedas a politicallymotivatedpersecution of hisson. Specifically,he said theRepublican-ledHouse ofRepresentativeshad pressured thecourts as itlaunched a seriesof investigationsinto his family.The president said:“A carefullynegotiated pleadeal unravelled inthe court room –with a number ofmy politicalopponents inCongress takingcredit for bringingpolitical pressureon the process.Had the plea dealheld, it would havebeen a fair,reasonableresolution ofHunter’s cases.”Under the terms ofthe deal, Hunterwould have beenspared jail in returnfor pleading guiltyto two minor taxo ences. Joe Biden accused of putting ‘family before country’ over son’s presidential pardonBy Tony DiverDEMOCRATS have accused Joe Bidenof putting “family before country” afterhe pardoned his son for federal tax andgun charges.The US president repeatedly said hewould not use his power to pardonHunter Biden in the fi nal months of histerm in office, but changed his mindover the Thanksgiving weekend.Members of his own party have sinceaccused him of laying the groundworkfor politically-motivated clemencyagreements by Donald Trump.Democrats have accused the president of undermining the rule of lawwith the most wide-ranging clemencyagreement since Gerald Ford pardonedRichard Nixon in the aftermath of theWatergate scandal in 1974.Biden’s pardon for son is widest since NixonDemocrats attack outgoingpresident as his about-turncould pave way for Trumpto pardon Jan 6 riotersThe Democrats claimed Trump was aself-serving liar... look who’s talkingJOE BIDEN’s decision to pardon hisson Hunter marks a new low for theDemocrats. As Adrian Vermeule hasnoted, dredging up the President’scriticism of the Supreme Court rulingon Donald Trump – “no one is abovethe law” misses the mark; this is not amatter of legal technicality butcartoonish nepotism that lays bare therank hypocrisy of his party.For months leading up to the 2020election, Democrats thundered fromthe pulpit about Trump’s venal,self-serving nature, how he was a liarunfi t to serve as president, and how heneeded to be replaced to protectAmerican democracy. In order toachieve this end, they proceeded to lieabout Hunter Biden’s laptop, lie aboutRussian collusion with Trump, andrepeatedly stated their doubts aboutany vaccine announced before pollingday in 2020. How lucky for them, then,that the announcement of Pfi zer’ssuccess was delayed because of achange in trial protocol after pressurewas poured on the company by publichealth off cials, with the new dataavailable just after the election.Once in off ce, the Democrats setabout making sure Trump wouldn’t winagain. A series of politically motivatedprosecutions were rolled out as theparty rediscovered the zeal forupholding the law that so curiously leftit during the Black Lives Matter riots.In 2020, suggesting crime was badwas so unfashionable Democraticstrategists were ostracised for merelysuggesting that heaping praise onrioters might be unpopular withvoters. In 2024, new and innovativelegal theories were deployed to makesure Trump and his allies felt the fullforce of their disapproval.Social media companies werepressured to censor Covid content theWhite House disliked, and traditionalmedia allies were drawn into colludingin the delusion that Biden was fi t togovern despite the evidence availableto anyone with eyes. And when after allthis they lost anyway, Biden made sureto pardon his son before he left off ce.Remind me again: who was the threatto democracy and standards in theOval Off ce here?The Democrats were so enraged byTrump that they assumed any meanswas legitimate as long as it led tokeeping him out of off ce. At the sametime, they sold themselves as the partyof moral superiority: the well-educatedpeople of conscience who just knewbetter than you what was right, andwhat was best for the country.It’s hard to think of a worsecombination. The belief that the partyhad moral permission to do whateverwas necessary to defeat its opponents– that they were the good guys becauseof who they were, no matter what theydid – led to its obvious conclusion. TheDemocrats richly deserve their yearsin the wilderness.CommentaryBy Sam Ashworth-HayesThiscartoonishnepotismlays barethe rankhypocrisyof his partyMedia alliescolluded inthe delusionthat Bidenwas fit togoverndespite theavailableevidenceto anyonewith eyesJared Polis, the Democratic governorof Colorado, said: “While as a father Icertainly understand [Mr Biden’s] natural desire to help his son by pardoninghim, I am disappointed that he put hisfamily ahead of the country.”He added: “This is a bad precedentthat could be abused by later presidentsand will sadly tarnish his reputation. This year’s seasonal display includes 83 Christmas trees, 9,810 feet of ribbon and more than 165,075 lights.ROBERTO SCHMIDT/AFP VIA GETTY IMAGESThe Daily Telegraph Tuesday 3 December 2024 *** 13By Our Foreign StaffUKRAINE has bought thousands ofuncrewed robotic ground vehicles nextyear to shuttle ammunition and supplies to infantry in the trenches andevacuate wounded soldiers.The buggy-like vehicles, an exampleof how technology is transformingtrench warfare in Ukraine, would sparetroops from operating in areas near thefront where Russian shelling anddrones are rife, Mykhailo Fedorov, thedeputy prime minister for innovation,said.Mr Fedorov, who has overseen droneprocurement for most of the war, toldReuters: “This year we purchased several thousand ground platforms, andnext year, I believe, we need tens ofthousands.”The vehicles, he said, are alreadybeing used along the front and in Russia’s Kursk region, where Kyiv’s troopscarved out an enclave in an Augustincursion.Ukraine has several training centresto teach their use, he added.Ukraine is slowly losing territoryto Russia in the east. The ruling Georgian Dream party has attempted to quell the unrest by claiming it is yet to decide on the issue.GIORGI ARJEVANIDZE/AFP VIA GETTY IMAGESWorld news14 *** Tuesday 3 December 2024 The Daily TelegraphWorld newsBy James Badcock in MadridTHE ruling Socialist party of Spainintends to ban transgender womenfrom competing in female sports and toremove the Q+ from the LGBTQ+acronym.The Socialists, led by Pedro Sánchez,the Spanish prime minister, includedplans to limit participation in femalesports to “people with a female biological sex” in a policy document decidedon at the party’s congress at the weekend. The decision also to remove Q+from a plan to protect sexual and genderminorities from the impact of social inequality sparked fury from LGBTQ+activists and politicians from Left-wingpartners of Mr Sánchez’s minority government. However, it drew supportfrom some feminist campaign groups.The amendments to the draft policyframework were introduced by a feminist faction within the Socialist partythat has fought against an ultra-liberalapproach to transgender rights underMr Sánchez’s administration.The passing of a transgender rightsreform in 2023, allowing anyone tochange their official sex simply by stating their wish to switch, caused a bitterrift within Spain’s ruling Left-wingforces.Carmen Calvo, the Socialist formerdeputy prime minister, said at the timethe reform would “destroy the powerfulbattery of equality legislation in ourcountry”.Activists espousing what they calltraditional feminism have flagged upabuses of the law, including newly registered transgender women attemptingto avoid convictions for violence committed against female partners.According to Spain’s transgender law,children as young as 12 can change thesex included on their state documents,although up to the age of 16 eitherparental or judicial support is required.For some feminists in the party, thereform threatened to damage the equality gains made by women.A spokesman for Ana Redondo, theSpanish equality minister, said the decisions were “a party matter” and therefore not binding. But the governmenthas been accused of “transphobia” bygender and sexual diversity campaigners.Sumar, the junior party in Spain’scoalition, said that “no one should beleft behind” when it comes to protecting rights, accusing the Socialist partyof adopting “far-Right ideas”.Irene Montero, the former equalityminister from the hard-Left Podemosparty, piloted the transgender reforminto law.“This is transphobia. Transgenderwomen are women, whether they havea penis or a vagina,” said Ms Montero,who is now a MEP for Podemos.She noted that Human Rights Watchhas said that sex tests carried out bysome federations or sports organisations are “degrading practices” basedon “arbitrary definitions of femininityand racial stereotypes”.She warned that “transphobia insport generates transphobic violence”in other areas of life.The feminist campaign group Contrael Borrado de Mujeres (Against the Erasure of Women) celebrated the Socialistcongress’s resolution but said it wasimportant to follow through with legislative action.“The Sports Act and the 14 regionallaws that allow the participation of menwho identify themselves as women inwomen’s sports competitions must nowbe amended,” the group said.Ms Redondo, a socialist who replacedMs Montero after the 2023 general election, has attempted to bridge the riftbetween feminists over the transgenderissue.Spain plansto ban transwomen fromfemale sportSocialist governmentaccused of transphobia asit also makes move toremove ‘Q’ from LGBTQ+Trolleyed in Thailand Two bikini-clad backpackers – a 23-year-old from Germany and a 19-year-old from Australia – arewheeled home by a caring policeman after overindulging in the bars of the party island Koh Phi Phi in Krabi, Thailand.ASIA PACIFIC PRESS VIA VIRALPRESSBy Joe BarnesTHE US Navy is trialling a new systemfor reloading warships at sea because ofthe increasing threat posed by China.While a vessel can fire dozens of missiles in minutes, it often takes twomonths to reload and make it war-readyagain. Those delays could prove fatal ina war with China because the fightingcould be thousands of miles from thenearest US naval base.“The ability to rearm at sea will becritical to any future conflict in thePacific,” Carlos Del Toro, the US Navysecretary, told The Wall Street Journal.Any war with China, probablysparked by an attempt by Beijing toinvade Taiwan, would be fought in theWestern Pacific. This would leave USwarships at risk of running out ofammunition potentially in days whilebeing up to 5,000 miles from their closest military base. Because of the concerns, naval engineers have adapted a30-year-old crane and fitted it to a warship to enable ship-to-ship missiletransfers from a container vessel.In a recent test of the prototype system, naval logisticians zip-wireddummy missile containers betweenUSNS Washington Chambers, a supplyship, and USS Chosin, a missile cruiser.The so-called transferable reloadat-sea method (Tram) could be includedon all warships being modernised if thetests continue to be successful.The US Navy only has about 11nuclear-powered aircraft carriers andnine smaller assault ships that are ableto sustain themselves at sea indefinitely.By upgrading older warships, thenavy believes it could increase thatnumber fivefold to about 100.While the system is being developed,US military chiefs are on the hunt formore allied bases that could be used toreduce the time it takes to re-arm vessels. One option under consideration isa port in Darwin, Australia, which isabout a four-and-a-half-day sail to theSouth China Sea, compared with threeweeks back to the US.USS Dewey was re-armed at the portfor the first time in September.The usual ports of Japan and Guamwould probably be targeted by Chinesemissile attacks in a war, promptinganother reason for US military chiefs tofind more friendly facilities.US Navy tests re-arming at sea to counter threat of ChinaThe Daily Telegraph Tuesday 3 December 2024 *** 15CommentSyria is Russia’ssouthern fi ank– and it iscrumbling fastIn a bizarre twist of fate, itis possible that theIslamist group HayatTahrir al-Sham (HTS) maybe inadvertently coming tothe aid of the West – andmore importantly, Ukraine– by disrupting Putin’ssouthern fi ank.Exactly eight years ago, inDecember 2016, Aleppoeventually fell to Assad andthe Russians when the Syrianregime rained down chlorinebarrel bombs for 17 days. Thisled to the surrender of300,000 civilians, and therebel ff ghters disappearedinto Idlib province. Many ofthe civilians were latertortured and murdered byAssad’s brutal regime.Since I last visitednorth-west Syria in 2020,things seem to have beenrelatively quiet there, andcertainly since 2022 all eyeshave been on Russia’s illegalinvasion of Ukraine.But it appears that thevarious rebel factions,especially the Free SyrianArmy and HTS, have beenregrouping, rearming andmost importantly training.These rebels we see today inAleppo are not the ragtagff ghters that I saw in Idlibprovince between 2013 and2020, but well-equippedoutff ts who seem to knowwhat they are doing. Thereare rumours that Turkey isbehind the resurgence of theSyrian opposition, butperhaps others have alsohelped.The Russian “playbook”used in Ukraine wasdeveloped in Syria, and Isaw at ff rst-hand how Assadand Putin attacked hospitals,destroyed the powerinfrastructure and razedmost of Idlib province to theground. This scorched earthpolicy was intended todestroy the population’s willto resist – and it worked inSyria, but not yet, thankGod, in Ukraine.This was also the purposeof the chemical weaponattacks, which Putin is alsousing to great effl ect inUkraine.In my opinion Assad isstill in power because heused chemical weapons, andthis has not gone unnoticedby Putin.Meanwhile, just as theBattle of Kursk was Hitler’sfatal over-extension in 1943,so Syria could be adangerous case of overreachfor Putin in 2024. Themodern tyrant similarlywants to rule Europe and itsenvirons. Syria is Putin’ssouthern fi ank and it has thestrategically important portof Tartus, which he mustff ght to save.But Iran is now a muchdiminished ally followingthe defeat of Hezbollah.There are few other Iranianmilitias who can gallop toAssad’s aid, and no doubtIsrael will also look foropportunities to furtherweaken the Damascusregime, which has beeninvolved in many attacks onIsrael.On the face of it, this ispotentially a diffi cult timefor the West, as HTS isproscribed as a terroristgroup by the UN and de factoby Washington andWestminster. But in thecloak-and-dagger world ofstrategic geopolitics,anything which hastens theend of hostilities in Ukraineis probably to be supported.HTS shunned Al Qaeda andIsis in 2016 and, unlike thosejihadists, it appears to beonly interested in oustingAssad from power. HTS doesnot seek to establish thecaliphate which undid Isis,nor a Taliban-typegovernment of the sort thatis ruining Afghanistan.According to my oldcontacts in Syria, thereappear to be moderategroups alongside HTS, mostespecially the Free SyriaArmy, who are trying tocreate a new “Arab Spring”for the civilians in Syria whohave suffl ered so much.This is not a perfectworld. But if the situation inSyria can hasten a ceaseff reon Zelensky’s terms inUkraine, and even perhapslead to a fairer Syria, then Iwill whisper what I expectmany Western governmentsare thinking: that this isprobably a positivedevelopment.FOLLOW SherelleJacobs on Twitter@Sherelle_E_JREAD MORE attelegraph.co.uk/opinionMany of us in this country feel it;the prickling sense that ourstreets are succumbing todisorder. My neighbourhood – on the“wrong side of the tracks” in NottingHill – is tragically wasting away like thecrackheads on its street corners.What was once a cheeky enclave inan area dullened by gentriff cation isnow deff nitely dystopian. It was thesound of Portobello that ff rst drew mein – the crackle of rock ’n’ roll LPs andtaffl eta gowns catching the wind on thevintage stalls. Now I wear earplugs todrown out the shrieks of drug addictshaving psychotic episodes. Shopliftersraid Tesco with an air of discerningnonchalance – even tutting at the stafflthat a favourite biscuit brand is out ofstock – before striding out with booty.Both of my neighbours have beenburgled. I don’t know whether I live in aWestern or Grand Theft Auto; punchups are common and cars no longerstop at zebra crossings. ElderlyJamaican men, priced out of the area, sitpossessively on porches smoking grassas they marinate in memories of the“raging” 1960s, ghosts from another era.Such anecdotes ought to keep SirKeir Starmer up at night. Whileimmigration and the NHS crisis arewidely believed to be the do-or-diechallenges that threaten to overwhelmLabour, crime could be the issue thatstrikes it from behind. Though Labourhas been largely preoccupied withother problems, it is increasingly rattledby the polls; these show that thenumber of people who consider crimeto be the most pressing issue hasdoubled since 2016. Starmer has thusput cutting crime at the forefront of his“plan for change” this week. This isexpected to feature various stickingplasters, potentially including tags forknife offl enders and more communitysupport offi cers.Such gestures are unlikely to alterthe sense that Britain has become alawless land, where decent people arecondemned to live in constant anxietyover crime. Nor are they likely to allaymass dismay that petty criminality hasbecome a risk-free career choice, withoutlaws brazenly taking for free whatothers work hard to honestly earn.London has something of 1980s NewYork about it. “New York New York, sobad they named it twice”, the criminalsused to sing. London outlaws today rap“Steal it, Sell it, Sorted” – in mockery ofthe slogan announced regularly on theTube. Like the Big Apple 40 years ago,several of our capital’s boroughs are onthe brink of bankruptcy. Meanwhile,chunks of Red Wall territory, ravagedby County Lines gang warfare amidcollapsing police services, haveessentially been seized by organisedcrime. Having walked the streets of bothCleveland, Ohio and Cleveland, Englandat night, the former to me felt safer.True, concerns about crime arepartly psychological. Overall, Britainhas never been safer. Most crime hasplummeted by 90 per cent over the past30 years. London’s homicide rate hashalved over the last two decades. At thesame time, there are cracks in thispicture – and they are deepening.Shoplifting mushroomed by 30 per centin the year to June 2024, and burglariesinvolving knives have spiked. Withpolicing budgets partially reliant oncouncil tax, crime is surging indeprived Red Wall towns.Labour does not have a plan to dealwith this. Low-level crime is noweffl ectively decriminalised: in half ofneighbourhoods, no burglaries havebeen solved over the past three years.Cannabis is legalised in all but name aspolice warn that dealing with the drugis “just not a priority”.Labour MPs sigh that, likeimmigration, crime is an “intractable”problem. They lament that the Toryausterity hollowed the police force, andthere isn’t the money to reverse thedamage in this stagnant economy. Theybemoan that incarceration is a futileritual, with an underclass trapped in aloop of reoffl ending. The trouble withthe liberal Left is that they mistakecrunch points for vicious cycles. Intruth, Starmer could smash crime if heonly made the tough calls.There is much that Britain can learnfrom New York’s triumph over violentcrime in the 1990s. The formula wassimple and controversial. A beefed uppolice force arrested 20 per cent morepeople, and threw almost 25 per centmore people into jail. Though the citywas broke, it raised the cash to do thisby privatising infrastructure and takinghard decisions on public spending. Thethen mayor Rudy Giuliani wasdemonised for refusing to pour moneyinto crumbling schools and slashingbudgets for things like youth centres,which liberals incorrectly assumedwould undermine hardline effl orts.Experts are divided about NYC’sfamed “broken window” approach.which saw police cracking down onminor nuisances. But it made peoplefeel better. It also had symbolic power,preventing street corners wheretroublemakers congregated to playloud music from becoming moredangerous into the night. And bycutting taxes on everything from hotelsto clothes, it encouraged a boom thatmotivated people on the margins tohold down jobs.It is doubtful that Labour could everpursue such a model, which violates itsdeepest beliefs. Cracking down onminor crimes would require it to renegeon its commitment to “racially sensitive”policing in multi-ethnic areas. Buildingmore prisons is unthinkable to amovement that has swallowed wholethe received wisdom that “incarcerationdoesn’t work” – a view that confi atesprison’s poor record as a rehabilitatinginstitution with its role in deterringpeople from crime in the ff rst instance.Labour will pay the price for itsparalysis. Crime risks driving more RedWall voters away from the party. Fear atthe endemic disorder on the streets andoutrage at the chronic disorder on theborder are also starting to feed offl eachother in voters’ minds. It is also nolonger unthinkable that a crime paniccould trigger a collapse in its youngsupport. One emerging characteristic ofGeneration Z is its reverence for order.They are exchanging viral recordings ofLondon phone-snatchings and BondStreet shoppers shoving their LouisVuitton purchases into Primark bags.The Shadow Cabinet are starting toget tentatively excited about thelong-term opportunity this presents forthe Right; it is dawning on Tories that avow to build more slammers – not justmore houses – might help win overyoung voters.Crime, they say is the dog that hasn’tbarked. A more appropriate metaphormight be that it is the great white sharkthat hasn’t surfaced. Labour is driftinginto a political mauling that could provenot just brutal but fatal.Britain is facing a crime tsunamithat could sweep Starmer awayOrder can be restored, as1990s New York proved –but this Government doesnot have the will for itPettycriminalityhas becomea risk-freecareerchoice, withoutlawsbrazenlytaking forfree whatothers workhard tohonestlyearnSHERELLE JACOBSHAMISH DE BRETTON-GORDONFOLLOW MichaelDeacon on Twitter@MichaelPDeaconREAD MORE attelegraph.co.uk/opinionWay of the World Michael DeaconAs Parliament debated the meritsof enabling sociopaths to coercethe vulnerable into a prematuredeath, a peculiar little row broke out.The Tory MP Danny Kruger – whoopposes the Terminally Ill Adults(End of Life) Bill – had just uttered thewords “assisted suicide”. Horriff ed, theLabour MP Cat Eccles – who supportsthe bill – leapt up to complain.“The honourable gentleman is usingincorrect language,” she protestedindignantly to the Speaker. “It is notsuicide. That is offl ensive. I ask himplease to correct his language.”This was a curious intervention.Because, even if one supports the Bill,one should at least be able tosee that “assisted suicide” isan accurate description ofwhat is being proposed.A patient, having assertedthat he or she wishes toend his or her own life,will deliberately ingesta substance that willcause his or her death.That, incontestably, issuicide. So it can hardlybe “incorrect” – let alone“offl ensive” – to refer to itas such.Indeed, if any termis “incorrect”, it’s“assisted dying”– because it’sa euphemism, chosen by campaignersto make what they want sound morepalatable. Much like the euphemismsGeorge Orwell noted in his 1946 essay,Politics and the English Language.Politicians, wrote Orwell, would say“paciff cation”, when what they reallymeant was “defenceless villages…bombarded from the air, theinhabitants driven out into thecountryside, the cattle machinegunned”. Similarly, they would say“rectiff cation of frontiers”, rather than“millions of peasants… robbed of theirfarms”. These political euphemismsweren’t used merely for the sake ofbrevity. “Such phraseology,” Orwellexplained, “is needed if one wants toname things without calling up mentalpictures of them.”For the same reason, in the currentdebate, supporters of the bill prefer“assisted dying” to “assisted suicide”.Why, however, should they growso furiously indignant when theiropponents use the latter term instead?I think it’s all about ego.As has been obvious from the outset,the Bill’s most fanatical advocatessee themselves as secular saints.They are morally unimpeachable,their motivespure, and theirplans thereforebeyond reproach.Their thoughtprocess, in essence, seems to be: “I amgood, kind and virtuous. So this policythat I support is automatically good,kind and virtuous, too.”That shocking word “suicide”,however, threatens to shatter thislovingly tended illusion. For a tiny,terrifying moment, it forces thesepeople to consider that what theyare advocating is not, as they like totell themselves, noble, compassionateand without risk. It is dark, dystopianand dangerous. But, rather than dwellon this awful thought, they angrilydemand that those giving voice to itbe silenced.In effl ect, then, the euphemism“assisted dying” isn’t designed merelyto fool the public. It’s designed to helpits advocates fool themselves.Since Donald Trump’s victory,some on the Right seem tohave convinced themselvesthat, at least in the US, wokery is over.Clearly such people don’t read TheNew York Times. The leading journalof progressive America isn’t giving upwithout a ff ght. We know, because theother day it published an article thatcontained the following new phrase:“Non-transgender women.”Until not so very long ago, of course,we had a shorter, simpler way ofreferring to such people. It was“women”. But evidently The NewYork Times has decided that that wordis horribly outdated and reactionary.Henceforth, it seems, there are tobe two categories of people whoaren’t men: transgender women andnon-transgender women. Meaningthat, in effl ect, the default type ofwoman is now male. Meanwhile,women who happen to be female arenow a mere subset of their own sex.Whether these new classiff cationswill catch on outside the offi ce of TheNew York Times, I don’t know. On thewhole, though, I tend to hope that theywon’t.
