Because I’m not sure The NewYork Times has thought this through.Imagine, for example, that you’re amember of the crew on a sinking ship.When rushing to ready the lifeboats,you daren’t shout “Women andchildren ff rst!”, for fear of soundingbigoted and exclusionary.To be truly inclusive, therefore,you’ll shout: “Transgender women,non-transgender women, non-binarywomen, gender-questioning women,gender-fi uid women, bigenderwomen, trigender women,demigender women, pangenderwomen, omnigender women,agender women and children ff rst!”On the one hand, that should helpyou avoid the wrath of The New YorkTimes. On the other, by the time you’veff nally ff nished reciting the above list,you’ll have drowned.one should at least be able tosee that “assisted suicide” isan accurate description ofwhat is being proposed.A patient, having assertedthat he or she wishes toend his or her own life,will deliberately ingesta substance that willcause his or her death.That, incontestably, issuicide. So it can hardlybe “incorrect” – let alone“offl ensive” – to refer to itIndeed, if any termis “incorrect”, it’s“assisted dying”explained, “is needed if one wants toname things without calling up mentalpictures of them.”For the same reason, in the currentdebate, supporters of the bill prefer“assisted dying” to “assisted suicide”.Why, however, should they growso furiously indignant when theiropponents use the latter term instead?I think it’s all about ego.As has been obvious from the outset,the Bill’s most fanatical advocatessee themselves as secular saints.They are morally unimpeachable,their motivespure, and theirplans thereforebeyond reproach.Their thoughtTo order prints or signed copies of any Telegraph cartoon, go to telegraph.co.uk/prints-cartoons or call 0191 603 0178  [email protected]16 *** Tuesday 3 December 2024 The Daily TelegraphLetters to the EditorESTABLISHED 1855We accept lettersby post and email.Please includename, address,work and hometelephonenumbers.111 BuckinghamPalace Road,LondonSW1W 0DTEMAILdtletters@telegraph.co.ukNEWSLETTERSign up to receiveLetters to theEditor everyFriday attelegraph.co.uk/lettersFOLLOWTelegraph Letters@LettersDeskSince Labour took ofi ce on July 5, pledging tocontrol the borders, more than 20,000would-be asylum seekers have crossed theChannel. Far from the trafi c stopping, the pace ofarrivals has accelerated. The numbers arriving arelikely to drop away in the coming weeks as wintertakes a hold but the trend remains upwards.The criticism aimed by Labour at the lastgovernment for its failure to “stop the boats” isbeing turned against the new administration. Thiswas never an easy matter to deal with but Labourmanifestly failed to help the Tories do so, thwartingevery eff ort to introduce disincentives in thesystem, notably the Rwanda scheme. Anotherpromise to end the use of hotels to house migrantshas also been jettisoned, with more rooms thanever now being occupied.Yvette Cooper, the Home Secretary, is irritatinglyholier-than-thou on this issue, continuing todenounce her predecessors without fullyacknowledging how hard this task is. She hasrecently concluded an agreement with Iraq toreturn migrants, share intelligence on criminalgroups and bolster its border security, calling it an“important new partnership” with the country.When the Tories did the same with Albania shesaid it was being used predominantly to returnhistoric cases. Might that also be true of heragreement with Baghdad or will Iraqis arriving byboat be removed immediately?Since they will all be treated on a “case-by-casebasis”, and proving where they come from isdifi cult because they throw away their ID, it is hardto see how the deal will make much diff erence. Theagreement will cost the UK taxpayer a further£800,000, admittedly a small sum in the greatscheme of things but one suggesting this is not anentirely serious initiative.Ms Cooper told MPs that returns of asylumseekers will now rise signiffl cantly and will act as adeterrent to future migrants. We shall see. But withannual asylum costs now more than £5 billion,success in this area is vital.Indeed, it is about to become a more pressingissue with the renewed turmoil in Syria. Therevival of the civil war there is bound to trigger afresh refugee crisis with people leaving to avoid theffl ghting and heading for Iraq, Turkey and otherroutes used by people trafi ckers. Failure to controlimmigration, both legal and illegal, was a key factorin the defeat of the Tories. It is now becoming amajor test for Labour.Migration is now amajor test for LabourJoe Biden has had more than his fair share oftragedy in his personal life, losing his ffl rst wifeand baby daughter in a car accident and thenwatching his son Beau die of cancer. It is hardlysurprising that his parental instinct kicked in at theend of his presidency to issue a “full andunconditional” pardon to his son Hunter, who wasfacing jail after being convicted of ffl rearms and taxoff ences. In a statement, he said: “I hope Americanswill understand why a father and a president wouldcome to this decision.”Doubtless many do understand: family comesffl rst. Mr Biden’s difi culty, however, is that heemphatically said he would not do this and hasbeen critical of Donald Trump’s use of theexecutive pardon during his ffl rst term in ofi ce. Theoutgoing president is guilty of breathtakinghypocrisy, further undermining the Americanpublic’s already jaundiced view of politicians andtheir ethics.President Biden says his son was the victim of apoliticised justice system that treated him moreharshly because of who he was. This is preciselythe point Mr Trump has made about the successionof cases brought against him, that they wereessentially an aspect of “lawfare” – the use of thelegal system to pursue political opponents.It could be argued that the accusations againstMr Trump are diff erent in nature but the point isthe same. Moreover, he was prosecuted for keepingsensitive documents from his time in ofi ce at homewhereas Mr Biden, who did the same, was not. Thisbias has caused great damage to the US legalsystem. One way to resolve it now would be for MrBiden to pardon Mr Trump and for lawfare againstpolitical opponents then to end once and for all.Pardoning presidentsThere may be no such thing as a free lunch, butthere is such a thing as a free royal portrait. Itturns out, though, that most of our schools,churches, universities and hospitals would rathernot have one. To celebrate the start of King Charles’sreign, the last government arranged – at a cost ofroughly £131.81 per picture including delivery (and atotal of £2,710,705) – for a framed photo of our newmonarch wearing the uniform of an Admiral of theFleet to be off ered gratis to all public institutions.Only around one third of schools, a quarter ofAnglican churches, an eighth of our universities anda mere three per cent of hospitals accepted the gift.Even among Lord Lieutenancies, the King’s ofi cialrepresentatives, take up was not universal. Onebranch of our nation’s public service has remainedunabashedly loyal; all coastguard centres said yes.Declining portraitsSIR – Sir Keir Starmer blames Brexitand the Tories for the huge increase innet migration (report, December 2). Heshould take a closer look at his owncontribution to the problem.He objected to everything that theprevious government attempted to doin order to reduce the huge in ow, andhis rhetoric served only to encourageall and sundry to come to our shores.It will be interesting to see howthese numbers look in 12 months’ time.It all rests on his shoulders now.John HinchslifiLongridge, LancashireSIR – The Prime Minister has criticised“the open border experiment” of massmigration, and accepted that it hasdone us harm and needs to change(report, December 2). On top of this,the Ofi ce for Budget Responsibilityhas accepted that low-skilled migrantsare an economic cost to the Treasury.So, will ordinary people, whopointed these things out a long timeago, now receive an apology? I think inany business or organisation, it’s agood idea for the boss to understandthe shop  oor.James CrudgingtonNottinghamSIR – Kemi Badenoch’s admission thatthe Tories “got it wrong” onimmigration must rank as theunderstatement of the year (report,November 28). What compounds thiserror is that the Conservatives weretold year after year that they had got itwrong, but simply refused to listen.They knew better than everyone else.This is a mistake that Labour isrepeating with relish – hopefully withthe same electoral consequences.David MillerChigwell, EssexSIR – One reason why many Toryvoters defected to Reform UK, andsome even to Labour, at the lastelection is that successive Torygovernments seemed to forget whotheir friends were. They tried to be allthings to all people, and ignored theircore of middle-England supporters.What you can say about the Labourincumbents is that they know verywell who their friends are – givingabove-in ation pay rises to most of thepublic sector, while chargingemployers many millions of pounds inNational Insurance and in ictingminimum wage rises, imposing VATon school fees and attacking farmersthrough inheritance tax.I’m hopeful that, with KemiBadenoch at the helm of theOpposition, this will be a one-termgovernment – but the Tories need tolearn a valuable lesson if they are toform the next.Mark BrownLutterworth, LeicestershireSIR – I assume Jaguar’s brilliantcreative team has been appointed tohandle the Government’s strategyrelaunch (report, December 2).Alan FieldScarrington, NottinghamshireThe PM can no longer avoid responsibility for soaring immigrationSIR – By pardoning his son, after hevowed not to interfere with the legalprocess, Joe Biden has addedhypocrisy to the list of his failures(report, telegraph.co.uk, December 2).Most grievous was his clinging toofi ce though he was clearly incapable,thereby forcing the Democrats to putforward an unelectable, last-minutecandidate in Kamala Harris.Now he has done his Republicansuccessor another favour, opening thedoor to the avalanche of presidentialpardons that Donald Trump has up hissleeve – not least, of himself.The American political system looksrotten to its core.John HicksManchesterChagos delusionSIR – The plan to hand over the ChagosIslands (Letters, December 2) to aforeign power that has no legitimateclaim and cares little about the localpopulation is a geostrategic blunder.We should stop now. If we think thisvirtue-signalling will make us popularwith those who would do us harm andmake the world safer, we are deludingourselves.Admiral Lord West of Spithead (Lab)London SW1Bungalow ordealSIR – I, too, have recent experiencewith residential development (Letters,November 29), but it was not theplanning department to blame for theordeal. The Forestry Commissionserved us with a restocking notice,with the sole object of preventingimplementation of a detailed planningconsent for nine bungalows.Work had already begun, but wasforced to stop, and only after fouryears did we manage to defeat itthrough a High Court judicial review,with an award of costs against thepublic purse. Madness.Angus ThornesWestclifi -on-Sea, EssexVarieties of blissSIR – Roger Collings (Letters,December 2) declares that marital blissis accomplished by “both being untidy,going to bed at the same time andhaving separate bathrooms”.My husband and I have entered our56th year of marital bliss with himbeing tidy (I’m not), going to bed atdiff ering hours, sharing a bathroom,and having separate bedrooms.Margaret ReedTrowbridge, WiltshireSIR – When my husband of 52 yearsand I have an occasional argument, itonly needs one of us to say, “Well youinvaded Poland!” and the tension isdissipated and everything put intoperspective.Janet NewisSidcup, KentSIR – I remember reading the followingadvice: there’s the man you want tomarry, the man you should marry andthe man you do marry.Thirty years on, I still congratulatemyself on scoring a hat-trick.Diana WalshStoke Gabriel, DevonSIR – To keep my husband on his toes, Iintroduce him to people as my ffl rsthusband. We’re 65 years married nextyear, and so far it has worked.Jackie FuellLondon SE25SIR – My wife of 58 years says the secretof a happy marriage is not to expect toomuch.Jonathan WardLong Melford, Sufi olkBiden’s pardonSIR – I was 17 when I fell in love withLondon’s East End and Smithffl eldMarket (Letters, December 2).It was March 1958, and I was on myway to St Bartholomew’s Hospital foran interview with the matron, MissLoveridge. I remember feeling quitegrown up as I walked under thearchway, and was instantly bewilderedby a place teeming with life.I was fascinated by cheerybummarees, in their caps and smocks,rushing around carrying dead animalson their broad shoulders to thedistribution lorries. I watched abutcher as he worked with speed anddexterity, with a touch of theatrethrown in, and wondered if all thesurgeons at St Bartholomew’s showedsuch pride as they wielded theirscalpels.The butcher seemed to know whereI was going (prospective nursingstudents were always interviewed on aThursday), and as I went on my way hewished me luck.During my training I frequently hadcause to walk through the market inmy uniform, and I remember withgreat fondness the wolf whistles, thecheek and the encouraging repartee Ireceived. What fun we had.Greta BarnesBanbury, OxfordshireSIR – Nicholas Anderson’s anecdoteabout a  ying pig’s head (Letters,December 2) reminded me of a patientI treated while training at StBartholomew’s. He was a meat porterand had suff ered a perforated eardrum, having been hit on the side ofhis head by half a pig carcass.Graham Smelt FRCSHuddersff eld, West YorkshireWine circuitSIR – While serving in NorthernIreland, a custom arose that, when onebrought a bottle of wine to a friend’shouse (Letters, November 30), youwould sign the label.The bottle would then continue itstour of the married quarters’ patch,being signed by each additionalrecipient, until it fetched up with aprevious signatory. It was then to bedrunk.I think it was the circulating bottleof Blue Nun that I was most afraid of.Lt Col Lyndon Robinson (retd)Mursley, BuckinghamshireSIR – The only thing worse than beingbrought a bottle of olive oil rather thanwine by a dinner guest (Comment,November 29) would be to receive ascented candle.Roger SouthCapel, SurreyStartling Smithfi eldSIR – It was interesting to read of thedecline in popularity of mistletoe(report, November 26).Growing up on a farm inWorcestershire in the 1950s and1960s, we always left a bunchhanging in the hall for 12 monthsuntil it was replaced the nextChristmas.This was traditional inWorcestershire and Herefordshire, Iseem to recall.Kate ForresterMalvern, WorcestershireTraditional hanging of mistletoe at ChristmasSIR – It is to be hoped that, as theTerminally Ill Adults (End of Life) Billproceeds (“Patients may have to payfor assisted dying”, report, December2), MPs will subject its supporters’claims to the closest scrutiny,including the following.First, Dame Esther Rantzen claimedthe Bill “off ers everyone equal choice”.However, while the Bill grants a choiceto someone predicted to die within sixmonths, even if they are not suff ering,it denies one to those who are suff eringgravely from an illness that will last sixdecades.If the Bill is enacted, such obviouslyarbitrary discrimination will bechallenged in the courts. The slipperyslope is logically inherent in the veryarguments of “choice” and “relief ofsuff ering”.Second, Alicia Kearns MP objectedto talk of “assisted suicide”, suggesting“it comes from a very religious place”.It comes from a very secular place: theSuicide Act 1961, which deffl nes thecrime of assisting suicide. It is, rather,the tendentious and misleadingeuphemism “assisted dying” that isobjectionable, con ating euthanasia,physician-assisted suicide, palliativemedicine and the withdrawal oftreatment. To their credit, the Dutchtalk plainly of euthanasia andphysician-assisted suicide.Third, Kim Leadbeater MP claimedthat no jurisdiction that started outwith “terminal illness” has expandedits law. Not so. Colombia’s law is nolonger limited to the “terminalpatient”, and Canada no longerrequires death to be “reasonablyforeseeable”.The former governor of WashingtonState (who had Parkinson’s) admittedthat he campaigned for an Oregontype law “as a ffl rst step” in the hopethat other states would follow, “thenation’s resistance will subside, theculture will shift and laws with morelatitude will be passed”.Finally, Ms Leadbeater claimed thather Bill has the strongest safeguardsin the world. Yet MPs should bereminded that Sir James Munby, thedistinguished former president of theFamily Division of the High Court,concluded that, in respect of theproposed involvement of the judiciary,“the Leadbeater Bill falls lamentablyshort of providing adequatesafeguards”.Professor John KeownKennedy Institute of EthicsGeorgetown UniversityWashington DC, United StatesMPs’ duty to challenge assisted dying claimsA commemorative postage stamp for Christmas 1980, designed by Jerey MatthewsALAMYT he Chancellorship of theExchequer and the post ofDefence Secretary are the lastCabinet jobs where a woman mustconfront a possible credibility gap. Ourculture still subliminally links ffl nanceand war more with men than withwomen.Rachel Reeves is very conscious –and rightly proud – of being the ffl rstfemale Chancellor. Interviewedshortly before she took ofi ce, shesaid: “I always feel like I need to provemyself.” This feeling may beaggravated by the fact that she is notonly a woman, but quite a young one,with two youngish children. A55-year-old female Chancellor wouldhave an easier time of it. Perhaps LordAlli should have given her hot andcold running nannies rather thanclothes.I wonder if this need to prove herselfdrives Ms Reeves to adoptunsustainable positions. In the oldcaricature, women are changeable andweak. Perhaps she thinks she should beextra ffl rm. I can think of no otherexplanation for her remarks at the CBIlast week. She said, “I am not comingback [to Parliament] with moreborrowing or more taxes.”It is surprising that these wordshave not attracted more notice,particularly as the Prime Ministerhimself almost directly contradictedthem two days later. No Chancellorsetting out in the job can make such apromise. It is not in his or her powerto fulffl l. If, for example, the bondmarkets become reluctant to buyBritish government debt, the cost ofpublic borrowing to pay theconsequently higher interest couldwell force the Chancellor to raisetaxes or cut spending, or both. MsReeves must know this, so why didshe say what she said? If she sticks toher promise and refuses to askParliament for more, Sir Keir might beforced to ffl nd a diff erent Chancellor.Similar anxiety may lie behind MsReeves’s desire to establish herself as aneconomist. There is absolutely no needfor the Chancellor to be an economist.Indeed, very few have been, butinsecurity may have encouraged her totalk up her time at the Bank of Englandand HBOS.I even wonder whether the fact thather husband is a senior ofi cial in theDepartment of Food and Rural Aff airs(Defra) may drive her to be tougher onfarmers than she need, so that no onecan accuse her of pillow talk.If you want to understand thediff erence between Sir KeirStarmer and Sir Tony Blair as Labourmodernisers, just consider thefollowing passage from AlastairCampbell’s diaries at the turn of 1994-5– the start, therefore, of Blair’s secondyear as Labour leader.David Blunkett, the shadoweducation secretary, had justunilaterally briefed a newspaper thatLabour planned to impose VAT onindependent school fees. Blair went“berserk”, recorded Campbell, who hadto deal with this on a delayed NewYear’s Eve train journey, whispering onthe phone in a crowded carriage.“‘Are you telling me,’ said Tony, ‘thatone of our own people has done aninterview and … we are going into theNew Year with a story about Labourtaxing people to educate theirchildren?’ I said I am, and theexasperated silence spoke volumes.”Blair asked Campbell whetherLabour “would ever get serious”.“Do we care about what a fewactivists think, or do we care aboutwhat millions of people think?”The next day Blair made another aidering the BBC explicitly to disownBlunkett’s story. Blair told Campbell:“Until we face up to the scale of changeneeded … we might as well not bother.We’re ffl nished.”Sir Keir fought and won the lastelection on the strength of the word“change”; but now we wonder if heknew what it meant. On school fees andmuch else, the “few activists” aregetting their way and the “millions ofpeople” are ignored.I gather Sir Tony has strong views onthis matter. I hope he will soon sharethem with the public.Of the new Jaguar ad, I mustspeak cautiously. Although I drivea car, I know nothing about them andhave never, since the early 1980s,bought one with my own money. Tome, they are necessary, but boringthings. I just want them to work. Thisapproach isolates me from my fellowmales, who are frequently obsessedwith wheels.So the fact that the new ad leaves mecold tells you nothing about whether itworks for its intended market.But a historical comparison mayhelp. In 1997, British Airways removedthe Union  ag from their livery andreplaced it with a range of so-calledethnic tailffl ns. In the same year,Margaret Thatcher was ffl lmed touringthe trade stands at her party’sconference. She spotted the newdesign on a scale-model of a BA plane.Exclaiming, “Absolutely terrible!”, shedrew a tissue out of her famed handbagand wrapped it round the off endingtail ffl n. The ffl lm went viral. After fouryears of ensuing aggro from the publicand its staff , BA gave in and restoredthe national carrier’s national  ag.The Jaguar ad contained nothingabout the car, a decision which, givenwhat was prematurely revealed beforethe launch last night, was just as well.Sadly, Lady Thatcher is no longerwith us to cover the pink thing inmountains of tissue paper. Nor is herhusband, Denis, who sometimes drovea Jaguar and was the epitome of thecar’s traditional “gin and Jag” vibe. As Isay, I know nothing about cars, but Isense that this new entrant ismotoring’s equivalent of KamalaHarris – vapid, unpatriotic and whatDonald Trump would immediatelybrand “a loser”.Has Rachel Reeves signed her own death warrant?CHARLES MOORENOTEBOOKThe Daily Telegraph Tuesday 3 December 2024 *** 17Puzzles, Mind Games & Telegraph Toughie18 *** Tuesday 3 December 2024 The Daily Telegraph8312.89+25.59 (+0.31pc)£$Rate1.2629Change-0.82¢£€Rate1.2062Change+0.27¢FTSE 250 20769.05 -2.52 (-0.01pc)FTSE All Share 4536.91 +12.03 (+0.27pc)FTSE All Share Yield 3.54 -0.01FTSE Eurotop 100 4013.02 +31.49 (+0.79pc)Nikkei 225 38513.02 +304.99 (+0.80pc)EURO STOXX 50 4846.73 +42.33 (+0.88pc)S&P 500 6046.39 +14.01 (+0.23pc)Nasdaq 19404.97 +186.80 (+0.97pc)52WkHigh 8474.4152WkLow 7387.09Yield 3.57 -0.01P/E Ratio 13.85 +0.0552WkHigh 45071.2952WkLow 35592.22CURRENCIESFTSE 100 DOW JONES44755.97-154.68 (-0.34pc)COMMODITIESBIGGESTRISERHowden Joinery838½p+22½ (+2.76pc)BIGGESTFALLERVistry630½p-25½ (-3.89pc)GOLD At 18.00$2640.75(£2091)-21.11 (-0.79pc)BRENT CRUDE At 18.00$71.71(February)-1.36 (-1.86pc)MARKETSpcAt 18.00Previous close10am 12pm 2pm 4pm8340832083008280826010am 12pm 2pm 4pm450004510044900448004470044600Previous closeBorrowing bingeHow France’s spirallingdeficit could bringdown EmmanuelMacron’s governmentShare of the spoilsWhy stock marketinvestors think the USelection dealt them aTrump cardBen WrightPage 20Page 24 Page 24 Page 23OnlineSign up for our free dailyBusiness Briefi ngtelegraph.co.uk/business-briefi ngBy Ben Marlowand James WarringtonTHE Post Office’s embattled boss isexpected to lose out on a bonus packageworth more than £750,000 afterabruptly stepping down from his post inthe wake of the Horizon scandal.Nick Read, who took over in 2019, isunderstood to have forfeited payoutstotalling as much as £767,000 as a resultof his decision to step down in September. It caps a torrid tenure for the chiefexecutive, whose time in charge hasbeen overshadowed on more than oneoccasion by disputes over pay.Mr Read, who has held senior roles atTesco, HBOS and Thomas Cook, wasentitled to a short-term bonus of up to45pc of his basic salary, and a furtherlong-term bonus of up to 43pc, for themost recent fi nancial year.With Mr Read earning basic pay of£435,750 in 2024, he would have beenin line for a maximum payout totalling£383,460 for the year ending March2024, the Post Office’s most recentannual report shows.In addition, it is understood that the58-year-old stood to pick up roughly thesame amount in fi nancial rewards againfor the coming year, taking the maximum possible bonuses over the twoyears to £767,000. However, by handing in his resignation he has forfeitedthose too, the Post Off ce said. The fulldetails of the executive bonus plan forthat 12-month period will be revealed inthe forthcoming 2024 annual report,due to be published later this month.A spokesman for the Post Off ce saidthe loss of earnings was partly voluntary on Mr Read’s part, and partly afunction of the rules on pay.“Nick Read has voluntarily waivedhis rights to both the short-term andlong-term awards available to him forthe year ended March 31 2024. WhenNick resigned on Sept 17 2024, he knowingly forfeited any bonus for the fi nancial year to March 31 2025. As detailed inhis resignation statement, he remains afull-time employee of the companyuntil March 2025.”During the judge-led inquiry into theHorizon IT scandal that led to thewrongful conviction of hundreds ofPost Office sub-postmasters, Mr Readfaced accusations of repeated attemptsto secure generous pay rises, at a timewhen many of those workers were battling long delays to their right to fi nancial compensation.He took over as chief executive in2019 with a pledge to “right the wrongsof the past”. Earlier in the year, itemerged that former Post Off ce chairman Henry Staunton had twice askedthe Government for Mr Read’s pay to bedoubled. Mr Staunton later told theinquiry that Mr Read had threatened toleave over the issue.Jane Davies, a former Post Officehuman resources director, had previously testifi ed to a select committee ofMPs that Mr Read had become“obsessed” with his pay. Mr Read admitted his efforts to secure such a riselooked “very poor” given so manybranch owner-operators were stillawaiting payouts over the Horizon ITfailings. However, Mr Read denied everthreatening to resign over remuneration. Former postal minister Kevin Hollinrake later told the inquiry that he feltit was “right to give him [Mr Read] adecent chance to see through reform ofthe Post Off ce’s culture”.However, he said he felt that Mr Read“was being paid lots whilst not doing avery good job”, while pointing out thatit was his understanding that therewere 143 people earning more than£100,000 working at the Post Off ce.Mr Hollinrake, who held ministerialresponsibility for the Post Off ce fromOctober 2022 until the general electionon July 4 2024, also said he had come tothe conclusion that Mr Staunton was“incapable of chairing the organisation”. In 2023, Mr Read agreed to returna £54,000 portion of his bonus for thatyear that was tied to his participation inthe Horizon computer scandal inquiry.The climbdown followed rebukesfrom inquiry chairman Sir Wyn Williams and Darren Jones, then chairmanof the business and trade select committee. The Post Off ce and Mr Read laterapologised.The Post Office announced in September he would be leaving in Marchnext year. He had already temporarilystepped back in July in order to dedicate his “entire attention” to preparingto face the inquiry in October.Mr Staunton has been succeeded byformer Camelot boss Nigel Railton asinterim chairman.Post Ofice scraps bonus for outgoing chiefNick Read expected tolose out on package worthmore than £750,000after stepping downBy James WarringtonTHE Guardian is offl ering toconceal the identity ofjournalists who cross thepicket line as the Left-wingnewspaper braces for its fi rstsignificant strike in morethan four decades.Bosses have told reportersthat if they fear colleagues’reaction they do not need toput their names on their articles if they choose to go intowork during this week’swalkout over plans to sellThe Observer to Tortoise, aloss-making start-up.Any stories written bystrike-breakers can insteadrun under a generic “Guardian staffl ” byline, employeeshave been told. The Guardianhas a hybrid working policy,allowing journalists to workfrom home part-time, sothose who choose to workmay not have to walk past aphysical picket line. Editorsare also expecting to rely onpre-written articles to helpplug holes in the newspaper’s output.The Guardian is scrambling to limit the damage ashundreds of journalists prepare to strike for 48 hoursfrom tomorrow.A spokesman for GuardianMedia Group said: “While werespect the right to strike, wedo not believe a strike is thebest course of action in thiscase and our talks with theNUJ continue.”Guardian offers writers anonymityBy Szu Ping ChanANGELA RAYNER’S employmentrights overhaul will hit Britain’s army ofagency workers and inffi ict permanentdamage on the economy, the country’sbiggest recruiters have warned.Bosses of more than a dozen companies have called on the Government toreconsider sweeping changes to rulescovering agency work, including a banon “exploitative” zero-hours contracts.In a letter to Jonathan Reynolds, theBusiness Secretary, executives said:“Growth is the Government’s priority.Yet the approach taken in the currentconsultation delivers the opposite ofthis. If applied, it would undermineboth the temporary and permanent jobsmarkets, slowing job search by reducing opportunities and potentiallyexposing workers to poorer treatmentand false self-employment.”Executives from companies including Adecco, Hays, Manpower and Randstad signed the letter, warning thatapplying these rules to the country’snear-one million agency workers wouldput £34bn of economic activity at risk.It is understood that the Department ofBusiness and Trade has not yetresponded to requests for a meeting bythe recruitment chiefs, but a recruitment source said engagement had beengood ahead of the Monday deadline.A government spokesman said:“We’re committed to ending one-sidedffi exibility for agency workers and others. We will consider all responses tothe consultation once it closes.” Factory bosses are tearing up spending plans in the wake of Rachel Reeves’sBudget as the Chancellor’s £40bn taxraid sends shock waves through supplychains. The S&P Global purchasingmanagers’ index fell to a nine-monthlow of 48 last month, below the 50 levelthat divides growth from contraction.Workers’ rights Bill ‘to hit growth hopes’By Michael BowA WATER company co-owned by theConservative Party treasurer is preparing to table a £4bn offl er to take controlof Thames Water as the embattled utility strives to avoid collapse.Castle Water, which was co-foundedby Tory treasurer Graham Edwards,is among a clutch of bidders readyingfinancing proposals to inject freshe q u i t y i n t o T h a m e s a h e a d o fa bid deadline on Dec 5.The utility, which has 16m customersin London and the South East, has beenrunning an auction process forwould-be acquirers since March.The water giant is struggling underan £16bn debt pile. Current shareholders have refused to stump up any moremoney, saying it is “un-investable”.As part of the rescue, the water supplier has been seeking at least £3.3bnof new equity from investors overthe next fi ve years to fund its survival.Castle, which is based in Scotland andemploys 450 people, is among a number of parties willing to back Thames,sources said.Mr Edwards co-owns Castle alongside water veteran and former investment banker John Reynolds, who actsas its chief executive.Best known as the co-founder ofproperty giant TT Group, Mr Edwardsalso sits on the board of the Centre forPolicy Studies and is the current Toryparty treasurer. Castle is the UK’s largest privately owned independent watergroup. It works with around 450,000corporate customers across the UK.It is backed fi nancially by the billionaire Pears family, which owns £6bnof property in London through theWilliam Pears Group.If Castle were to take control of theutility giant, its aim would be torelist the business on the London stockmarket and return Thames to publicownership for the fi rst time in decades,sources said.Separately, Thames has confirmedit has appointed a new chief restructuring officer, Julian Gething, to help itsturnaround effl orts.Castle Water and Thames Waterdeclined to comment.Tory treasurer in £4bn Thames Water bid1mAgency workers that could be a ectedby the Government’s employment rightsoverhaul, according to recruitersBy Christopher Jasperand Matt OliverTHE ousting of Carlos Tavares as theboss of Vauxhall’s owner is unlikely tosave its Luton van plant, industryexperts have warned.S t e l l a n t i s ’s a r c h c o s t- c u t t e rannounced he would axe the factorywith the loss of up to 1,100 jobs only lastweek. Mr Tavares’s abrupt departurewas announced on Sunday night, raising hopes Luton could survive undernew leadership. Renault boss Luca deMeo was named as a potential successorby car industry peers.However, industry experts saidwhoever is appointed is likely to con-fi rm the closure. Stellantis is seeking tostreamline a sprawling automotiveempire that also includes Fiat, Peugeot,Citroën and Chrysler.One seasoned executive said themove to consolidate electric van production at Ellesmere Port in Cheshirewas “probably the right decision” andone that Stellantis will be determined tosee through. He said: “I think whoevercomes in next is likely to say, ‘This is notmy problem, that was Carlos’s decision’and let him take the blame.”A spokesman for Stellantis, whichblamed the Luton decision on theGovernment’s net zero rules for electriccars, said the blueprint for reorganisingproduction still stands. The Unite unionsaid it will continue to support members and do whatever it can to maintainvehicle production in Luton.The departure of Mr Tavares shockedthe industry with more than €3.8bn(£3.2bn) wiped offl the value of Stellantis as its shares fell by more than 10pc.Analysis: Page 21Vauxhall’sLuton plantstill at riskJULIAN STRATENSCHULTE/REUTERSShow of strength Thousands of Volkswagen workers strike amid fears that the German car giant is poised to announce mass job losses and factory closures.Waving signs reading “You want war, we are ready!” employees at plants across the country, including Wolfsburg (pictured), walked out.The Daily Telegraph Tuesday 3 December 2024 *** 19By Melissa LawfordSIR KEIR STARMER has been warnedthat he will miss his housing targetby “at least” 388,000 homes, in a blowto the Prime Minister’s efforts torelaunch his Government.Labour will be unable to meetits pledge to build more than 1.12mhomes by 2029 because planningreforms fail to get to the root of Britain’sproblems, according to the Centre forCities, a think tank. Completions areforecast to be at least 25pc short of thePrime Minister’s key manifesto promiseto build 1.5m homes over five years.Shortfalls will be biggest in majorcities, with Greater London poised tomiss its target by 196,000 homes overfive years – 60pc below its goal.On Thursday, Sir Keir will announcea new series of “milestone” promises,against which he will ask voters to judgehim. These are expected to includeLabour’s aim to build 1.5m homes overthis Parliament.The Centre for Cities’ analysis showsthat even the Government’s reduced listof priorities may prove hugely challenging to meet.At the end of July, Angela Rayner, theHousing Secretary, unveiled a “radicalplan” to “get Britain building”. Policiesincluded a new draft National PlanningPolicy Framework (NPPF), which willreinstate mandatory housing targets forlocal authorities, increase the targets inmany areas and open up “grey belt”land for development.However, the Centre for Cities saidSir Keir would either need to “scrap thegreen belt completely” or remove thediscretionary element of the planningsystem. This would mean councilswould no longer be able to make planning decisions on a case-by-case basis.Instead, areas would be designatedconstruction zones and any developerswith the proper paperwork would befree to build there.A government spokesman said:“Despite the dire housebuildinginheritance we are trying to fix, we willdeliver the 1.5m homes our countrydesperately needs and get Britainbuilding again.”Meanwhile, average house priceshave risen to £268,144, just 1pc belowtheir all-time high, according toNationwide data, after climbing by3.7pc in the year to November. Butnearly half a billion pounds was wipedoff value of the biggest housebuilderslisted in London on Monday amid fearsover a combined hit from Budget taxrises and a Government claddingclampdown.Vistry was the biggest faller on theFTSE 100, with shares down by 3.9pc.Rival blue-chip housebuilder TaylorWimpey slipped by 1.4pc, while Persimmon was down by 1.3pc.Labour will miss housing pledge by ‘at least’ 25pc, PM is warnedBy Michael BowST JAMES’S PLACE is planning to axe500 jobs as it struggles to rebuild itsreputation following a fees scandal.Britain’s largest wealth manager willmake hundreds of staff redundant inthe coming months under a restructuring plan drawn up by Mark Fitzpatrick,the new chief executive.The redundancies, first reported byindustry publication Citywire, represent about 15pc of the company’s3,200-strong workforce. The group’s4,800 advisers will not be affected.Mr Fitzpatrick is planning to cut£100m per year from St James’s Place’sbloated costs by 2027 and aims to save£500m by 2030.He took over in October 2023 with aplan to revive the faltering companyafter a regulatory crackdown on fees bythe Financial Conduct Authority.The Cirencester-based group, whichmanages the finances of one millionBritons, had come under scrutinyfollowing claims that some customershad failed to receive an end-of-yearmeeting to discuss their finances towhich they were entitled.The FTSE 100 company last yearbooked a £426m provision and cut itsprized dividend to refund customerswho had experienced advice failings.In some cases, customers who werepaying for the meetings did not receivethem, or there was no record they hadtaken place.Mr Fitzpatrick’s revival plan hasalready won the backing of shareholders. When he unveiled the cost-cuttingmeasures in July, St James’s Place’sshares surged the most since 2008.The company has previouslydeclined to say how many jobs were atrisk under the turnaround plan, despiteadmitting that roles would be cut. A StJames’s Place spokesman said: “Our costreduction plans are focused on simplification and standardisation of processeswithin the business, but a programmeof this size and scale will inevitablyimpact colleagues.“We have now begun consultingwith colleagues to share our proposalfor how this might impact roles, the outcome of which will not be known untilnext year.”St James’s Place axes 500jobs in wake of scandalBy Hannah BolandSPENDING on household essentialshas fallen at its sharpest pace in fiveyears as Budget gloom hits consumerconfidence.Supermarket spend fell 1.8pc lastmonth, according to new figures published by Barclays, while spending onessentials, including groceries and fuel,were down 3.1pc over the month.This was the steepest drop since 2019when Barclays first started collectingthe data. Two thirds of people are looking for ways to reduce the cost of theirweekly shop, according to Barclays,which cited growing concerns amongshoppers on the state of the economy.Spending on debit and credit cardswas down by 0.5pc in November compared with last year. Jack Meaning,chief UK economist at Barclays, said:“Understandably, a number of factorsweighed on consumer spending inNovember, notably easing consumerconfidence post-summer, and expectations that post-Budget, inflation andinterest rates will stay higher in thecoming months.”Confidence in the UK and globaleconomies tumbled last month, Barclays said, while more people were concerned about inflation. The Barclaysdata came as separate figures from theBritish Retail Consortium (BRC )revealed a dip in sales last month, bothin store and online. Total retail saleswere down 3.3pc in the four weeks toNov 23 compared with a year earlier.Helen Dickinson, the chief executiveof the BRC, said the fall was partly downto Black Friday falling a week later thisyear. However, she said: “Even so, lowconsumer confidence and rising energybills have clearly dented non-foodspending. Spending on fashion was particularly weak as households delayedpurchases of new winter clothing.“Retailers will be hoping that seasonal spending is delayed, not diminished and that customers get spendingin the remaining weeks running up toChristmas. If not, retailers will be feeling the squeeze from both sides asreduced revenues are met with hugeadditional costs next year.”Sales are suffering as retailers warnthey will have little choice but to put upprices in response to the Budget. InOctober, the Chancellor unveiled£40bn of tax rises, including a £25bnincrease in employers’ National Insurance contributions.Retail leaders including Sainsbury’sboss Simon Roberts and Asda’s outgoing chairman Lord Rose have warnedthat the higher costs risk leading toprice rises in supermarkets.Supermarkets suffer biggestsales slump in five years‘TheFrancoGermanmotor,which hasbeen centralto makingthings workat theEuropeanlevel, is bust’‘Moreinstabilitycould havea knock-oneffect on theeconomicoutlook’FRANCE is facing the prospect of thecollapse of its government amid a rowover how to tackle the country’s largebudget deficit.Both the hard Right and hard Lefthave vowed to call a vote of noconfidence in Michel Barnier’sadministration after the primeminister forced through acontroversial budget aimed atrepairing France’s public finances.Marine Le Pen’s party hasthreatened to bring down Barnier’sadministration in a row over tax risesand spending cuts.Barnier, who was appointed asprime minister in September, haspushed through €60bn (£50bn) of taxincreases and spending cuts in aneffort to cut the French deficit, whichis spiralling dangerously high.Without action, France risked thewrath of both financial markets andthe European Commission in Brussels.However, Barnier’s minoritygovernment struggled to win supportfor his plans.Yesterday, Barnier said negotiationswith both hard Right and hard Left hadfailed, forcing him to pass his budgetby decree.But the move opens the way for avote of no confidence in parliament.Marine Le Pen’s party has alreadythreatened to use this to bring downthe government.The political upheaval hastriggered a fresh bout of turmoil infinancial markets, with Frenchborrowing costs jumping higher andstocks tumbling.The threat of government collapse isthe latest blow to President EmmanuelMacron, whose credibility has beenbadly damaged by his decision to call asnap election in the summer. Thatmove backfired and left his supportersin a weakened position in parliament.The dire state of France’s publicfinances have forced Barnier toconfront difficult budget decisionsdespite his lack of parliamentarysupport. France’s deficit has alreadyrisen from 4.7pc of GDP in 2022 to5.5pc in 2023, with credit ratingsagency Standard and Poor’s estimatingborrowing will hit 6.2pc this year.EU rules say members should run abudget deficit of no more than 3pc andhave a plan in place to get back to thatlevel if the deficit is above it. Thosewho do not ultimately can be fined.Under Barnier’s planned budget,analysts estimate the deficit will fall to5.3pc next year.Not only is this still far higher thanthe EU’s target, Frank Gill, at S&PGlobal Ratings, says France has a poortrack record when it comes toborrowing, which worries markets.“France clearly stands out,” he says.Borrowing has increased inFrance even as other countries havehad more success in getting deficitsdown after the pandemic and theenergy crisis. France kept subsidies forenergy bills longer than some othernations, while its economic recoveryhas also proved underwhelming,hitting tax receipts.Government spending nowaccounts for more than 57pc of GDP,while debt is just over 110pc of GDP.Benefits spending, including onpensions, has risen to 25pc of GDP.In Britain, where anxiety aboutpublic finances is also high, statespending is 45pc of GDP and debt isjust under 100pc.France’s problems stem from thepensions system. Macron has struggledto raise the state pension age as thepopulation ages, battling majorprotests to move France’s age from62 to 64.And while three quarters ofworking-age Britons are employed,only around 68pc of French adults arein work, according to the OECD,putting pressure on the benefits bill.Generous pensions and benefits areforcing Paris to borrow, but this is fastbecoming an unsustainably costlyarrangement. France’s debt interestbill this year is €53bn, which will soakup around 3.6pc of tax revenues. S&Ppredicts interest costs will rise to€75bn, or 4.5pc of revenues, by 2027.This debt interest squeeze has putBarnier in an extremely difficultposition. He must cut spending – orraise taxes – in a manner that satisfiesnot just the EU but also France’spowerful hard-Left and hard-Rightparliamentary minorities, not tomention voters. “That is a prettydifficult mandate for a prime ministerwho is lacking a majority in thenational assembly,” says Gill.Barnier proposed a package ofmeasures but several proved highlycontroversial, including increasing thetax paid on electricity and delaying aninflation-linked increase in pensions.He also wants a one-off taxsurcharge on the profits of bigbusinesses and an extra levy onhigh-earning households.Barnier offered some concessions tothe opposition, including on theelectricity tax, in an effort to winsupport in parliament. But this failed,resulting in the use of a decree and thethreat of a no confidence vote. If thegovernment does fail, France will beplunged deeper into political upheaval.Macron cannot call another snapelection until the summer, raising theprospect that he will have to try toform another government to hold outuntil that point.The threat of months of politicalturmoil in France have led to a sharprise in government borrowing costsand a sell-off of French stocks.France now pays an interest rate of2.9pc on its 10-year debt, far above the2.05pc paid by Germany. Paris’sborrowing costs are now in line withGreece’s. On the stock market, theCac 40 dropped by as much as 1.2pcyesterday. The euro fell almost 1pcagainst the dollar to below $1.05.If the government falls, analystsexpect a variation of last year’s budgetwould remain in place, resulting inhigher borrowing. On top of that thegovernment has repeatedly missed itsborrowing targets with weakeconomic growth and poor consumerspending key factors.Gilles Moec, the chief economist atAxa, says: “More instability could havea knock-on effect on the economicoutlook. Consumer confidence hasalready declined, and the savings ratecould rise further, thwarting therebound in consumption on which thegovernment is counting to support taxreceipts in 2025.”Political instability and the budgetcrisis risk combining with threats suchas Donald Trump’s planned trade war.France could lose its governmentjust as Germany faces snap elections. Itrisks leaving the EU effectivelyleaderless just as the new US presidenttakes over in January, says Iain Begg,professor at the London School ofEconomics’ European Institute.“The Franco-German motor, whichhas been traditionally so central tomaking things work at the Europeanlevel, is bust,” he says.“Paris is in difficulty, Olaf Scholz ison his way out, probably to bereplaced by Friedrich Merz as thenext German chancellor.”Mabrouk Chetouane, at NatixisInvestment Managers, says it is“incredible timing” for Europe toface the loss of two key governments,noting that the challenges fromthe Ukraine war and China alsostill remain.“We are not well equipped tonegotiate anything,” he says.“This is not the moment wherewe need a weak Europe. We need astrong Europe, strong leaders andstrong economies. We don’t havethem, unfortunately.”Why the deficitcould bringdown Macron’sgovernmentDire state of France’spublic finances haveforced the presidentto confront difficultdecisions, reportsTim WallaceWinter wonderland The construction site of the 26thHarbin Ice and Snow World in Heilongjiang, China.CFOTO/AVALONBusiness15pcThe proportion of St James’s Place’sworkforce that face losing their jobs as itseeks to cut £100m of costs per year20 *** Tuesday 3 December 2024 The Daily TelegraphBy Matt OliverBRITISH bill payers have spentan “absurd” £1bn to temporarily switchoff wind turbines so far this year as thenational grid struggles to cope withtheir power.T h e a m o u n t o f w i n d p owe r“curtailed” in the first 11 months of 2024stood at about 6.6 terawatt hours (TWh),up from 3.8 TWh in 2023. Curtailment iswhere wind turbines are paid to switchoff at times of high winds to stop thegrid being overwhelmed. The jump incurtailment follows the opening ofmore wind farms at a time when thecountry still lacks the infrastructureneeded to transport all the electricitythey generate at busy times.Clem Cowton, the director of external affairs at Octopus, said: “It’s absurdthat Britain pays Scottish wind farmsto turn off when it’s windy, while simultaneously paying gas-power stationsin the South to turn on.”Octopus and others have called forthe country to shift to a regional pricingsystem, which they say would incentivise more wind farms to be built closerto where power is needed.This would reduce the need for hundreds of miles of cables to deliverelectricity from the North and Scotland,where many wind farms are, to theSouth where demand is greatest.However, the plans would be controversial as it would likely mean homesand businesses in the South paid morefor their energy than those in the North.Jason Mann, an electricity marketsexpert at FTI Consulting, said the highcurtailment costs underlined the lack ofcables to transport power to the South.However, he also argued the £1bn costwas partly the result of Britain’s nationalelectricity pricing system. Mr Mannsaid: “At some point you have to dosomething that encourages greaterdemand in the North, for examplelower prices.”According to the National EnergySystem Operator (Neso), curtailmentcosts are on course to surge to £6bnby 2030 if the status quo continues.Ministers are said to be examiningpotential options for market reform.Critics of regional pricing argue thatswitching to such a system wouldplunge a slew of planned developmentsand net zero targets into uncertainty.In 2022, some 3.5 TWh of power werecurtailed but prices were much higherafter Russia’s invasion of Ukraine. Justfive years ago, 1.9 TWh hours werewasted at a cost of £242m.A government spokesman said: “TheNational Energy System Operator’sreport shows we can achieve cleanpower by 2030 with cheaper electricity.We achieved a record-setting round ofrenewable projects, with enough powerfor the equivalent of 11m homes.“We will work with industry toupgrade our infrastructure to getrenewable electricity on the grid.”Britain is paying ‘absurd’ £1bn for wind farms to be switched offBy Michael BowBRITAIN’S largest smart meter provider has been taken over by privateequity in a £4bn deal, amid a mountingbacklash against the Government’sambitious rollout plan.Calisen, which operates 15m smartmeters across the UK, has been sold toSingapore’s GIC and Swedish privateequity firm EQT, according to a statement yesterday.The duo will pay about £4bn for a66pc stake in the business, taking overthe stake from previous backers BlackRock, Goldman Sachs and Mubadala,one of Abu Dhabi’s sovereign wealthfunds. Calisen is based in Manchesterand employs 1,380 people across the UKand installs and operates smart meterson behalf of a range of companiesincluding British Gas. It also installselectric vehicle charging points, as wellas heat pumps.EQT and GIC have snapped up thecompany as they look to cash in on therollout of smart meters across the UK.The Government wants 75pc of UKhomes to have the devices installed bynext year, up from 59pc last year.However, there are mounting concerns about the timeline for the rolloutof smart meters following issues withthe devices. Citizens Advice recentlywarned that half of the 52,000 energycomplaints it received this year wererelated to inaccurate readings given bysmart meters.Martin Lewis, the TV consumerchampion, recently urged Ed Miliband,the Energy Secretary, to overhaul the“perverse” rollout of the devicesbecause some deliver inaccurate bills.Smart meters are meant to giveenergy customers a live reading ofusage to deliver more accurate bills.However, about one in 10 devices donot work properly. Around 4m devices– about 11pc of the 35m smart meterscurrently installed in UK homes – are“dumb”, meaning they fail to communicate data with the energy supplier properly. Suppliers now install secondgeneration meters, known as SMETS2,but the first generation devices knownas SMETS1 can go into dumb mode ifconsumers switch energy providers.Calisen is one of the UK’s largestinstallers and operators of smartmeters, alongside Smart Metering Systems, Macquarie and Horizons.The group operates under two brandnames: Calvin Capital, which owns andmanages the devices; and Lowri Beck,which installs the equipment and provides meter reading.Private equity pays £4bnfor smart meter companyBy Szu Ping ChanBRITAIN’S attitude towards work haschanged since lockdown amid a risingsick-note culture that is damaging theeconomy, the former boss of Waitrosehas claimed.Many workers are now more worriedabout sick pay than other in-work benefits, Lord Price said, as he highlightedresearch from his think tank WorkL,which showed that almost a fifth ofpeople who said health was a majorissue in their workplace were most concerned about paid leave while unwell.“It’s a reflection of attitudes,” he said.“We picked up lots of comments about‘we want sick pay to be increased’ or‘we want sick pay time to be extended’.”Lord Price, who also served as trademinister, said lockdown during thepandemic had “undoubtedly played apart” in the rise in economic inactivitysince 2020, with close to 2.8m peoplenow neither in work nor looking for ajob due to long-term illness.Lord Price told The Telegraph: “I justthink that those Covid years found people being paid to be at home. As a consequence of not working, I think there is amindset switch about: ‘Well, the statewill pay for us to be at home.’” He alsocalled for an overhaul of the benefitssystem as he unveiled a new reportwarning that the majority of employersbelieved Gen Z workers “lack driveand ambition”.WorkL’s study covered 100,000 companies across more than 100 countriesand singled out Britain’s worklessnesscrisis as a major issue for its economy.The study found that British workerswere among the unhappiest in theworld. Just 74pc of Britons believedtheir job meant they were doing something “worthwhile”.WorkL found that 90pc of employerssaid younger employees “view workdifferently”. Britons aged 19 to 24 hadthe highest “flight risk” among majoradvanced economies, suggesting theywere less attached to the world of work.But Lord Price argued that differentattitudes towards work among generations was not because young peoplewere lazy.Rather, he blamed lockdown for creating poor working experiences.He said: “For a lot of young people, theytake on jobs and they never go tothe office. These are people who arebeing recruited into jobs who are notseeing their managers face-to-face.They’re not seeing their colleagues,and guess what? After four or fivemonths, they’re pretty disillusioned.”Lockdown broke UK’s workethic, says ex-Waitrose boss6.6 TWhThe amount of wind power ‘curtailed’ inthe first 11 months of 2024 is nearlydouble the amount from last year‘Carlos cameunder attackin Italybecause thecompanywas seen asbeing moreFrenchcentred’‘Tavares’sapproach ofcutting costsworked upto a point,when therewasn’t somuchpressurefrom China’JUST nine months ago, Carlos Tavareslooked like one of the mostunassailable chief executives in theautomotive business.The former Stellantis boss – whoseabrupt departure emerged late onSunday – had overseen yet anotheryear of record profits at the world’sfourth biggest carmaker.This was partly the result ofrelentless cost-cutting, after thePortuguese executive in 2021masterminded the stitching togetherof Fiat Chrysler and PSA Group tocreate a carmaking Frankenstein’smonster. But behind the enormousscale of Stellantis, problems have beenbrewing that have since broughtTavares’s tenure to a screeching halt.They threaten to create a nightmarefor his successor as well.Among them is the question ofwhether Stellantis, a global behemothspanning 14 brands includingVauxhall, Fiat, Peugeot and Jeep, cancontinue as a vast chimera, analystssay. At the same time, the chiefexecutive’s demise is the latest sign ofturmoil gripping Europe’s car industryas Chinese rivals muscle in to theirmarkets around the world.“The situation is getting so difficult,not just for Stellantis but for all thelegacy Western carmakers,” says FelipeMunoz, an auto industry analyst at JatoDynamics. “Tavares’s approach ofcutting costs worked up to a point,when there wasn’t so much pressurefrom China.“But what we are seeing is that thisstrategy has limits.”An engineer by training and arace-car obsessive, Tavares earned areputation among investors forgripping costs and delivering doubledigit profit margins no matter what.He rose through the ranks atRenault – where he began as a test cardriver – and Nissan, before taking overas chief executive of PSA Group in2014, the French giant best known forPeugeot and Citroën.There, he oversaw a spectacularturnaround and the acquisition of theOpel and Vauxhall brands fromGeneral Motors in 2017.But his masterstroke followed sixyears later, when he agreed a megamerger with Fiat Chrysler, the Italiancar giant controlled by the powerfulAgnelli family.Three years of record profitsfollowed, as he once again bore downon costs and delivered bigger savingsthan many investors had dreamed. Itlooked like classic Tavares.“Carlos is probably the smartest guyI’ve ever worked with,” says AndyPalmer, a former Nissan executivewho worked with Tavares at theJapanese company.“His knowledge of the car isextraordinary, he’s a real product guy.But he also has a very soundunderstanding of finance and business,so he’s a rare talent. I wouldn’t say he’sruthless, but he can sometimes comeacross as a bit of a cold fish – and thathas allowed him to get things done.”As with many leaders, however, thequalities Tavares was praised for at theheight of his powers have now beenblamed for his undoing.In July, it became clear thatStellantis was in big trouble whenthe company revealed half-year profitshad collapsed from €10.9bn (£9.1bn)to €5.6bn. Worse, many of theproblems appeared to have theirgenesis in the chief executive’stough-love approach. Criticscomplained of stagnating productranges, high prices, quality issuesand falling market shares in nearlyevery region.Tavares, who said he was humbledby the July numbers, put the problemsdown to a “bump in the road” andimmediately set about reshufflingexecutives and trying to impose tighterdiscipline – suggesting he was intenton sticking around.But the dismal results were followedby a profit warning in September thatwiped 50pc of the value from Stellantisshares. Then the company announcedin October that Tavares would stepdown in early 2026. By Sunday night,the chief executive’s fate was sealed asthe company confirmed his departureamid reported differences with theboard over future strategy.Yesterday, insiders claimed Tavareshad been moving “too fast to retrievehis reputation”, and was too focused onthe short term, managing to “angereverybody in the process”, accordingto the Financial Times.Industry experts also pointed to aplethora of disputes the chiefexecutive had become embroiled inaround the world.In Italy, Stellantis has been engagedin a war of words with Giorgia Meloni’sgovernment over the company’sdecision to move production of AlfaRomeo cars to Poland. Ministers in theMeloni administration accused thecompany of trying to misleadconsumers with the “Italian sounding”name of Milano for a foreign-madeSUV model, which was laterrenamed Junior.Elsewhere, Stellantis has heavilycriticised electric vehicle salestargets in the UK and recentlyannounced the closure of its vanfactory in Luton. And in North America– traditionally its biggest market –Tavares was accused by dealershipsand unions of leaving brands includingChrysler, Dodge, Jeep and Ram in an“anemic and diminished state”following mass layoffs, productioncuts, rows with suppliers and qualitycontrol issues.In September, Stellantis was forcedto recall 194,000 hybrid Jeep SUVs toaddress fire risks involving a batterycomponent. On top of all this,Stellantis, like rivals such as Renaultand Volkswagen, is battling for marketshare around the world, with low-costChinese brands racing ahead in theelectric vehicle (EV) segment.“Carlos came under attack in Italybecause the company was seen asbeing more French centred. Then hecame under attack from theAmericans, with Jeep essentiallyfailing. So he has really suffered at thehands of both geopolitical andownership issues,” observes Palmer.Many now think Stellantis willstruggle to revive itself whilestruggling under the weight of somany marques – not least similar massmarket brands such as Citroën andFiat. Moreover, many Europeancompanies simply cannot competewith their vertically integratedChinese counterparts onmanufacturing costs.This is why Volkswagen isundergoing its own painful shake upthat could see it lay off tens ofthousands of workers and closefactories in Germany for the first time.Against this backdrop, says Jato’sMunoz, whoever takes over fromTavares is bound to look at shuttingdown or spinning off some of thebrands that currently sit in theStellantis tent.Ultimately, Munoz says, Tavares’sbiggest legacy will be the creation ofStellantis – though whether it survivesthe company’s next phase is up fordebate.“I think there are going to have to besome big decisions soon,” Munoz adds.Nine months ago, it was a safe betthat Tavares would be the man makingthem. Now, someone else will betrying to keep the monster he createdunder control.Calls to dismantle ‘Frankenstein’ Vauxhall ownerQuestions grow overfuture of car giantStellantis followingthe departure ofCarlos Tavares,reports Matt OliverStatement piece Artist Philip Colbert at his exhibition,Battle for Lobsteropolis, at the Saatchi Gallery in London.GUY BELL/SHUTTERSTOCKBusinessThe Daily Telegraph Tuesday 3 December 2024 *** 2122 *** Tuesday 3 December 2024 The Daily Telegraphfrom some circles – includingMicrosoft itself – may have been thebiggest factor in its retreat, but itdoesn’t change the fact that the techgiant was totally wrong-footed in thefi rst place.Having had an initial 250p a shareoff er emphatically rejected by DirectLine, can Aviva count on a morereceptive response as it contemplates asecond bid? “We expect that anti-trustconstraints will be limited,” saysAlex Mackenzie, at BNP Paribas Exane.But that feels premature. True, thethreshold for the CMA to intervenetends to be when 25pc or more of anymarket is in the hands of a singleplayer, but it can’t aff ord to be tooprescriptive, especially in the wakeof the cost of living crisis.In home insurance, the marketshares of Aviva and Direct Line areroughly 12pc and 10pc respectively.That would create a new dominantforce with more than a fi fth of thatsegment concentrated in the handsof one company. The potential forreduced competition and meaningfulprice rises must be real.In car insurance, the need forscrutiny is just as pressing, even iftheir combined clout isn’t quite asconsiderable and Admiral would stillretain the number one spot. WithAviva controlling 8pc of the marketand Direct Line speaking for 12pc of allcustomers, their share would be onlyslightly less than in home insurance.A combined 20pc would still begrounds for real concern. The costof insuring a car has soared 82pc inthe three years since lockdown ended,according to the Offl ce for NationalStatistics.It now costs an average of £612 ayear to insure a vehicle, according tothe latest fi gures from the Associationof British Insurers. When theincreasing cost of buying a car, aswell as repairing one, and fi lling it withpetrol is factored in too, many peoplesimply cannot aff ord to drive any more.Yet most people can’t aff ord not to,either. Seven in 10 adults say theirlifestyle requires having a vehicle, arecent Ipsos study found. It meansmore and more are taking on debts tokeep driving, according to Comparethe Market, or in the most extremecases, driving uninsured cars.It’s not as if the union-crippledrailways or hollowed-out local busservices represent a realisticalternative form of getting to andfrom work for millions of drivers.There are other red ffi ags. Bothcompanies share the same institutionalshareholders and the suggestion fromthe Aviva side is that these fundmanagers are receptive to a deal. Is itbecause there is already an expectationof raised prices and higher returns?The timing of it looks somewhatsuspect, too. Direct Line boss AdamWinslow is surely entitled to more timeto show his turnaround plan can havethe desired eff ect given that he’s onlybeen at the company since March.There is also a suspicion that Blanc hasturned to takeovers for want of otherideas. Having been at Aviva for morethan four years now, is she looking tocement her legacy with some empirebuilding? Might Blanc also be countingon the political connections forged as amember of the Prime Minister’sBusiness Council?The CMA’s track record when itcomes to studying the insurance sectoris hardly reassuring. In 2014, it wasforced to admit that there was nothingit could do to address complaints aboutthe excessive cost of courtesy cars fordrivers involved in accidents. It limitedaction to fi ddling around the marginsof the price comparison market.With prices seemingly out ofcontrol, the CMA would be duty-boundto ignore all the noise from criticsand get its act together this time. If itcomes to fruition, Aviva’s deal mustbe investigated.US election has dealt investors a Trump cardPresident-elect promiseslow taxes and high tarifi sbut no one really knowswhat he’s thinkingSOMEBODY make it all make sense.In the weeks since he was re-electedto the White House Donald Trumphas threatened to impose tariff s onthe country’s three biggest tradingpartners, announced plans to deportmillions of illegal immigrants despitea tight labour market, hung out withcrypto bros and done little to dampenspeculation he’ll meddle with centralbank independence.Yet the S&P 500 is up 4.3pc. There’salso been an uptick in the pace thatglobal investors have pulled moneyout of Europe and emerging marketsin order to throw it at US equities overthe past month, according to datacompiled by Barclays.Most economists agree that raisedtariff s on imports would likely lead tohigher prices for US consumers, whichwould ultimately spell bad news for UScompanies too.One possible explanation doing therounds is that investors are choosingto believe in the parts of the Trumpagenda they like the sound of – tax cutsand deregulation – while choosing toignore the fact that the president-electis tooling up for a trade war.Not so, according to the latest assetsallocation survey by Absolute StrategyResearch (ASR), one of the best gaugesof investor sentiment and the fi rstsince the US election. It suggestsmoney managers are increasinglyworried that Trump’s not bluffl ng andis now showing a 58pc probability thatinffi ation, having steadily fallen steadilysince its peak in May 2022, will behigher a year from now.ASR concludes this is a reffi ectionof investor concerns about theconsequences of higher tariff scombined with Trump’s plans todeport millions of illegal immigrantsat a time when the labour market isalready stretched.Yet, the research outfi t’s fi ndingsalso show a sharp swing in favour of USequities with the probability they willoutperform non-US equities jumpingby a chunky 11 percentage points sincethe previous quarter to hit 63pc.The president-elect has certainlygiven investors plenty to chew over.First, there was the appointment ofScott Bessent, a well-respected formerhedge fund manager, as TreasurySecretary. This was warmly welcomedby investors (with short memories).There were similar hopes thatGary Cohn, the former chief operatingoffl cer of Goldman Sachs, would helpTrump see economic sense duringthe fi rst administration. In his bookFear: Trump in the White House,Bob Woodward claimed that Cohntwice removed draft letters fromTrump’s desk – one cancelling a tradeagreement with South Korea and onewithdrawing the US from the NorthAmerican Free Trade Agreement(Nafta) – so that the president wouldn’tsee them.But, in the end, he lasted barely ayear, with the more populist wing ofthe White House team having dubbedhim “Globalist Gary”. He eventuallyresigned from the National EconomicCouncil in March 2018 after Trumpcancelled a meeting that Cohn hadarranged with the end users of steeland aluminium in an attempt todissuade the president fromimposing tariff s on these rawmaterials. Cohn was eventuallyreplaced by Kevin Hassett, a staunchdefender of Trump’s punitive tariff s,who the president-elect announcedlast week would take up the sameposition in the second administration.In a recent interview with theFinancial Times, Hassett argued thatTrump’s protectionist rhetoric wasmerely the opening gambit that wouldlead to negotiations and eventuallymutual reductions in tariff s.Trump himself somewhat spoiledthe party mood in US markets byannouncing on social media that hewas planning to hit Mexico and Canadawith 25pc tariff s unless Uncle Sam’snearest neighbours did somethingabout the ffi ow of drugs and illegalimmigrants into the US.It’s true that by the end of the week,Trump had also posted about havinga “wonderful conversation” withMexican president Claudia Sheinbaumwhile the Canadian prime ministerJustin Trudeau had rushed down toMar-a-Largo for a calming dinner.But those optimists who saw all thisas a sign that Trump will mostly usetariff s as a threat with which tosqueeze concessions out of othercountries must contend with the factthat he’s now deploying the tactic inthe service of foreign policy as muchas economic policy. Goldman Sachslabelled the latest sabre-rattling “theopening salvo of Trade War Two”.The worry is that sabre can’t stayin its scabbard forever; Trump willeventually have to come good onthreats, fi rstly to prove they are notempty and, secondly, to raise therevenue he’ll need to fund hisproposed tax cuts.Presidents have a fair amount ofleeway to impose tariff s if they believethere is a risk to national security(which is a fairly nebulous concept).But targeting a single country wouldbe considered discriminatory by theWorld Trade Organisation. It’s unlikelyTrump will care much about that.Nevertheless, he might bepersuaded that broad tariff s of 10pc to20pc on all imports across the board,which is what he promised on thecampaign trail, would make it harderfor the individual countries to claimthey were being picked on. Long storyshort, it’s still incredibly unclear howTrade War Two is likely to pan out. Atthis point, investors looking for acoherent economic policy in Trump’ssocial media posts are about as likely tofi nd enlightenment as soothsayerspoking around in the entrails of asacrifi cial goat.Such uncertainty will weigh heavilyon countries and companies that mayor may not be in the fi ring line. Thebond yields in export-heavy nationssuch as Germany have fallen sincethe election. And this is the real keyto investor bullishness about the US.It’s not so much that investors lovewhat Trump might do for domesticmarkets. Rather they are looking atwhat his inffi ationary policies mightmean for the price of Treasuries andthe reopening of trade hostilities coulddo to international companies.Because the Land of the Free ishome to the world’s reserve currency,investors have little choice but to cutit more slack than they ever would toother countries should they behave inthe same way. The rest of the worldcan only look on jealously at Trump’s“exorbitant privilege” at the same timeas hoping he doesn’t do anything crazyenough to jeopardise it.Businesses should be bold and use tractor power to send a message to the GovernmentSINCE the Budget, farmers from acrossthe UK have run a hugely eff ectivecampaign against Labour’s proposedchanges to inheritance tax.Thousands joined a march inLondon, MPs were lobbied inParliament and in their constituencies,and a social media campaign exposedthe Government’s apparent lack ofunderstanding of how its own policywould aff ect the future of farming.Much of the campaign has beendriven not by the offl cial bodyrepresenting farmers, but rather byindividual farmers and their familiesacting spontaneously. Polling showsthey are decisively winning theargument, with over half of the public(57pc) backing the farmers and just24pc supporting the Government.Even some Labour MPs are nervouslyquestioning the changes.What is striking in contrast is themuted opposition from businesses andentrepreneurs to the many antibusiness measures featured in thesame Budget.Individual companies or theirleaders have certainly spoken outagainst many of the changes,particularly the rise in employers’National Insurance. Sir James Dyson,the inventor and designer, has calledthe Budget “spiteful” and Cobra Beer’sLord Bilimoria has warned that it willdampen business growth.Eighty of the largest retailers evensigned a joint letter explaining that theconsequences of Rachel Reeves’sactions would be higher prices andjob losses. Humiliatingly for theChancellor, she was greeted with asullen silence at the CBI conference atthe start of the week.But it is all just too polite anddefeatist. In fact, Rain Newton-Smith,the CBI’s director-general, appearedto accept from the start the principlethat businesses should pay more taxes,saying: “No one is questioning that weneed to see the taxes rise to really helpfund our public services.”This sort of attitude is the reasonthat the Government felt emboldenedto pile more taxes on to business,despite all the pre-election promises.The reason is simple: mostcorporations and larger companiesneed to maintain a positiverelationship with the Government,especially the ones most potentiallyaff ected by regulatory change.But if we cannot rely on Britain’smajor corporations to storm thepolitical barricades, where are thesmall businesses and the nextgeneration of entrepreneurs?The Budget actually hitentrepreneurs even harder than largerbusinesses. On top of increases toNational Insurance and the minimumwage, the Chancellor upped the capitalgains tax for small businesses from10pc to 18pc. Yet the entrepreneurs’voices are largely silent. Why? Well,the next generation of entrepreneurswho have yet to start their ventureshave no one to speak up for them.Our research at Onward found thatthere are 7m people ready to get theirown venture started. Many of theseindividuals will simply have looked atthe Budget and decided to postponetheir dream of starting up a newcompany, possibly forever.That is a loss to them individually.But it is also a blow to Britain as it isstart-ups that drive innovation,competition and growth. Similarly,there are millions more microbusinesses that will have beendeterred from expanding. Their voicesare also missing from the debate, but itdoes not mean there is not genuineanger among those entrepreneursabout being let down by Labour.Since the Budget, I have talked todozens of businesses that are nowcalculating how to pass on the taxhikes to customers or even planning torelocate abroad.One hospitality entrepreneur Ispoke to is not only reducingheadcount but even paying to get outof lease obligations so as to contractthe size of his company. They all havethe same message: Britain has becomean unfriendly place for their business– why bother?They believe that everyentrepreneur has the odds of successstacked against them at the best oftimes, but that it’s virtually impossiblewith the amount of tax and regulationthey now face.What they do see are many seriousthreats to the British economy on thehorizon: the lingering impacts of thewar in Ukraine, rises in energy costsand trade wars. More concerning isthat there is little sign of anyconcrete plans from the Governmentto deliver the supposed growth at thecore of its manifesto.Some remember, too, that the lastLabour government promised to endwhat they called “boom and bust”.That ended with the fi nancial crash of2008 that led to a deep recession.The Budget was a step backwards:more taxes and more regulation are norecipe for that economic revival.Maybe it is time for businesses to learnfrom the farmers and get theirentrepreneur “tractors” out on to thestreets to ensure the Chancellorunderstands that message.‘As the US is home to theworld’s reserve currency,investors have no choicebut to cut it slack’ANDREWBARCLAYAMANDA BLANC, the Aviva chiefexecutive, must have been rubbing herhands with glee when the boss ofBritain’s embattled competitionwatchdog stood up to speak at theChatham House think tank last month.Competition and Markets Authority(CMA) chief Sarah Cardell came outwith a fresh pledge to support“productive and sustainable growth”.After years of griping from the City andWestminster about alleged overThe CMA has aresponsibility to protectmotorists in the eventof any takeoverDrivers beware: Aviva wants to eatits competition in car insuranceBusinessBENWRIGHTBENMARLOWBear in mindSculptures aredisplayed at HongKong airport duringthe welcomeceremony of theexhibition “PandaGo!”. The installationwill showcase 2,500artworks at fourdifierent venuesthroughout the city,where enthusiasm forthe bears has grownsince two cubs wereborn at a theme park.CHAN LONG HEI/AP PHOTOanxiety concerning harm toconsumers, her words were taken asthe herald of a more permissive erafor takeovers. Perhaps it was just acoincidence, but within days corporateBritain was being assailed by a barrageof fresh bids – now joined by a £3.3bnoff er from Aviva for smaller rivalDirect Line.Cardell is astute enough to knowthat changing the mood music in thisway was a big move. But it’s unlikelyshe was expecting the shift to be testedso quickly and in such a big way.Make no mistake about it: Aviva’smove is a huge examination for theregulator for the simple and obviousreason that any tie-up with Direct Linecould have a serious impact oninsurance prices. The implications fordrivers threatens to be signifi cant, andthe CMA will have no choice but totake a proper look at the proposedtie-up if a deal is agreed. For millions ofcash-strapped households, the fearmust be that in her desire to silencethe CMA’s many critics and rise tothe challenge on growth, the deal iswaved through without fullconsideration to what it means forpremiums. With car insuranceespecially having gone through theroof in recent years, the watchdogmust not allow political pressure toweaken its bite.No doubt, as Panmure Liberumanalyst Barrie Cornes points out, theCMA “will have a view on thecombined group but we assume thatAviva have considered this and havediscounted it as being an issue”.Yet there are plenty of examples ofbig companies thinking they’vecorrectly anticipated the reaction ofregulators and politicians only to beproved completely wrong.Take Microsoft’s misjudged $70bn(£55bn) approach for ActivisionBlizzard in 2022.
