They have fargreater access to  nancial marketsacross a range of geographies and assetclasses with the help of their preferredchoice of investment platform.They can build their portfoliosquickly and e ciently at the tap of ascreen or click of a mouse. They can doso directly through company shares, orvia actively managed collectives suchas funds and investment trusts, pluspassive options like index trackers andexchange-traded funds (ETFs).They have the option to use morecomplex instruments, such asderivatives, to enhance returns – ormagnify losses, as the case may be.They can see live prices, where thebid-o er spreads at which they cansell and buy are clear and, in normalmarket circumstances, narrow.They have access to unparalleledlevels of information: the granularityof detail published in companies’annual reports and the regulatorydisclosure requirements they mustmeet are almost overwhelminglyin-depth. They have innumerableweight of information available meansit can be di cult to sift out what isimportant and what is not, or evenwhat is factual and what is not,depending upon the source.Company executives are now oftenincentivised with bonuses, stock oroptions to hit certain goals. Those of anunscrupulous bent may cut corners intheir quest to meet these targets, or atleast obfuscate the ever-growingadjusted and underlying numbers.Debt mountains have grown, oftenwith the aid of policymakers who haveforced down the cost of loans to try andsolve debt crises by encouragingever-greater borrowing.It feels like accidents are becomingmore frequent and more violent, asmarkets stumble through the 1973-74bear market, the 1987 Black Mondaycrash, the collapse of Barings in 1995,the Asian debt crisis of 1997-98, thebursting of the media and telecomsbubble in 2000, the global  nancialcrisis of 2007-09 and the Europeandebt crisis of the early 2010s. Don’tforget the collapse of a series ofAmerican banks (and one giant Swissone) in 2023.As a result, policymakers have hadto intervene more than once to try andrestore order to markets, not leastbecause they feared a spillover into the“real” economy from the  nancial one.That is a black mark in the eyes ofanyone who believes in free markets,as there is no way that gains should bepocketed when times are good, onlyfor losses to be shared whencomplexity, opacity and leverage nally come home to roost.Investors must be  exible and notset in their ways. They must continueto carefully lay down their investmentstrategy, target returns, time horizonand appetite for risk.Then, harder still, they must stick totheir disciplines, doing their own duediligence along the way – andhopefully Questor will be here foranother 60 years (and more) to help.columns, websites and blogs that canbe accessed to help with research.They have the support of regulators,who strive to ensure that  nancialservice providers put customers at thevery heart of their operations, seek tohelp them obtain the best possibleoutcomes relative to their  nancialneeds and goals, and insist companiesobserve the highest standards ofgovernance and behaviour.In sum, it is all a very long way fromthe days of stock prices on tickertape,closed shops, men-only institutions,inside information and bowler-hatted,pinstriped stereotypes.As such, investors who have kept anopen mind and embraced rapidchange, as Getty counselled, have hadevery chance to protect and enhancetheir wealth. But as Groucho Marxonce warned: “Be open-minded, butnot so open-minded that your brainsfall out.” The myriad advances of thelast 60 years have not stoppedscandals, panics, or crashes, nor havethey tempered animal spirits.There is a temptation to over-trade,incurring additional costs and taxesthat compound and erode returns ase ciently as holding for the long termand reinvesting dividends can boostthem. The average holding times forlisted securities has collapsed, partlybecause the percentage of tradingdriven by algorithm-controlledprogrammes has surged. The sheerInvesting inthe digital ageTechnology hasinformed andempoweredstock-pickersin a way perhapspreviouslythoughtunimaginable‘Be openmindedbut notso openmindedthat yourbrainsfall out’A French data giant is considering a£1bn swoop for the media companybehind Britain’s TV ratings body.Paris-based Ipsos, known for itsmarket research services and politicalpolling, con rmed yesterday that it isin discussions over a potentialacquisition of Kantar Media.Kantar Group, which is owned byadvertising agency WPP and privateequity  rm Bain Capital, also carriesout market research, as well as surveysmeasuring consumer behaviour.Its media division provides audienceresearch across several formats, but isbest known for collecting data forBarb, the UK’s o cial TV ratingsagency. A deal for Kantar’s media armcould pave the way for sales of otherparts of the group.Ipsos plots £1bntakeover of TVratings agencyTopps Tiles has defended itsperformance in recent years after oneof its largest shareholders accused itof making a series of “costly blunders”.The homeware retailer said it is“continuing to take market share in adi cult trading environment” after itwas attacked by Austrian investor MSGalleon. Topps Tiles said its 5.4pc dipin annual adjusted revenues was “asubstantial out-performance of anoverall market which was estimatedto be down 10-15pc”.The response comes after PiotrLipko, MS Galleon’s managingdirector, wrote to Topps Tileschairman Paul Forman, to complainabout the company’s “completefailure” to adapt to modern retailtrends, according to The Sunday Times.Topps Tiles hitsback at ‘costlyblunders’ jibeAn o ce space start-up founded byLord Cameron’s former technologytsar is preparing to appointadministrators.Second Home, founded in 2014 byRohan Silva, a one-time special adviserto the former prime minister, o ersstylish commercial space in London,Lisbon and Los Angeles. But it hasstruggled in recent years to containcosts, while facing collapsing revenuesafter the pandemic and the rising trendof remote work.It is understood that restructuringexperts from FRP Advisory, whichpreviously handled Second Home’s2022 recapitalisation, are being linedup to advise on a possibleadministration. Both FRP and SecondHome were approached for comment.Cameron aide’sofice start-up onbrink of collapseThe electronic vape manufacturer thathas rescued Typhoo is looking torelaunch the 121-year-old company asan ice tea maker amid declining salesof its traditional hot drink product.Sandy Chadha, the chief executiveof Supreme, said he was consideringthe move to appeal to a youngergeneration of consumers.Hot tea has been in long-termdecline in Britain, compared with therise of co ee and energy drinks.Typhoo collapsed intoadministration last week after  ndingno way of paying back tens of millionsof pounds in debts.London-listed Supreme, best knownfor e-cigarette brands 88Vape andElar and for distributing batteries,rescued the company in a £10.2m deal.Typhoo to switchto ice tea as hotdrink sales stutterPat Gelsinger, the chief executive ofIntel, has been forced to retire afterfailing to return the Americanmicrochip company to the cuttingedge, despite promises of billions fromthe Biden administration.Intel secured almost $8bn (£6.3bn)from the US Commerce Departmentunder Joe Biden’s Chips Act. But thecompany has struggled to stem lossesafter ceding leadership in productionof the most advanced processors toTaiwan’s TSMC.The company is expected to post its rst annual loss since 1983, when USchipmakers were hammered bycheaper Japanese competitors.Mr Gelsinger was given the option ofretiring or being removed at a boardmeeting last week.1ntel chief forcedout after Biden’sbillions fail to deliverRead Questor’s rules of investmentbefore you follow our tips:telegraph.co.uk/go/questorrulesBaby products brand Mothercare hasslipped to a loss for the past half-yearafter another signi cant slump in sales.The retailer said it was knocked bytough trading in the Middle East ande orts by franchise partners to workthrough old inventory.Mothercare, which sells its rangesthrough retail giant Boots in the UKand has franchised stores in the rest ofthe world, has been working on atransformation plan. It reported thatturnover dropped by 27.5pc to £21mfor the 26 weeks to Sept 28, comparedwith the same period last year.It said this followed a 12pc fall inglobal retail sales by franchisepartners. It posted a £1.8m pre-tax lossfor the half-year, swinging from a£1.7m pre-tax pro t a year earlier.Mothercare swingsto loss after MiddleEast sales slumpThe Daily Telegraph Tuesday 3 December 2024 *** 25Court & SocialLast week, writes Julian Pottage,Bridge Correspondent, the highestscores each day in the EnglishBridge Union’s open 12-board onlinematchpoint duplicate pairs gameswere as follows:Monday72.45% scored by Jakub Mitke andJackie Davies in Game 1 (2pm).Tuesday80.51% scored by LesleyBlanchard and Stuart Fletcher inGame 3 (7.30pm).Wednesday76.49% scored by the Israeli pairIlia Goldfarb and Gai Maman inGame 3.Thursday71.05% scored by Allison Green andJeremy Crouch in Game 2 (3.30pm).Friday72.95% scored by Neil Thomasand Roger Hopton in Game 2.Saturday68.27% scored by John Cox andAlex Hannon in Game 2.Sunday71.88% scored by Flo Baldwin andan Advanced BBO Bot in Game 3.Bridge newsONE HUNDRED YEARS AGOLONDON, WEDNESDAY DECEMBER 3, 1924ASSEMBLING OF THENEW PARLIAMENT.THE SPEAKER’SRE-ELECTIONEXCHANGES OFCOURTESY.The new Parliament assembled yesterday, and theHouse of Commons elected its Speaker. That is thefirst duty of every new House – to choose a spokesman who may speak in its name to the Crown, anda chairman who shall be its obedient servant on alloccasions, and its stern master if and when required.Mr. Whitley – for the fourth time in four years– went through the familiar ceremony of election with complete self-possession, listeningmodestly from a seat below the gangwaywhile his praise was spoken by mover andseconder, “submitting himself humbly to thewill of members,” if their choice should fall onhim, assuring the House, as he stood on thesteps of the Chair before taking his seat, of hisprofound sense of the honour done to him,and his complete devotion to its service, andthen receiving the congratulations of theleaders of the respective parties. It is alwaysa pleasing ceremony, especially when, as yesterday, there is no discordant note in thewhole proceedings, no breath of dissent, noquerulous echo of past disappointment, aswhen the late Mr. Ginnell used to torture andlacerate himself while striving vainly towound Mr. Lowther. Yesterday all wentblithely as at a wedding; no stranger couldhave guessed that the serried ranks, whichconfronted one another so good-humouredly,were but lately at each other’s throats.Serried ranks! The Unionists filled all their half ofthe House, and overflowed into the first twobenches below the gangway opposite. TheLabour-Socialists made a solid phalanx behindtheir leaders, who turned up in strengths andfilled the Front Opposition Bench. As for the Liberals – well, it was a sight almost for tears to thosewho remembered their past glories, and eventheir past defeats, to see the Liberal remnantshrunken to the two top benches below the gangway, which for many a long year were the abodeof the Irish Nationalists.There was a notable hesitancy to take Mr.Redmond’s old place. Mr. Lloyd George, slipping in almost unnoticed, took a seat a yardor two down the bench, forbearing to occupythe leader’s place till the Liberal meeting,which was due later, had given him the otherhalf of the leadership mantle. But let it not bethought that the place of honour was leftunfilled! No; Captain Wedgwood Benn tookit, to the general amusement, and – “thinkingof the Old ’Un” – revolved the epigrams andsarcasms with which a little later he was toassail Mr. Lloyd George. Mr. Kenworthyseized the corner seat just below, from whichMr. John Dillon used to radiate sweet melancholy. And in the bench below sat Mr. T. P.O’Connor, showing to what small unitarycompass a party can dwindle and yet becheerful in extreme adversity.The Prime Minister drew a great cheer from hissupporters when he came in, and Mr. MacDonald,entering a few moments later, was also warmlygreeted by his friends. The others, great andsmall, settled down in their places amid a starling-like babble of talk and laughter. When theHouse was set for the play to begin one wellknown figure with folded arms was seen on theTreasury Bench. It was Mr. Churchill in a characteristic pose, contemplative, pleased – there!All round were new faces. Mr. Whitley gavetheir actual number – 216. Yesterday theywere still characterless and unknown. TheSpeaker-Elect contrived incidentally to givethem a wise word of counsel. “The quality oflistening,” he said, “is as important, at first, atany rate, as that of speech.”PEARLS NICELYGRADED.The speeches of mover and seconder were verygood of their kind – Mr. Lloyd George indeed,kindly said that he had never heard better. LordHenry Cavendish-Bentinck, who proposed Mr.Whitley’s re-election, has never, though he is anold member of the House, wholly conquered hisnatural diffidence. Yesterday he was nervous, butdetermined, and went straight at all his fences –over or through – landing safely each time. He gavea good character sketch of the perfect Speaker ingeneral and of Mr. Whitley in particular, and, as anold hunting man, chose his metaphors from theworld of horseflesh. Recalling how Mr. Manners-Sutton, a Speaker of nearly a century ago, hadbeen described as “riding the House with a snafflerather than a curb,” Lord Henry said that Mr. Whitley also had “a firm seat, and hands that were lighton the bridle.” Some day, perhaps, members willtake their metaphors from the motor world, but forthe present the horse still holds its own.Mr. Baldwin likened the Speaker to “thelinch-pin of the chariot.” Mr. T. P. O’Connor,claiming an Irishman’s right to be more emotionally rhetorical, likened him to “the corner-stone of the great arch” of Parliament,which he pictured in an expansive sentenceas the bridge between Great Britain, theDominions, and the world.Mr. Smillie, who scarcely broke silence in the lastParliament, showed that he possessed an unsuspected – at any rate by strangers – sense ofhumour. He spoke of himself as “a late apprentice”to the House, so late an entrant, indeed, that hefeared he would never become a master-craftsman. But Mr. Baldwin genially observed as to thisthat his success proved the virtue of the “dilution”which had allowed him to become an apprenticeat his time of life – a thrust which amused the tradeunionists facing him.There is no need to refer at greater length tothe many eulogies that were uttered. Enoughto say that the pearls were nicely graded. Mr.Smillie praised Mr. Whitley’s “immense anduntiring kindness”; others spoke of his courage and wisdom; Mr. MacDonald cheerfullywelcomed in him the champion of Parliament as the home of free discussion and theupholder of the rights of minorities. Mr.Lloyd George, after a side-glance at the far-offdays when he and Mr. Whitley learnt the rulesof the House by “circumnavigating” many ofthem, spoke wistfully of the special protection needed by small parties, while majoritiescould always look after themselves.It was all a very pleasant interchange of dignifiedcourtesy, with the Speaker-Elect the most dignified and courteous member of the party, pleased,of course – as who could fail to be pleased? – tohear all these expressions of obviously sincereesteem, and himself in turn desirous to assurethe House that his supreme endeavour would beto do its will and maintain the high traditions ofthe Speakership and of the House of Commons.An agreeable opening day for the new House ofCommons, and, in sparkling contrast with themire and gloom and general wretchedness of theday without.BUCKINGHAM PALACEDecember 2ndThe Viscount Hood (Lord inWaiting) was present at LondonStansted Airport this afternoonupon the Arrival of The Amir ofthe State of Qatar and SheikhaJawaher Bint Hamad Bin SuhaimAl-Thani and welcomed TheirHighnesses on behalf of The King.ST JAMES’S PALACEDecember 2ndThe Duchess of Edinburgh, Patron,Chartered Management Institute,this afternoon attended a jointChartered Management Instituteand Marks and Spencer Group plcIndustry Leaders Reception atWaterside House, 35 North WharfRoad, London W2.KENSINGTON PALACEDecember 2ndThe Duke of Gloucester, GrandPrior, the Most Venerable Order ofthe Hospital of St John of Jerusalem,this afternoon received ProfessorMark Compton (Lord Prior).For more details about the Royalfamily visit www.royal.ukToday’s birthdaysMr Nicholas Hood, Chairman,Wessex Water, 1989-99, is 89;Prof David Phillips, President,Royal Society of Chemistry,2010-12, 85; Mr Mike Gibson,former Ireland and Lions rugbyinternational, 82; Mr RalphMcTell, singer and songwriter,80; Mr Paul Nicholas, actor andsinger, 80; Mr Craig Raine,poet, 80; Mr Ozzie Osbourne,singer and songwriter, 76; LordGlennie, a former Senator of theCollege of Justice in Scotland,74; Mr Franz Klammer, formerchampion Alpine ski racer, 71;Lord Prior of Brampton,former chairman of NHSEngland, 70; Lord Clark, aSenator of the College of Justicein Scotland, 69; Miss JulianneMoore, actress, 64; Mr PaulEastwood, former Great Britainrugby league player, 59;Ms Sharon Graham, GeneralSecretary, Unite the Union, 56;Mr Mark Boucher, formerSouth Africa cricketer, nowcoach, 48; the Earl of Arundeland Surrey, 37; Ms PhoebePaterson Pine, archer;Paralympic gold medallistwomen’s individual compound,Tokyo 2020, 27.Today is the anniversary of thefirst human-to-human hearttransplant, carried out byChristiaan Barnard in CapeTown, in 1967.CourtCircularThe Cavalry ColonelsThe annual dinner of the CavalryColonels was held last night at theCavalry and Guards Club.Lieutenant General Sir EdwardSmyth-Osbourne, Chairman,presided.Service dinnerJudge Hassall retired as a CircuitJudge on November 25 2024.Penelope Ireland has beenappointed a Circuit Judgedeployed to the Northern Circuit,based at Manchester Civil JusticeCentre, with effect from December2 2024. She will be known as HerHonour Judge Ireland.Legal newsTuesday, December 3Commons: Oral questions:Treasury. Ten Minute Rule Motion:Elections (ProportionalRepresentation). Legislation:National Insurance Contributions(Secondary Class 1 Contributions)Bill; Second Reading.Adjournment: International Day ofPersons with Disabilities.Westminster Hall: Debates:Domestic production of criticalminerals; Leisure services in WestLancashire; Family farming inDevon; Freight crime; Statutoryframework for home-to-schooltransport for children with SEND.Lords: Oral questions:Appointment of members for theParliamentary Assembly of theCouncil of Europe; Ensuringproposed planning reformscontribute to the delivery of netzero carbon emissions; Securingadditional illegal migrant returnsagreements with foreigngovernments; Great British EnergyBill – committee stage (day 1).Wednesday, December 4Commons: Oral questions:Scotland Office. At noon,questions to the Prime Minister.Ten Minute Rule Motion: (detailsto be provided). Opposition DayDebate: Debate on a Motion in theName of the Official Opposition,subject to be announced.Adjournment: End of RadioTeleswitch Service in rural areas.Westminster Hall: Debates:Biosecurity; Governmentsupport for the hospitality sectorin Eastleigh; Sexual andgender-based violence againstPalestinians; Support for andidentification of the children ofprisoners; Future of farming.Lords: Oral questions:Encouraging entrepreneurs tostart up businesses in the UnitedKingdom; Work by theDepartment of Education and theForeign Development andCommonwealth Office topromote the attractiveness ofhigher education sectorinternationally; Labour PartyManifesto pledge to givedecision-making over theallocation of the SharedProsperity Fund to democraticrepresentatives of in Wales.Legislation: Football GovernanceBill – committee stage (day 3).Short debate: Progress towardsuniversal provision of fractureliaison services in Englandby 2030.Week in WestminsterREES.—Richard John ‘Dick’.Passed away peacefully on Monday25th November at home, aged 83 years,surrounded by his loving family. YT.Online Ref: W0031187WATSON.—Ralph Michael George‘Michael’. Influential dental journalistand commentator, former dentalpractitioner and officer in the RADC,who passed away at his North Essexhome on 17th November 2024, aged 82,after a short illness. Much loved husbandto Jackie, father to Stephen, Tim andCharles and grandfather to Casperand Daisy. Awarded the OBE in 2002for services to dentistry, Michael willbe much missed by his friends, widerfamily and former colleagues for hisgenerosity, humour and penetratingintelligence. The Funeral will be heldat 12 noon on Thursday 19th Decemberat St Mary’s Church, Lawford followedby a Committal at Weeley Crematoriumat 1.15 p.m. Family flowers only please.Donations, if desired, to the RNLI andSt Helena Hospice, c/o Geo PaskellFuneral Services, 15 High Street,Manningtree, Essex, CO11 1AG.All enquiries to Geo Paskell.Tel: 01206 396709.Online Ref: W0031168MORANT.—There will be a MemorialService for Algie Morant on Thursday23rd January at St Saviour’s,Brockenhurst at 2.30 p.m. This will befollowed by a reception in the churchhall. Enquiries.Tel: 01263 713113.Online Ref: W0031164PETER WILLIAMS IS 50!Wishing you a very happy birthday.Lots of love and best wishes from Kathy,Beau, Richard and Rose xxxxOnline Ref: W0031171HAPPY BIRTHDAY ROSIE WATERS!!Hoping you have a magical day. LoveAmber and Jake xxOnline Ref: W0031188DeathsLegal NoticesMemorial servicesIn memoriamText for the dayPersonalGeneral personalMessagesBirthdaysMr H. Crichton-Stuart andMiss C. MonroThe engagement is announcedbetween Hugh, second son of thelate Lord James Crichton-Stuartand of Mrs Peter KnatchbullHugessen, of Ropley, Hampshire,and Christine, only daughter ofMr George Melville Monro andMrs Dawn Monro, of Cape Town,South Africa.Online ref: W0031065Forthcomingmarriage26 *** Tuesday 3 December 2024 The Daily TelegraphT ERRY GRIFFITHS, who has died fromcomplications of dementia aged 77,was the rank outsider who in 1979pulled off one of the biggest shocks insnooker history when he snatched theWorld Championship from under the noseof Dennis Taylor; he went on to become oneof only 11 players to win all of the majortriple-crown events.The Welsh-born Griffiths, a formerpostman and bus conductor, was a qualifierwhen he beat Alex “Hurricane” Higgins13-12 in a thrilling quarter-final at theSheffield Crucible.In the semi-finals, another close-foughtmatch ended with him beating EddieCharlton by 19-17. “I’m in the final now, youknow,” the astonished and disbelievingvictor told the BBC’s David Vine, beforeclinching the title with a 24-16 victory overTaylor. It was only his second professionaltournament.The dapper Griffiths went on to becomeone of snooker’s most recognisable playersin the 1980s and early 1990s, defeatingHiggins 9-5 at the Wembley ConferenceCentre to win the Masters in 1980 anddefeating Higgins again, 16-15, at Preston totake the UK Championship (and TripleCrown) in 1982.Renowned for his elegant, slow andmethodical play, Griffiths reached at leastthe quarter-finals of the WorldChampionship for nine consecutive yearsbetween 1984 and 1992, reaching the finalfor a second time in 1988 when he lost 18-11to Steve Davis.A modest, cheerful man, Griffiths mightnot have generated the sort of excitementthat surrounded more flamboyant rivalssuch as Higgins or Jimmy “Whirlwind”White. Nor did he keep the tabloids busywith his antics away from the green baize. Adevoted family man, he never likedspending too much time away from home inLlanelli, and fellow players had countlessstories of his small kindnesses andunassuming decency.Joe Johnson has recalled how when hecame from 12-9 down to beat Griffiths 13-12in the quarter-finals of the 1986 WorldChampionship, Griffiths followed him intohis dressing room to wish him good luckand advise him about handling the attentionand media should he win the title.In later years Griffiths, a self-confessed“amateur psychologist”, became a BBCsnooker commentator and a much soughtafter coach, inspiring the likes of StephenHendry and fellow Welshman MarkWilliams with his down-to-earth wisdom.The youngest of three children of a metalworker, Terence Martin Griffiths was bornin Llanelli on October 16 1947. He won aplace at the local grammar school but,missing his childhood friends who went tothe nearby secondary modern, he playedtruant to such a degree that he was expelledfrom the grammar school and was able tojoin his friends.Griffiths began playing snooker at a localclub when he was 14 and became theyoungest winner of the Llanelli and Districtsnooker championship aged 16. Afterleaving school, he worked briefly in a coalmine before becoming a bus conductor, ajob which gave him more time to practise.He later worked as a postman and then asan insurance salesman.After winning several amateur titles,including the Welsh AmateurChampionship in 1975 and English AmateurChampionships in 1977 and 1978, Griffithsturned professional aged 30 in June 1978,inspired by his fellow Welshman RayReardon, who won six world titles between1970 and 1978.His professional career startedunpromisingly in the UK Championshipthat year when in the qualifying rounds heled Rex Williams 8-2 but was beaten 9-8.Then came the 1979 World Championship– and fame.Although he won the Triple Crown andremained at the top level in the sport formany years, some commentators arguedthat Griffiths’s efforts to get the better of hisrival Steve Davis led him to make changes inhis technique which cost him his naturalflair. He announced his retirement fromprofessional snooker in 1996 to become theWorld Professional Billiards and SnookerAssociation’s director of coaching.Though he was never comfortable withcelebrity (a fact that made him invaluable asa coach in helping other players to deal withit), Griffiths merited a small footnote in thehistory of pop when he was one of the stableof players who sang with Chas ’n’ Dave ontheir 1986 single Snooker Loopy, whichreached No 6 in the charts and on whichGriffiths could be heard mocking his ownwell-coiffed appearance with the line “I’llbuy another eight hairbrushes for mebarnet”.Griffiths was appointed OBE in 2007.In 1969 he married Annette, who surviveshim with their two sons, Wayne, a snookercoach, and Darren, a PGA golf professional.Terry Griffiths, born October 16 1947, diedDecember 1 2024Terry GriffithsDapper Welsh snooker player who sensationally took the World Championship at the Crucible in 1979OLAV THON, who hasdied aged 101, was aNorwegian tycoonand outdoorsman who builtNorway’s largest propertycompany, including theThon hotel chain, beforebequeathing his vast fortuneto a charitable foundation.His penchant for redwoollen hats, colourful (butwell-worn) tweed suits,neckerchiefs and bow ties,was distinctly unNorwegian. His dress was arare hint at extravagance; hewas known for livingfrugally. He was, he said,proud of his tax bill, andrefused to become a taxexile. In the early 2010s,however, he publiclysupported the populistanti-tax, anti-immigrationProgress Party.He bought his firstproperty, an apartmentbuilding, in 1950. Today theOlav Thon Gruppen ownssome 500 commercialproperties, 80 hotels and 90shopping centres.In 1974 he acquired hisfirst hotel, the celebratedHotel Bristol in Oslo, whichhad hosted jazz residenciesby Josephine Baker. In 1939it had also been the scene ofthe German mathematicianHans Ferdinand Mayer’sinfamous “Oslo Report”, anintelligence leak detailingNazi munitions that hetapped up on the hoteltypewriter and sent to theBritish Embassy in Oslo inthe guise of two letters.In 2013 the Forbes rich listestimated Thon’s net worthat some $6 billion. Thefollowing year, Thon, whohad no children, put hiscompany interests into acharitable foundation with afocus on medical researchand education.Olav Thon was born onJune 29 1923 in the village ofÅl in Hallingdal valley ineastern Norway. His parents,Sevat and Margit, weresuccessful farmers withbusiness interests inhydroelectric schemes.Eight-year-old Olav boughtChristmas cards in bulkfrom mail-order cataloguesbefore selling them on toneighbours at a profit.In his late teens, he begantrading in fur and leather,which earned him thenickname “The Fox”. He waslater criticised for sellingfurs to the Germans duringthe Nazi occupation ofNorway, although hecountered that his staffsimply sold to anyone whocame through the shop door.His empire grewconsistently during thepost-war years, with a minorblip in 1953 when he wasarrested for customs fraud.He received a fine and asuspended sentence. Heopened his first restaurantin the mid-1960s.His time away frombusiness was largely spentoutdoors. “Olav walked,hiked or skied every day. Heand his partner practicallylived in their tent, swam inthe lakes, ate the trout theyfished,” said his friend, thepolar explorer Erling Kagge.“His advice to everyone waskeep your pleasures simple.”Thon’s foundation hasconstructed and restoredmore than 90 mountain andforest cabins for theNorwegian TrekkingAssociation. He could besharp about what he saw asNorway’s spoilt oil-richyouth: “You can’t feed a catwith cream and food in thekitchen and expect him togo catch mice,” he remarked.As a philanthropist andenvironmentalist he was notwithout his critics. “He’sbuilt a picture of himself as aguy who gives away all hismoney and walks in themountains,” observed DagEllingsen, author of The Fox:Olav Thon and His Methods.“But on the other hand, he’sbeen building malls allaround Norway andSweden, making peopledrive long distances to buythings.”Thon’s honours includeda doctorate from theKarlstad University inSweden and membership ofthe Norwegian TrekkingAssociation.Thon’s first wife IngerJohanne Gjaerum died in2018. Thon had been livingwith his partner, SisselBerdal Haga, a former judge,for many years, but had notdivorced his wife becauseshe had been suffering fromAlzheimer’s disease.Thon and Sissel, whosurvives him, married in theHotel Bristol in 2019. Thonwore his trademark red hatduring the service, eventhough it was midsummer.The couple spent theirhoneymoon camping.Olav Thon, born June 291923, died November 162024Olav ThonColourful Norwegian propertytycoon devoted to good causesJOHN CLEARE, who has died aged 88,was regarded as the outstanding Britishmountain photographer of the post-warera; he also filmed live climbing sequencesfor the BBC, as well as Clint Eastwooddangling from a rope on the Eiger NorthFace.In 1965 the BBC was asked to help theSwiss film an ascent of the Matterhorn tomark the 100th anniversary of its first ascentby Edward Whymper. Led by a formerBattle of Britain fighter pilot, the coolheaded Alan Chivers, Cleare was one of twocameramen given the job of filming theclimbers for six 25-minute transmissionslots, a feat that required him to film, thenleapfrog ahead of the climbers and positionhimself higher up the mountain for thenext shot.Each portable radio-camera was cabled toa large metal pack on his back, with morecables linked to another heavy transmissionbox carried by a second man, and onwardsto an aerial. This had to be carried on top ofthe usual climbing gear of rope and ice axe.The weight was “crucifying”, Cleare said.Afterwards Chivers radioed Cleare tostand down. “ ‘We’re off you now, John.Thank you, my boy.’ I let the camera fall onits strap and collapsed against the rope,”Cleare recalled. “With a rumble, the bigboulder on which I had been proppedtumbled away to go plunging down thenorth face. Rusty [Baillie, his climbingpartner] pulled me back into balance and asherpa put a mug of fresh-brewed tea intomy hand. It was done.”Two years later the team reunited for theBBC’s landmark live production, filmingclimbers on the Old Man of Hoy, a 450ft seastack off Orkney. The broadcast required16 tons of equipment to be ferried 450 milesfrom the Firth of Clyde to Hoy in Armylanding craft; a platoon of Scots Guardsfresh from the Borneo jungle was alsoloaned. Cleare found himself once againbehind a radio camera, and recorded theclimbers moving up through the south face.About 15 million people tuned in to theweekend broadcast.In 1971 Cleare was asked to join aninternational attempt on the still-virginsouth-west face of Everest as climbingcameraman. After surviving tragedy and a10-day storm, the attempt failed, anddestroyed Cleare’s marriage in the process.In 1974 he was recruited by ClintEastwood as one of his two climbingcameramen for his movie The EigerSanction. Bravely, Eastwood insisted on theclimbing scenes being filmed on the actualface – then still a formidable proposition forprofessional climbers, let alone actors.John Silvey Cleare was born in London onMay 2 1936, the only child of an RAFengineer. During the war he and his motherwere evacuated to Snowdonia, where he fellin love with the mountains. He waseducated at Wycliffe College inGloucestershire, where his housemasterwas the inspirational AA “Bertie”Robertson, an international athlete,mountaineer and caver.Young John’s first climb was upSnowdonia’s Tryfan (918m). Robertson thentook the boys “to the Mendips, to seriouscaves with tight squeezes, ladder pitches andunderground rivers, which for a 12-year-oldwere the ultimate adventures,” Clearerecalled. He also discovered Whymper’sScrambles Amongst the Alps, the seminal bookof the Matterhorn’s first ascent.He was determined to enjoy his NationalService, gaining a commission with theRoyal Artillery, which he counted as “animportant part of my education”, not leastbecause he discovered photography. Hethen spent three years at the GuildfordSchool of Photography, from where he washeadhunted for Jocelyn Stevens’s glossilyrevamped fashion magazine Queen. A yearlater he was headhunted to photograph abook of British gardens published in 1962.It was not long before he managed tocombine his profession with his passion.Rock Climbers in Action in Snowdonia, acollaboration with Tony Smythe, waspublished in 1966. Reissued in 2016, itremains a classic. In all, his photographswould illustrate 17 books and many otherprivate commissions. His Mountain Cameralibrary, set up with the help of the BBC tosell his images worldwide, was renowned.Over the ensuing decades he travelledwidely, shooting from the Karakoram toKinabalu, Patagonia to Peru. He spent twomonths in East Africa for the BBC,journeyed to the source of the Amazon,made the first camel crossing of Australia’sGreat Victoria Desert, narrowly escapedarrest in the Congo and once spent Easterwith the Sami people in Lapland.As a climber he led three expeditions tomajor Himalayan peaks, including Nepal’sHimal Chuli (7,800m), his highest summit,and in his fifties took to ski mountaineering,leading ski expeditions up Kedar Dome(6,832m) in India and Muztagh Ata (7,546m)in Chinese Xinjiang. In his eighties hebecame fan of e-bikes.A great raconteur, Cleare was known forhis generosity and kindness.His first marriage ended in divorce. He issurvived by his second wife, whom he metin 1974, and a daughter from his first.John Cleare, born May 2 1936, diedOctober 6 2024John CleareAudacious mountain photographer who scaled the Matterhorn to film a live ascent for the BBCC ESARE BONIZZI, who has diedaged 78, was an Italian Capuchin friaralso known as “Fratello Metallo”, or“Brother Metal”, the name under which heperformed (in his habit) as a heavy metalsinger.Bonizzi discovered the joys of rocking in1990. He was then undertaking his ministryamong the tram drivers of Milan and at thecity’s Catholic University. An acquaintanceinvited him to a concert by Metallica, theAmerican heavy metal band.His brown garb and white beard ledsomeone in the queue to loudly questionhis presence. Everyone else rounded on thetroublemaker.“I’ve always understood evangelisationas something of the street, of the people,”said Bonizzi. “I started having contacts withthese young people. I was able to talk aboutJesus, I told them life is marvellous, and thatalcohol is dangerous.”In exchange, he became a metalhead.“Metal is the most energetic, vital, deep andtrue musical language that I know,” he said.Beginning with Come fiamma (“Likeflame”, 1990), he started to record albumsas an aid to devotion. He called himself a“preacher-singer”.The LPs were in many styles, from NewAge to classical. “I used to make music forgrandmothers,” he later quipped. Steadily,however, he moved to a “more beautiful,stronger but soft, real metal.”He put together a band, overcomingtheir perhaps understandable doubts.“Five minutes after having met BrotherCesare,” said lead guitarist Cesare Zanotti, “Idecided to go ahead… because he managesto convey so much energy that even othermusicians and youngsters often don’tmanage to express.”Bonizzi did not shy from the realities oflife in his lyrics, singing in rasping tonesabout God and faith, but also sex, drugs andalcohol (in the form of Bacchus, the god ofwine). He would play up to 150 gigs a year,cutting a distinctive figure as he twirled thecords around his waist while he bellowed.He was invited several times to performat Gods of Metal, Italy’s leading heavy rockfestival. In 2009, after releasing his 16th andheaviest album to date, Misteri, reworkingsof songs by women from southern Italywho sang about the Virgin, he appearedas the festival’s opening act, warming upthe audience for Judas Priest. He namedhis own favourite band as Megadeth andbecame a spiritual adviser to its founder,Dave Mustaine.Bonizzi had his critics. He maintainedthat the satanic imagery favoured by metalbands was more about marketing thangenuine diabolic belief and claimed only“one or two” used drugs. He co-opted thenumber 667, in honour of 666, “the numberof the Beast” associated with many metalbands.In 2009, however, controversy came toa head over an LP cover which showed himmaking a gesture he said was a symbol ofthe Trinity but which many associated withSatan. He had previously been reprimandedby a Superior General for appearing ontelevision as a representative of his orderand had agreed not to do so again. Hedecided to hang up his microphone, sayinghe was uncomfortable with his face beingused so prominently in the sale of his music.“The Devil has distanced me from mymanagers, making me run the risk ofbreaking up with the band and with myfriars,” he said. “He lifted me up to become acelebrity; now I want to kill him.”The youngest of 10 children, CesareBonizzi was born at Offanengo, nearCremona in Lombardy, on April 5 1946.When young, he discovered the local dancehall and by the age of 13 he was performingon stage.After leaving school he did militaryservice and worked as a labourer, welder,barber and sales representative for Bulova,the watchmaker. He also volunteered forTelefono Amico Italia, a charitable listeningservice for those in crisis.Bonizzi displayed a gift for empathy inthis role but became disgusted with himselfafter making big sales to two people whowere grieving. He took himself to a Hinduashram, then the Cistercian abbey of Lérins,the silence of which he found chilling,before turning up in 1975 at a Capuchinfriary in Milan. Despite his long hair andripped jeans they took him in: “Finally, Iwas home.” He subsequently did missionarywork in Ivory Coast and in 1983 wasordained a priest.“I don’t play to draw people closer toChrist, to the church or to religion,” Bonizzireflected. “I do it to convert people to life,to understand life, to grab hold of life, tosavour life, to experience life and enjoy it.”Cesare Bonizzi, born April 5 1946, diedNovember 19 2024Cesare BonizziCapuchin friar known as ‘Brother Metal’ whose heavy rock group was accused of ‘satanic’ imageryObituariesDUNCAN RABAN/POPPERFOTO VIA GETTY IMAGESALESSANDRO GAROFALO/REUTERSJOHNCLEAREMOUNTAINCAMERAGriffiths (circa 1985): he was renowned for his elegant, methodical play and personal decencyCleare as climbingcameraman onEverest in 1971,during an attemptwhich was fraughtwith setbacksBonizzi, left,singing with asound system on awheelbarrow: hemaintained that‘metal is the mostenergetic, vital,deep and truemusical language Iknow’Thon in his trademark red hatThe Daily Telegraph Tuesday 3 December 2024 *** 27Radio choicePrecious BloodRadio 4, 2.15pmPhilip Palmer’s terrificWelsh noir thriller PreciousBlood plays out over threeafternoons (until Thursday)this week – a tangledtale about a tight-knitcommunity with a darksecret at its heart. Furtherafield, if you’ve wonderedwhy French wine is goingthrough a rough patch atthe moment, CrossingContinents (Radio 4, 9pm)covers the story in depth,tracing issues fromagriculture to politics;also from that side of theChannel, In the Studio(World Service, 1.30pm/11.30pm) meets the superbcraftspeople restoringNotre-Dame Cathedralin Paris, ahead of itsreopening next week.Gerard O’DonovanRADIO 1FM 97.6-99.8MHZ6.58am Newsbeat 7.00 Radio 1Breakfast with Greg James 10.30Newsbeat 10.33 Rickie, Melvin andCharlie 12.00 Radio 1’s Christmas LiveLounge 12.45pm Newsbeat 1.00Matt and Jerry 3.30 Newsbeat 3.33Going Home with Vick, Katie andJamie on Radio 1 5.45 Newsbeat 6.00Radio 1’s New Music Show with JackSaunders 8.00 Radio 1’s FutureArtists with Sian Eleri 10.00 Radio 1’sPower Down Playlist with Sian Eleri11.00 Martha 1.00am Radio 1’s Drum& Bass Mix 1.30 Radio 1’s Drum &Bass Mix 2.00 Radio 1 Anthems 4.00Radio 1 Pop Anthems 5.00 - 6.58amRadio 1 Early BreakfastRADIO 2FM 88-90.2MHZ6.30am The Zoe Ball Breakfast Show9.30 Vernon Kay 12.00 Tina Daheley.Tina Daheley sits in for Jeremy Vine2.00pm Scott Mills. 6.00amShort Stories by Elizabeth BowenTelevision reviews What to watchTelevision & RadioHistory programmes can bebrilliant if you get them right.The Rest is History, a podcastpresented by Dominic Sandbrook andTom Holland, gets 11million downloadsa month. A generation of childrencan happily reel off facts about thebathroom habits of the Romans orHenry VIII’s diet plan thanks toHorrible Histories. But the BBC Artsdepartment is at a bit of a loss when itcomes to harnessing this interest, asdemonstrated by Renaissance: TheBlood and the Beauty (BBC Two).It is part-documentary, parthistorical re-enactment. For the latter,the producers clearly wish they couldgive us a new Wolf Hall, but theycan’t hope to afford it. Instead, they’vespent the entire budget on hiringCharles Dance to play Michelangelo.The programme is billed as the storyof “three men who will changeWestern art forever” yet the othertwo, Leonardo da Vinci and Raphael,are played by actors you don’trecognise and are not given a singleline of dialogue.Dance plays the elderlyMichelangelo looking back at his life.He’s great, bringing all the gravitasand wisdom you would expect,as he details his rivalries – first withLeonardo, who prompted strongfeelings of professional jealousy,and later with Raphael, the talentedyoung upstart. Aside from his straightto-camera monologues, though, therecreations are cheap and offer zeroin the way of storytelling.The documentary side of thingsconsists of a dozen or so talking heads– historians, art experts and artists –sharing their thoughts. The work ofMichelangelo and Leonardo (Raphaelgets much less of a look-in) has to beconsidered in its historical context,but this doesn’t quite mesh with thelikes of Antony Gormley and AlisonLapper responding emotionally andeloquently to the power of the art.Lapper, who lost her son, Parys, whenhe was 19, speaks movingly aboutMichelangelo’s Pietà: “It’s her son…in that quiet moment is she somehowsaying to God, ‘This is my everythingand I’m giving him to you?’”We could have a great documentaryabout the personal rivalries, ora serious appreciation of the artists’work, but this is neither one thingnor the other. And the contributionsfrom experts is variable, including“historian of sexuality” Kate Lister,who declares: “Michelangelo is gay.I’ll just say it,” and a film-makerwho opens proceedings by saying:“Florence is popping,” as if theprogramme is desperate to get downwith the kids. Anita SinghWhy did the BBC blow thewhole budget on one actor?In this box-set era, short stories onscreen are an endangered species.Why try and make your point in 30minutes when streaming offers endlessstretches of time to fill? So you have togive credit to Channel 4 for slippingthe unsettling Person of Interest intoits schedules. Not part of any series orstrand, Aysha Rafaele’s film is a jitteryexercise in late-night paranoia – asmuch for viewers as subject matter.With no time to lose we were straightinto the story of on-the-edge taxi driverShakil (Asim Chaudhry), anxiety etchedon his features as he endured the worstthat night shifts, in all their messydrunken chaos, could throw at him.Was he simply sleep deprived or wasthere more to his increasingly erraticand intolerant behaviour?Given the short running time,it wasn’t long before we had an answer.Shakil was grappling with a grief thathad him experiencing curious visions(why was Dot from Line of Duty in theback of his cab?) and steered him intoparanoia and appointments with anapparently empathetic therapist(Julia Davis).There was a lot to unpack in ashort time here and, in an impressiveperformance that punched way abovethe weight of the material, Chaudhrymade you feel the heavy burden thatShakil was carrying. His world hadturned as dark as the night he onlyseemed to exist in, his relationship asnear to breaking point as his sanity.But by over-egging the bleak pit thatShakil had fallen into, Person of Intereststretched plausibility to its limits. Everyfare that Shakil picked up seemeddredged up from the sewer levelsof humanity, at best unsympathetic,at worst violent. The misery washeaped on with a trowel.The end result being that, despiteChaudhry’s best efforts, the dramabuckled under the strain of trying tosqueeze a big story into a tiny space.Rather than feeling like a self-containedshort film, it seemed like a pilot fora six-part series where Shakil’s storywould get the treatment it deserved.Sometimes less is indeed more. But thisreally needed extra time and space todrive home its impact. Keith WatsonCharles Dance stars as an older Michelangelo in BBC Two’s docu-dramaHelen Skelton and JulesHudson are back liveat Cannon Hall Farm nearBarnsley in South Yorkshireto report on events at Roband Dave Nicholson’s126-acre holding as thebrothers care for theirlivestock and maintainthe year-round attraction.In tonight’s episode, theNicholsons visit a countyshow and we meet a farmerwith an innovative way tomilk his livestock, whileRohin Aojula (from Channel5 stablemate The YorkshireVet) takes his father to meetthe holiest of cows. Theseries is being shownnightly until Friday andin later episodes theNicholsons learn howFinnish farmers thrive inone of the world’s harshestclimates and we meeta producer of Cheshirecheese using traditionalmethods, while JB Gilldiscovers one of London’smost surprising spots,where city life andwinemaking meet. It’sperfect timing for the returnof this magazine series asthe farming communityis back in the news afterChancellor Rachel Reeves’sfirst Budget includedinheritance measures thatdirectly affect them. Thedebate highlighted just howimportant the UK’s farmers– most of them, like theNicholsons, toiling on familyfarms of under 200 acres –are to the nation’s foodsecurity. Veronica LeeWINTER ON THE FARMChannel 5, 8pmHelen Skelton catches up with Cannon Hall Farm owners Rob (left) and Dave NicholsonCOMEDYCheatersBBC One, from 9.45pm;NI, from 11.10pm; Wales,from 11.40pmThree more bite-sizeepisodes of the quirkyromcom about infidelitybring the second series toa close. Some storylinesabout on-off lovers Fola(Susan Wokoma), Josh(Joshua McGuire), Esther(Callie Cooke) and Zack (JackFox) get cheerfully resolved.DOCUMENTARYAn Apocalyptic OrderBBC Four, from 10pmThis gripping four-partFrench documentary seriesdelves into the Order ofthe Solar Temple, a cultbelieving in occult theoriesdeveloped by two “gurus”.Police officers andjournalists recount howdozens of corpses werediscovered in Switzerlandin 1994; more bodies wereat other locations in Franceand Canada.DRAMAStar Wars: Skeleton CrewDisney+The latest Star Wars spin-offhas arrived – and this oneshakes off the grittydarkness of recent offeringsto be aimed squarely atfamilies. It’s set in theMandalorian era and starsJude Law as a mysteriousrogue who joins forces withfour children who haveaccidentally whiskedthemselves away fromtheir home planet afterdiscovering a secret.The ListenersBBC One/BBC TwoWales, 9pmThe gripping if somewhatoverwrought psychologicaldrama continues as teacherClaire (Rebecca Hall), tornin several directions byher family and the group’sleaders – as well as her guiltover her relationship withher pupil Kyle (Ollie West) –heads towards a crisis.Hall’s excellent acting –understated and carefullywilling you to believe inher descent into hysteria –makes it worth watching.ENTERTAINMENTMary’s Foolproof DinnersBBC Two, 7.30pm; not WalesMary Berry wraps up thisseries on easy-to-cookmeals by inviting Will Kirk,one of the experts on TheRepair Shop, to join her forsome al fresco food. Theyfire up the barbecue fora chargrilled halloumi salad,while he teaches her howto whittle a spoon.FACTUALPrinces in the Tower:A Damning DiscoveryChannel 5, 9pmActor Jason Watkins andhistorian Tracy Bormanapproach the story of thetwo young princes – nineyear-old Richard and12-year-old Edward –who died in the Tower ofLondon as if it were a coldcase. They re-examine theshocking events of 1483 tomull over the possibilitytheir deaths were, asShakespeare’s plays hadit, ordered by their uncle,Richard III, but as thetitle promises they haveuncovered stunning newevidence. VLMary Berry shows WillKirk some easy recipesCheaters, starring SusanWokoma, draws to a closeRenaissance: The Blood and theBeauty ★★Person of Interest ★★★28 *** Tuesday 3 December 2024 The Daily TelegraphBBC One BBC Two ITV1 Channel 4 Channel 56.00 am Milkshake! 9.15 Jeremy Vine (S)11.30 Storm Huntley (S)12.55 pm 5 News at Lunchtime (S)1.00 Friends (AD) (R) (S)1.30 FILM: My Favourite ChristmasMelody (2021, TVM) Romanticcomedy starring Mya and RainbowSun Francks (S)3.15 FILM: Meet Me Under theMistletoe (2023, TVM) Premiere.Romance starring Sarah Fisher (S)5.00 5 News at 5 (S)6.00 Dogs Behaving (Very) BadlyAustralia (R) (S)6.55 5 News Update (S)6.00 am Breakfast (S) 9.30 Morning Liveat Christmas (S) 10.45 Caught RedHanded (AD) (R) (S) 11.15 HomesUnder the Hammer (S)12.15 pm Bargain Hunt (AD) (R) (S)1.00 BBC News at One; Weather (S)1.35 Regional News; Weather (S)1.45 BBC News at One; Weather (S)2.00 Scam Interceptors (S)2.30 Expert Witness (S)3.00 Escape to the Country (S)3.45 The Vintage French Farmhouse (R)(S)4.30 Antiques Road Trip (R) (S)5.15 Pointless (R) (S)6.00 BBC News at Six; Weather (S)6.30 Regional News; Weather (S)6.15 am My Life at Christmas with AnitaRani (AD) (R) (S) 7.15 Bargain Hunt(AD) (R) (S) 8.00 Sign Zone:Wonders of Life (AD) (R) (S) (SL)9.00 News (S)12.15 pm Politics Live (S)1.00 Impossible (R) (S)1.45 The Tournament (R) (S)2.30 Beat the Pack (R) (S)3.15 Celebrity Best Home Cook (AD) (R)(S)4.15 The Great British Winter (R) (S)5.15 Flog It! 4.10am A Play in a DayMain channels Film choiceToday’s televisionThe Listeners Between the Covers The Martin Lewis Money Show: Live Sue Perkins’ Big Adventure Princes in the TowerFV Freeview FS Freesat (AD) Audio description (R) Repeat (S) Subtitles (SL) In-vision signingJack in Time for Christmas (2024)Amazon Prime VideoFans of Bad Education and Fresh Meat,rejoice: Jack Whitehall is back witha brand new festive special. Strandedin America with just four days to makeit back to the UK in time for Christmas,Whitehall tries all manner of unlikelymethods to get home, from trains,huskies and planes to bobsleigh rides.Along the way, he gets help from a fewfamous faces including Michael Bublé,Rebel Wilson and Jimmy Fallon.Cat Ballou (1965)Film4, 4.50pm ★★★★Lee Marvin won a Best Actor Oscarfor his dual role in this classic Westerncomedy about Cat (Jane Fonda),a ranch girl who hires drunkardgunslinger Kid Shelleen (Marvin)to protect her father, Frankie (JohnMarley), from another killer, TimStrawn (also Marvin). After Frankieis killed, Shelleen has to clean up hisact and Cat takes up arms herself. It’sa very clever spoof of the wider genre.Boy Erased (2018)BBC Two, 12.05am ★★★★Joel Edgerton, writing and directing,adapts Garrard Conley’s agonisedmemoir about being sent to a Christian“gay conversion” camp into an equallypained film. (Edgerton co-stars, too,as the camp director.) Lucas Hedgesis superb as Jared, whose sexualityis revealed to his mother (NicoleKidman) and father (Russell Crowe);neither are open-minded. It’s a toughbut bracing watch.The Daily Telegraph Tuesday 3 December 2024 *** 2930 *** Tuesday 3 December 2024 The Daily TelegraphNature NotesBee-killing pesticides have been foundin 85 per cent of tested rivers inEngland, an analysis of Governmentfigures has found.The Rivers Trust and Wildlife andCountryside Link looked atEnvironment Agency data onneonicotinoid pesticides in 27 riversites tested between 2023 and 2024.Neonicotinoids destroy bees’nervous systems and are banned inEurope, but have been authorised foruse every year in the UK since 2021.Labour is thinking of allowing theuse of a neonicotinoid on sugar beetcrops despite promising to ban it whencampaigning for the general election,the Guardian reported.British Sugar and the NationalFarmers’ Union applied to be allowedto use the neonicotinoid Cruiser SB onsugar beet to tackle a plant diseaseknown as virus yellows. If approved, itwill mark the fifth year in a row ofemergency use of neonicotinoids.Bees at risk fromriver pesticidesWeather & CrosswordsThe Daily Telegraph Tuesday 3 December 2024 *** 3132 *** Tuesday 3 December 2024 The Daily Telegraph


--- [Rugby union match officials - Wikipedia] ---
Rugby union match officials are responsible for enforcing the laws of rugby union during a match. "Every match is under the control of match officials who consist of the referee and two touch judges or assistant referees." Further officials can be authorised depending on the level and form of the game.
